You should follow the instructions on the front cover of the answer booklet. If you need additional answer paper ask the invigilator for a continuation booklet. Section A Answer all questions. Section B Answer one question. You are advised to spend no more than 35 minutes on Section A. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. Cambridge International Examinations Cambridge International Advanced Subsidiary and Advanced Level 2 9609/11/M/J/16 Section A (Short Answer) Answer all questions. Distinguish between â€˜strategyâ€™ and â€˜tacticsâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two stages of business decision-making. [3]
mark_scheme_text: 1 (a) Distinguish between â€˜strategyâ€™ and â€˜tacticsâ€™. [2] â€˜Strategyâ€™ can be defined as â€˜a way of planning/achieving an objectiveâ€™ (the action/actions required to achieve an objective). OR â€˜Strategyâ€™ can also be defined as actions/activities that focus on the long-term direction of an organisation. â€˜Tacticsâ€™ defined as short-term policy decisions/methods that form part of the implementation of an overall strategy. One mark for each accurate definition given (maximum 2 marks). (b) Briefly explain two stages of business decision-making. [3] The different stages of business decision-making are said to include: â€¢ Identification of objectives/goals/problem identification. â€¢ Planning i.e. collection of information and ideas. â€¢ Analysis of information and ideas. â€¢ Formulation of alternative courses of action. â€¢ Choice of alternative courses of action. â€¢ Making a decision. â€¢ Implement the decision â€¢ Communicate the decision. â€¢ Evaluate the results of the decision. Two stages soundly explained. (3 marks) One stage soundly explained or two partially explained (2 marks) One stage partially explained or a list of two (1 mark) Please accept and reward other relevant points.

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define â€˜marginal costsâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two other types of business costs. [3]
mark_scheme_text: 2 (a) Define â€˜marginal costsâ€™. [2] Marginal costs are the additional costs of producing one more unit of output â€“ the extra variable costs required to make this extra unit. Sound definition given (2 marks) Partial definition given (limited understanding) (1 mark) Page 3 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Levelâ€“ May/June 2016 9609 11 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2016 (b) Briefly explain two other types of business costs. [3] The following costs are relevant: â€¢ Fixed costs â€“ costs that do not vary with output in the short-term, such as rent of premises. â€¢ Variable costs â€“ costs that vary with output, such as the cost of materials used in making a television/iPad. â€¢ Semi-variable costs â€“ these include a fixed and variable element, such as the basic wage of a salesperson (fixed) and the element of commission that varies with sales. â€¢ Direct costs â€“ costs that can be clearly identified with each unit of production and can be allocated to a cost centre â€“ e.g. the labour cost of a garage mechanic. â€¢ Indirect costs â€“ costs that cannot be clearly identified with a unit of production or allocated directly to a cost centre â€“ often referred to as overheads â€“ the cost of cleaning a retail shop. â€¢ Average costs â€“ the total cost divided by the number of goods produced (unit cost). â€¢ Total costs â€“ variable costs (labour & raw materials) plus fixed costs (independent of quantity produced). Examples will contribute to the quality of the explanation. Two types of cost soundly explained (3 marks) One type of cost soundly explained or two partially explained (2 marks) One type of cost partially explained or a list of two (1 mark) Please accept and reward other relevant points. Page 4 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Levelâ€“ May/June 2016 9609 11 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2016 3 Explain the possible consequences for a business of high labour turnover. [5] Answers could include: â€¢ Defined as the rate at which employees leave an organisation over a given period of time. â€¢ Possible indication of significant problems; low morale, poor motivation , poor working environment, uncompetitive pay, poor management and leadership, or induced by management dismissing lots of employees. â€¢ Possible consequences could include: â€“ business/production disrupted â€“ insufficient staff â€“ poor customer service â€“ existing staff put under more pressure â€“ skills gaps develop â€“ business objectives threatened/not achieved â€“ business reputation/brand damaged â€“ extra expenses on training, recruitment and selection of new staff â€“ staff may be difficult to replace â€“ discourages potential investors Perceptive students may recognise that a high rate of labour turnover is common in certain sectors â€“ businesses that rely on part-time workers, such as food restaurants, traditionally have high rates of labour turnover. It is not necessarily a negative issue. Effective explanation of possible consequences of high labour turnover (4â€“5marks) Li

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define â€˜inventory managementâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two reasons why a business might decide to hold a high level of inventory. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only.
mark_scheme_text: 4 (a) Define â€˜inventory managementâ€™. [2] Ensuring an appropriate level of inventory (stock) is held (1 mark) within the business. To ensure that inventory is held in appropriate conditions i.e. storage. (1 mark) Inventories are controlled/organised efficiently (1 mark) to ensure uninterrupted production/sales/customer service levels. (1 mark) Sound definition given (any 2 out of the 4 above) (2 marks) Partial definition given (limited understanding) (1 mark) Page 5 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Levelâ€“ May/June 2016 9609 11 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2016 (b) Briefly explain two reasons why a business might decide to hold a high level of inventory. [3] Answers could relate to a specific business such as a retailer and/or to particular product/service items within a business. Answers could include: â€¢ A buffer against anticipated high demand. â€¢ To eliminate/reduce the risk of stock out costs. â€¢ To respond to the risk of future supply difficulties. â€¢ To plan for significant seasonal variations in demand. â€¢ To take advantage of bulk supply price discounts. â€¢ To respond to anticipated future price rises in supplier materials â€“ achieve a capital gain. â€¢ To improve customer service with consistent high levels of product / service availability. Sound explanation of two reasons for high levels of inventory (3 marks) Sound explanation of one reason or partial explanation of two reasons (2 marks) Partial explanation of one reason or a list of two (1 mark) Please accept and reward other relevant points. Page 6 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Levelâ€“ May/June 2016 9609 11 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2016

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Analyse why it is important for a business to have policies on diversity and equality. [8] (b) Discuss the importance of â€˜work-life balanceâ€™ for employee welfare in a tertiary sector business. [12] 6 â€˜Because there is conflict between profit and corporate social responsibility (CSR), private sector businesses should not have CSR as an objective.â€™ Do you agree? Justify your view. [20]
mark_scheme_text: 5 (a) Analyse why it is important for a business to have policies on diversity and equality. [8] Level 4 Good analysis of the importance of diversity and equality policies [7â€“8] Level 3 Some analysis of the importance of diversity/equality policies [5â€“6] Level 2 Some explanation/application of policies on diversity/equality for a business [3â€“4] Level 1 Understanding of diversity and/or equality [1â€“2] Level 0 No creditable content [0] Answers may include: â€¢ Equality defined as breaking down barriers, eliminating discrimination, ensuring equal opportunity and access for all groups in the business. â€¢ Aim to outlaw discrimination on grounds of gender, marital status, sexual orientation, race, colour, nationality, religion, age, disability, political beliefs. â€¢ Diversity defined as celebrating differences and valuing everyone. Each person with visible or invisible differences valued and respected. â€¢ There are increasing legal requirements relating to equality and diversity (will vary between countries and regions). â€¢ But also policies developed to reflect the distinctive values of a business and embedded in day-to-day working practices. â€¢ Such policies regarded as essential for workforce efficiency and effectiveness. â€¢ Aim is to produce an inclusive organisation, a distinctive organisation culture that enhances the work experience. â€¢ Considered important to counter the negative effect of discrimination and to produce a richer, more tolerant working environment. â€¢ It is about promoting fairness, ensuring all are valued. â€¢ Perceptive answers will recognise the â€˜complianceâ€™ aspect of these policies, together with the proactive approach of many organisations â€“ and the potential consequences of such policies (some may be negative). Please accept and reward other relevant points. Page 7 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Levelâ€“ May/June 2016 9609 11 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2016 (b) Discuss the importance of â€˜work-lifeâ€™ balance for employee welfare in a tertiary sector business. [12] Level 4 Effective evaluation of the importance of work-life balance in context [9â€“12] Level 3 Limited evaluation of the importance of work-life balance in context [7â€“8] Level 2 Analysis and some application of work-life balance in context [3â€“6] Level 1 Understanding of work life balance [1â€“2] Level 0 No creditable content [0] Answers could include: â€¢ Work-life balance is about creating and maintaining supportive and healthy work environments that enable employees to have balance between work and other aspects of life â€“ family, friends, community, personal growth. â€¢ A focus on work life balance could lessen the danger of over worked employees affecting quality of service /reputation and profits. â€¢ Reduce employee stress/burnout/conflict between work and family. â€¢ Reduce negative effects of work life conflict â€“ such as health risks â€“ smoking, drinking, weight gain, depression â€“ on employees. â€¢ Cost implications of over-worked staff â€“ absenteeism

mark_scheme_text: 7 (a) Analyse how a business might use price elasticity of demand for pricing decisions. [8] Level 4 Good analysis of how a business might use PED for pricing decisions [7â€“8] Level 3 Some analysis of how a business might use PED for pricing decisions [5â€“6] Level 2 Some explanation/application of PED to pricing decisions in a business [3â€“4] Level 1 Understanding of PED [1â€“2] Level 0 No creditable content [0] Answers could include: â€¢ PED measures the responsiveness of customer demand for a product as the price of it is changed. â€¢ Setting the price for a product is one of the most important of all marketing decisions. â€¢ A useful concept to use in fixing/changing a price. â€¢ Gives an understanding of the sensitivity of demand for a product at different price levels. â€¢ Reduce price of product with a price elastic demand can increase revenue â€¢ Gives an indication of the price level that maximises revenue (used in price penetration strategies). â€¢ Can be used for a price discrimination strategy where there are different segments of the market with different elasticities. However, not as simple as might appear because: â€¢ the data on price elasticity may be inaccurate â€¢ it is hard to predict consumer behaviour â€¢ other factors need to be considered in pricing decisions â€¢ revenue maximising may well not lead to profit maximisation (costs need to be considered as well as price) â€¢ share of market to secure economies of scale and market dominance may be more important than revenue maximisation. Perceptive answers may well recognise the need for a business to assess the possible response of their customers to any change in price. Please accept and reward other relevant points. Page 10 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Levelâ€“ May/June 2016 9609 11 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2016 (b) Discuss the best ways a car manufacturer could use the marketing mix to increase its share of the market. [12] Level 4 Effective evaluation of the best ways a car manufacturer could use the marketing mix to increase market share [9â€“12] Level 3 Limited evaluation of the best ways a car manufacture could use the use the marketing mix to increase market share [7â€“8] Level 2 Analysis and some application of the marketing mix to a car manufacturer to increase market share [3â€“6] Level 1 Understanding of marketing mix [1â€“2] Level 0 No creditable content [0] Answers could include: â€¢ Marketing mix as a framework/process for identifying, anticipating and satisfying customer requirements profitably. 4Ps. â€¢ A tool of analysis and planning to focus on key elements/variables in designing a marketing strategy to achieve organisation objectives (e.g. increase market share). â€¢ Focus attention on Product: higher quality, customer perception, changes needed, car design, USP, reliability, engineering claims? â€¢ Price â€“ pricing strategy? â€“ competitive pricing? â€¢ Place â€“ appropriate retail outlets? â€¢ Promotion â€“ relevant and impactive promotion? â€¢ Perhaps a more balanced mark

The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/12/M/J/16 Section A (Short Answer) Answer all questions. Define the term â€˜entrepreneurâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two reasons why new businesses often fail. [3]
mark_scheme_text: 1 (a) Define the term â€˜entrepreneurâ€™. [2] Defined as someone who takes risks. Starting/managing/making decisions in a business. Organises the factors of production. Creative/has new ideas/spots gaps in the market. Sound definition given (any 2 of the above). [2] Partial definition given (limited understanding). [1] (b) Briefly explain two reasons why new businesses often fail. [3] Despite the enthusiasm and skills of an entrepreneur, many new businesses fail within one year. Lack of finance/capital â€“ banks unwilling to lend. Poor cash flow management. Poor management skills â€“ entrepreneurâ€™s not always good operational managers. Severe competition â€“ large companies/competitors squeeze. Limited portfolio of products/services. Very vulnerable to change and environments/threats â€“ e.g. recession, legal requirements â€“ technological change. Lack of market understanding. Lack of reputation/brand. Lack of record keeping. Sound explanation of two reasons for failure. [3] Sound explanation of one reason or partial explanation of two. [2] Partial explanation of one reason or a list of two. [1] Please accept and reward other relevant points. Page 3 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ May/June 2016 9609 12 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2016

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Distinguish between random sampling and quota sampling. [2] (b) Briefly explain two limitations of market research sampling. [3]
mark_scheme_text: 2 (a) Distinguish between random sampling and quota sampling. [2] Random sampling is where every member of the target population has an equal chance of being selected in a market research survey. Quota sampling is where the target population has been stratified and the interviewer selects an appropriate number of respondents from each stratum. Note: Do not award marks for candidates who simply repeat the words random and quota without any explanation of these terms. One mark for each accurate definition given. (b) Briefly explain two limitations of market research sampling. [3] Limitations could include the following: Imperfect sample design Inadequate budget to determine the sample, as it could be very/too expensive Sample size may be too small Difficulty in getting representation in the sample Some sampling may be tedious and time-consuming Untrained/inadequate interviewers Biased, ambiguous questions Could be very/too expensive. Note: some candidates may interpret market research sampling as product sampling/test marketing. Please accept this interpretation and award marks according to the quality of the limitations given. Full marks can be achieved for this approach. Two limitations soundly explained [3] One limitation soundly explained or two partially explained [2] One limitation partially explained or a list of two [1] Please accept and reward other relevant points. Page 4 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ May/June 2016 9609 12 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2016 3 Explain the costs and benefits to a business of a decision to hold low levels of inventory. [5] Answers could include: Costs such as: Insufficient stock to meet demands â€“ lost sales Lack of raw materials leads to idle production resources Unable to respond to exceptional/new orders quickly If not buying large quantity of inventory, miss out on bulk buying discounts (JIT). Benefits: Opportunity cost of capital invested in inventory is reduced Reduced storage costs Reduced risk of wastage/obsolescence Easier to organise and retrieve inventory. Very much depends on the type of business activity involved and whether demand is normal or exceptional. It is a trade-off between benefits and risk of incurring costs. Please accept and reward other relevant points. Effective explanation of costs and benefits of holding a low level of inventory. [4â€“5] Limited explanation of costs and/or benefits of holding a low level of inventory. [2â€“3] Understanding of inventory. [1] Please accept and reward other relevant points. Page 5 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ May/June 2016 9609 12 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2016

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Distinguish between a â€˜job descriptionâ€™ and a â€˜person specificationâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two ways in which employees can participate in business decision making. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only.
mark_scheme_text: 4 (a) Distinguish between a â€˜job descriptionâ€™ and a â€˜person specificationâ€™. [2] Job description is a detailed list of the key points about the job that is to be filled â€“ the tasks and responsibilities. Person specification is a list of the qualities, skills and qualifications required of a successful applicant. One mark for each accurate definition given. (b) Briefly explain two ways in which employees can participate in business decision making. [3] Opportunities could include: Representation at Board level â€“ a form of industrial democracy â€“ protect interests of employees. Participation through ownership â€“ employees hold shares. Collective bargaining arrangements/trade union membership. Suggestion schemes â€“ new ideas â€“ proposals for, say, cost cutting. Specific organisation forums â€“ e.g. worker co-operatives. Quality circles/focus/worker groups â€“ focus on analysis and problem-solving. Joint management/employee committees. Arrangements for employee/empowerment â€“ problem solving sessions â€“ delegated authority. Laissez-faire/democratic/paternalistic management/leadership allows participation in decision making. Delegation. Opportunities to vote on decisions. Two ways to participate soundly explained (3 marks). One way soundly explained or two partially explained (2 marks). One way partially explained or a list of two (1 mark). Please accept and reward other relevant points. Page 6 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ May/June 2016 9609 12 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2016

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Analyse the importance for a business of distinguishing between revenue expenditure and capital expenditure. [8] (b) Discuss the benefits and drawbacks to a business of using only internal sources of finance to fund expansion. [12] 6 â€˜The 4Cs marketing model is more useful for a retail business than the 4Ps marketing model.â€™ Do you agree with this statement? Justify your view. [20]
mark_scheme_text: 5 (a) Analyse the importance for a business of distinguishing between revenue expenditure and capital expenditure. [8] Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the importance of the distinction between revenue and capital expenditure 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the importance of the distinction between revenue and capital expenditure 5â€“6 2 Some explanation/application of revenue and capital expenditure to a business 3â€“4 1 Understanding of revenue and/or capital expenditure 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers could include: These are two different kinds of business expenditure and have significantly different impacts on a business â€“ revenue expenditure is a regular expenditure, gives benefit over the short term, maintains rather than enhances assets. Capital expenditure is long-term, irregular, and produces benefit (assets) over a number of accounting periods. Different in terms of how they are financed â€“ almost certainly financed in different ways â€“ capital expenditure more likely to be financed with debt/loan financing. Different in terms of how these types of expenditure are recorded in financial accounts . Revenue expenditure is on assets and expenses that give short term benefit to the business (1 year) and recorded in full on the Income Statement. Capital expenditure will be recorded on the Income Statement as a yearly depreciation amount and on the Statement of Financial Position as asset valuation. Please accept and reward other relevant points. (b) Discuss the benefits and drawbacks to a business of using only internal sources of finance to fund expansion. [12] Level Description Marks 4 Effective evaluation of the benefits and drawbacks of funding expansion only through internal sources of finance 9â€“12 3 Limited evaluation of the benefits and drawbacks of funding expansion only through internal sources of finance 7â€“8 2 Analysis and some application of the use of internal sources of finance to fund expansion 3â€“6 1 Understanding of internal sources of finance 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Page 7 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ May/June 2016 9609 12 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2016 Answers could include: Outline of types of internal funding sources, such as building up reserves through retained earnings, sale of unwanted assets. Benefits such as: No direct cost to the business Readily available Does not increase debt No loss of control â€“ no share sale Risk of high gearing reduced Start projects quickly â€“ no approval process externally Drawbacks such as: Using money from capital reserves or operating budgets leaves a business with less money to manage short-term expenses There may well be a limit to the kinds of expansion that can be funded internally (depends on amount of reserves available) Likely to fund only smaller projects where investment is modest, payback is quick and estimated returns significant. The viability of an investment project may be given less robust scrutiny when internal financin

mark_scheme_text: 7 (a) Analyse the importance to a large business of setting corporate objectives. [8] Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the importance of large business corporate objectives 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the importance of large business corporate objectives 5â€“6 2 Some explanation/application of corporate objectives to a business 3â€“4 1 Understanding of corporate objectives 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers could include: These specific organisation objectives become part of senior level management strategies. Set the context for divisional/departmental objectives and effective plans of action can be developed. Ensures that the business is focused and does not drift â€“ clear corporate objectives given such as growth, profit/sales maximisation. Present a clear set of guidelines and parameters for middle, junior management actions and strategies. Without these clear corporate objectives a business can drift and cease to compete and flourish. Give meaning and purpose for all engaged in the business. Please accept and reward other relevant points. Page 10 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ May/June 2016 9609 12 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2016 (b) Discuss how a large food retailer, with many shops, could effectively communicate corporate objectives to its workforce. [12] Level Description Marks 4 Effective evaluation of how corporate objectives could be communicated in context 9â€“12 3 Limited evaluation of how corporate objectives could be communicated in context 7â€“8 2 Analysis and some application of how corporate objectives could be communicated in context 3â€“6 1 Understanding of internal business communication 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers could include: Strong answers will recognise the particular communication challenge to reach all the retail outlets in different location areas â€“ senior management visits and presentations â€“ regional/local managers invited into HQ? Specific communication methods could be used: â€˜State of nationâ€™ address by CEO and/or senior managers Senior managers to middle managers to junior managers Team meeting/briefing Away days Training and development days Company newsletters/digital platforms/company website Internet/social media. Methods may not be as important or effective as developing participation and engagement opportunities for employees â€“ to educate, inform and inspire employees. Develop an open, sharing, two-way communication culture in the business â€“ train and retrain managers to share information and practise a management style that is supportive of staff engagement approaches. Effectiveness depends on the extent to which open communication is part of the bloodstream/DNA of the organisation and its managers and that there is full recognition of the need for consistency of approach over all this disparate business. Please accept and reward other relevant points.

Section B Answer one question. You are advised to spend no more than 35 minutes on Section A. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/13/M/J/16 Section A (Short Answer) Answer all questions. Define the term â€˜market segmentationâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two reasons why a business might segment the market for its products. [3]
mark_scheme_text: 1 (a) Define the term â€˜market segmentationâ€™. [2] A marketing approach that identifies different segments/sub-groups (1 mark) with similar characteristics within a market (1 mark) and gives correct examples e.g. age, social class, income, gender, geographical, demographical, psychographic (1 mark) and targets different products/services to those segments. (1 mark) Sound definition given (2 marks) Partial definition given (limited understanding) (1 mark) (b) Briefly explain two reasons why a business might segment the market for its products. [3] â€¢ Segmentation allows a precise targeting of a market/consumers â€¢ Allows a business to design and produce goods/services specifically aimed at a group â€¢ Helps to identify gaps in a market and to exploit them â€¢ A differentiated marketing strategy can be adopted â€¢ Allows small businesses to compete in a market and specialise to supply specific segments â€¢ Allows price discrimination to be used to increase revenue/profits Sound explanation of two reasons for market segmentation (3 marks) Sound explanation of one reason or partial explanation of two (2 marks) Partial explanation of one reason or a list of two (1 mark) Please accept and reward other relevant points.

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜workforce planningâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two benefits to a business of workforce planning. [3]
mark_scheme_text: 2 (a) Define the term â€˜workforce planningâ€™. [2] Workforce planning is defined as the analysis and forecasting of the numbers of workers (1 mark) and the skills/quality (1 mark) of those workers that will be required by a business organisation to achieve its objectives. Reference to the right people at the right place at the right time should be regarded as a sound definition and given 2 marks. Sound definition given (2 marks) Partial definition given (limited understanding) (1 mark) Page 3 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ May/June 2016 9609 13 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2016 (b) Briefly explain two benefits to a business of workforce planning. [3] Benefits could include: â€¢ A business will identify the existing skills and qualifications of its existing workforce. â€¢ A business will identify gaps in skills of existing workforce. â€¢ A business will analyse and anticipate the future needs of the organisation for particular skilled workers. â€¢ A business will ensure that in each department and throughout the organisation the numbers and skills of the workforce are appropriate. â€¢ Avoids the consequences of not having a workforce plan. â€¢ Assists the business to meet objectives and respond to current/future environmental/competitive situations. Two benefits soundly explained (3 marks) One benefit soundly explained or two partially explained (2 marks) One benefit partially explained or a list of two (1 mark) Please accept and reward other relevant points. 3 Explain why efficiency is important to a manufacturing business. [5] Efficiency is producing output at the highest ratio of output to input (â€˜doing the thing rightâ€™) and should be the concern of all businesses and business operations. Answers could include: â€¢ Some definition of efficiency given â€¢ A concern for efficiency is at the heart of objectives such as survival, high productivity, profitability, growth, market share â€¢ To be successful a business needs to be efficient in the deployment of all resources â€“ land, labour and capitalisation â€¢ Inefficiency can result in high costs leading to un-competitiveness and loss of market share â€¢ The transformation operations process needs to add value â€“ inefficiencies will reduce value added â€¢ A manufacturing business will need to focus on input resource efficiencies and will be concerned with the most efficient production methods and waste reduction in order to control and improve costs. This requires a focus on capital, labour and land deployment, not just at the operations level but throughout the business. Effective explanation of why efficiency is important in a manufacturing business (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of why efficiency is important in a business (2â€“3 marks) Understanding of efficiency in a business (1 mark) Please accept and reward other relevant points. Page 4 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ May/June 2016 9609 13 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2016

[3] Sources could include: â€¢ Personal, friends and family â€¢ Partnership funds â€¢ Bank loans â€¢ Government grants/assistance â€¢ Venture capital â€¢ Equity funding â€¢ â€˜Angelâ€™ investors â€¢ Micro finance â€¢ Crowd funding â€¢ Trade credit The size of any proposed new business will be an important factor in determining the source of funds. Sound explanation of two sources (3 marks) Sound explanation of one source or partial explanation of two (2 marks) Partial explanation of one source or a list of two (1 mark) Please accept and reward other relevant points. question_text: Analyse the advantages of a co-operative as a legal form of business. mark_scheme_text: 5 (a) Analyse the advantages of a co-operative as a legal form of business. [8] Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the advantages of a co-operative 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the advantages of a co-operative 5â€“6 2 Some explanation/application of the advantages of a co-operative 3â€“4 1 Limited understanding of co-operative businesses 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 â€¢ Co-operatives are joint ownership organisations (producer, workers, consumer). â€¢ A distinctive type of business organisation â€“ often a significant amount of democratic control and profits shared/distributed in proportion to membersâ€™ investment. â€¢ Producer co-operatives common in agriculture in developing countries. â€¢ Advantages claimed for co-operatives include: â€“ Members/users are involved and have opportunity to direct and control the business â€“ The business is designed and operated specifically for the members/users â€“ Resources are pooled for mutual gain â€“ Increased purchasing power with suppliers â€“ Increased marketing power â€“ joint advertising â€“ More consumer power â€“ less social/environmental damage â€“ Allows members with common interests to work together and assume responsibility (e.g. village post office/shop). (While a brief reference to the limitations of co-operatives may be relevant, this answer needs to focus on the advantages of co-operative businesses). Please accept and reward other relevant points. mark_scheme_text: 7 (a) Analyse how profitability ratios could be useful for any two business stakeholders. [8] Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of how profitability ratios could be useful for any two stakeholders of a business 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of how profitability ratios could be useful for any two stakeholders of a business 5â€“6 2 Some explanation/application of profitability ratios to stakeholders/ stakeholder of a business 3â€“4 1 Understanding of profitability ratios/stakeholders 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers could include: â€¢ Examples of profitability ratios (gross profit margin, profit margin, ROCE). â€¢ Examples of business stakeholders â€“ management, employees, shareholders, banks, government, competitors. â€¢ Some general uses of profitability ratios such as: â€“ indication of business performance and efficiency â€“ indication of business performance relative to other time periods and other businesses in the industry. â€¢ Application of what is indicated by profitability ratios to specific stakeholders, for example: â€“ shareholders/investors â€“ information to compare performance and decide to invest/increase investment â€“ employees â€“ indication of job security â€“ opportunity to press for better salaries/wages. Please accept and reward other relevant points. [12] Level Description Marks 4 Effective evaluation of the significance of limitations of published accounts on the usefulness for business stakeholders 9â€“12 3 Limited evaluation of the significance of limitations of published accounts on the usefulness for business stakeholders 7â€“8 2 Analysis and some application of limitations of published accounts 3â€“6 1 Understanding of published accounts 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers could include: â€¢ Identify published accounts â€“ Income Statement, Statement of Financial Position. â€¢ Identification of limitations of published accounts: â€“ historic statements â€“ what has happened in the past â€“ reflect only monetary/quantitative transactions â€“ qualitative factors â€“ reputation, loyalty of employees, integrity of management â€“ left out â€“ many items left to personal judgement of the accountant, e.g. inventory valuation, bad debts provision, depreciation â€“ window dressing However: â€“ published accounts are still of significant value; required by law â€“ provide reliable information and indicators for internal and external stakeholders â€“ aid to decision-making for internal and external stakeholders â€“ can be used for comparable analysis for different time periods â€“ reveals important business performance information â€“ so still valuable for internal and external stakeholders Please accept and reward other relevant points.

question_text: Peterson Manufacturing (PM) PM is a large public limited company operating in the secondary sector. PM manufactures four types of industrial ovens. PM only sells its ovens to other businesses. Most of PMâ€™s customers are bakeries which use the ovens to produce bread and cakes. Jacob is the Marketing Director of PM. He has been examining the data shown in Table 1. Table 1: Revenue data by oven type ($000s) Oven Type 2013 2015 A 855 770 B 745 755 C 550 450 D 350 400 Jacob is worried about the future success of PM. He is aware of how the market for PMâ€™s products has been changing. â€¢ Market research shows that customers prefer the more advanced products of competitors. â€¢ The sales team has reported that customers are becoming more demanding when negotiating prices. â€¢ In PMâ€™s industry there is an increasing emphasis on developing effective customer relationships. â€¢ PM relies on trade fairs to attract new customers. However customer attendance at these fairs is declining. Jessica, the Human Resources Director, is proposing to replace the performance related pay scheme with a profit sharing scheme. The current system of bonuses, based on meeting production targets, will end. Table 2 contains a summary of these pay schemes. Table 2: Summary of different pay schemes Current performance related pay scheme Proposed profit sharing scheme â€¢ Workers are paid an hourly wage. â€¢ Workers also receive a bonus based on meeting production targets each month. â€¢ 90% of production targets were met in 2015. â€¢ Workers will be paid an hourly wage. â€¢ Workers will also receive a bonus based on the profit of PM each year. Some workers are not happy about this change and worker representatives have asked for a meeting with Jessica. Workers know that revenue and profit have been declining over the last few years. Workers blame poor marketing and a lack of product development. However PM managers hope that new types of oven will improve company performance in the future. Workersâ€™ bonuses have been rising but only due to increased hours worked. 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 3 9609/21/M/J/16 [Turn over (a) (i) Define the term â€˜public limited companyâ€™ (line 1). [2] (ii) Briefly explain the term â€˜worker representativesâ€™ (line 32). [3] (b) (i) Refer to Table 1. Calculate the percentage decrease in total revenue between 2013 and 2015. [3] (ii) Briefly explain one likely effect on PM of this decrease in total revenue. [3] (c) Analyse how PM could use the 4Cs to improve its customer relationships. [8] (d) Discuss the advantages and disadvantages to PMâ€™s employees of the change from performance related pay to a profit sharing scheme. [11] 4 9609/21/M/J/16

question_text: Scented Candles (SC) SC has been trading for one year. SC is a sole trader business owned by Jane. SC produces and sells a range of luxury scented candles. The business is operated from a shop in a town where there are several competitors. However, employment in the local area is rising and average incomes are increasing. Jane did not carry out market research before starting SC. In the first year she set a very small budget for promotion spending. Jane has found it difficult to decide on an effective pricing strategy for the candles. Revenue has been 15% less than expected. Jane knows that consumers have to consider opportunity cost when deciding what to buy. Three months ago Jane decided to reduce the selling price of one type of candle, Candle A. She hoped this would increase the demand for this product. Table 1 shows the effect of this price change. Table 1: Data for Candle A Sales (units) per month Price Before change 500 $

question_text: Refer to Table 1. Calculate the difference in unit cost between the two supply options for turbine blades. [6] (b) Recommend whether CJE should outsource turbine blade production. Justify your answer using your answer from (a) and other information. [12]
mark_scheme_text: 2 (a) Refer to Table 1. Calculate the difference in unit cost between the two supply options for turbine blades. [6] Unit cost = total cost/output or (fixed + variable cost)/output (1 mark if no relevant calculation) Ousourcing: Transport cost is $20 (1 mark) Unit cost is $590 [$570 + $200/10] (2 marks) Extend factory: Additional fixed cost is 600 000/3000 = 200 (1 mark) Assuming max capacity then unit cost is $500 + [$600 000/3000] = $700 (3 marks) Note: If a candidate makes a clear assumption regarding output up to a max of 3000 then treat as OFR. Identify outsourcing and extend factory as $590 and $700 (5 marks) Difference is 700 â€“ 590 = $110 (6 marks) Own Figure Rule (OFR) applies. (b) Recommend whether CJE should outsource turbine blade production. Justify your answer using your answer from (a) and other information. Page 4 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International A Level â€“ May/June 2016 9609 32 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2016 Answers could include: â€¢ Unit cost of extending factory should not include current annual fixed costs â€“ these have to be paid anyway. â€¢ Outsourcing: Seems to be cheaper (but even if a â€˜more expensiveâ€™ result is obtained in (a), there is still scope for discussion e.g. time factor and flexibility of production). â€¢ It is also quicker â€“ this could be a real advantage if CJE is in danger of not being able to meet customer orders. â€¢ Outsourcing fits in better with the aim of greater flexibility and cost cutting. â€¢ Has greater maximum additional capacity. â€¢ However: Quality is a big issue â€“ and perhaps the deal breaker. If CJE do not want to risk their quality/reliability reputation is this worth taking the risk? â€¢ Why did the other engine maker cancel an order? â€¢ Will transport costs rise? â€¢ Will distance prove to be a problem if CJE uses JIT? â€¢ Deliveries within 4 months compared to 15 months to complete factory. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Case Study May/June 2016 3 hours No Additional Materials are required. READ THESE INSTRUCTIONS FIRST An answer booklet is provided inside this question paper. You should follow the instructions on the front cover of the answer booklet. If you need additional answer paper ask the invigilator for a continuation booklet. Section A Answer all questions. Section B Answer one question. You are advised to spend no more than 40 minutes on Section B. The businesses described in this question paper are entirely fictitious. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/33/M/J/16 Xiang Mobile (XM) Smartphones at a smart price When XM released its first smartphone (a cell phone which connects to the Internet), the X1, in 2012 the industry could not believe its price. Consumers, in country Y where XM is based, could buy this high specification smartphone for just $350. This represented astonishing value compared to competitorsâ€™ products, including the market leading brand, which sold for $800. XM quickly followed the X1 with an even lower priced line of smartphones starting at just $150. These offered a lower specification yet delivered high value. This helped XM to become its countryâ€™s second largest smartphone brand within just two years. The business was founded by Xiang Sen, an entrepreneur with a record of success in technology markets. To attract senior executives from other technology companies Xiang offered them the status of co-founders and shares in the business. Most potential investors were doubtful that a new brand could succeed in the crowded smartphone market. Entering the market would require large amounts of capital. Venture capitalists were approached for finance in return for equity. Not all firms are the same Unlike rivals, XM does not spend significant sums of money on traditional advertising. Marketing costs are less than 1% of revenue; the industry average is 6%. XM has targeted its products at technology enthusiasts. The company has kept costs low by selling most of its smartphones online through its own website and one of the countryâ€™s largest online retail sites. XM has not opened any of its own shops. The company uses social media, public relations and its own customers to help promote its products. With limited availability of products, XM knows that every time it has something to announce customers will listen. Creating scarcity XM is still a relatively small business compared to the market leader. When the latest model of smartphone, the X2, went on sale in 2014 the first batch of 50 000 units sold out within minutes. XM only releases a limited quantity of smartphones each week. Inventory is kept low and orders are shipped directly from the contracted manufacturer in country Y. Xiang, the CEO, believes that through creating a scarce supply of products XM has raised brand awareness and achieved free publicity. Industry specialists believe that XM could sell many more units at pric

To what extent should the shareholders of XM be pleased with the companyâ€™s financial performance in 2015? [16] Level Knowledge 3 marks Application 3 marks Analysis 5 marks Evaluation 5 marks 2 3 marks Good knowledge shown of financial performance 3 marks Points well applied 4â€“5 marks Good use of reasoned argument or theory to explain shareholder view of XMâ€™s financial performance 4â€“5 marks Good judgement shown in answer and conclusion 1 1â€“2 marks Knowledge shown of financial performance e.g. different measures 1â€“2 mark Some attempt to apply 1â€“3 marks Some use of reasoned argument or theory to explain shareholder view of financial performance 1â€“3 marks Some judgement shown in answer and/or conclusion 0 No creditable content. A range of financial ratios can be used to analyse the success of XM from the perspective of shareholders. Shareholders are the owners of the business and shareholder value is represented by the increase in the value of shares and by the dividend received. Answers may include: â€¢ The business is profitable and post-tax profits have increased by $70m between 2015 and 2016. Shareholders are interested in profit as it will affect share price and the ability of the firm to increase dividends paid. â€¢ Operating PM has increased from 11.13% to 12.2%. â€¢ [Operating PM = operating profit / revenue Ã— 100] â€¢ ROCE has increased from 4.2% to 5.3%. â€¢ Dividends have risen to $25m but there has been a fall in the dividend yield to 1.7%. Shareholders may be disappointed that the firm is not returning more dividends to them. â€¢ The PE Ratio has increased slightly to 6.8. This suggests that the market is confident about the future ability of XM to generate profits. The higher the PE ratio the more confident are investors. â€¢ There has been a substantial increase in the share price of 50%. This is good for shareholders who will make a capital gain if they sell their shares. â€¢ XM has suffered from cash flow shortages in the past and there is some evidence of limited working capital in the business currently. The quick ratio has deteriorated to 0.82 and this means that for every $1 of short term debt the business only has $0.82 of liquid assets. Does this suggest that the business faces potential liquidity problems

mark_scheme_text: 1 (a) Define the term â€˜mission statementâ€™. [2] A written statement of the core aims/values/purpose/objectives of a business. Sound definition (2 marks) Partial definition (limited understanding) (1 mark) (b) Briefly explain two limitations of mission statements. [3] Limitations could include: â€¢ Often written in very general / vague terms in order to appeal to internal and external stakeholders as to have little impact. â€¢ Often long and aspirational â€“ a wish list with little operational value. â€¢ Often seen as a PR exercise â€“ so little motivational impact (other than negative cynicism!). â€¢ If not supported by management has little positive impact â€¢ Seen as being distant from operational reality. Two limitations soundly explained (3 marks) One limitation soundly explained or two partially explained (2 marks) One limitation partially explained or a list of two (1 mark)

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜redundancyâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two ways a human resource management department could help employees who are to be made redundant. [3]
mark_scheme_text: 2 (a) Define the term â€˜redundancyâ€™. [2] When a job is no longer required in a business and the employee doing that job loses the job through no fault of their own. Sound definition (2 marks) Partial definition (limited understanding) (1 mark) (b) Briefly explain two ways a human resource management department could help employees who are to be made redundant. [3] Answers may include: â€¢ Re-deployment opportunities identified. â€¢ Produce an appropriate redundancy package. â€¢ Access additional help for employees such as job advice, counselling. â€¢ Training/new skills â€¢ Financial help Two ways HRM could help soundly explained (3 marks) One way soundly explained or two partially explained (2 marks) One way partially explained or a list of two (1 mark) Page 3 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ October/November 2016 9609 11 3 Explain why the distinction between market orientation and product orientation could be important for a business operating in a very competitive market. [5] Answers may include: â€¢ These are two contrasting approaches to marketing. â€¢ Market orientation is a market-led approach where the focus is on the customer â€“ market research is used to identify customer needs, perceptions and potential demands â€“ this information is then analysed and used to develop products and services to meet these demands â€“ associated with the 4Cs and CRM. â€¢ Product orientation is a product-led approach â€“ a focus on getting the product right â€“ then market it effectively and sell it to the customer â€“ associated with the traditional 4Ps approach to marketing. â€¢ Market orientation is said to be a more effective approach in the digital age where customer relationships and interactions are encouraged. â€¢ The context of â€˜a very competitive marketâ€™ would suggest that a market orientation would be the marketing approach to adopt in order to survive and thrive. Effective explanation of the difference and significance/importance of these two approaches in a very competitive market (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of the two different approaches with possibly limited reference to the context of a very competitive market (2â€“3 marks) Understanding of market orientation and/or product orientation (1 mark) NB If the definition of product and market orientation are the wrong way round but there is subsequent relevant comment relating to a competitive market then 2 marks maximum can be awarded.

mark_scheme_text: 5 (a) Analyse the benefits and limitations of a labour intensive production process for a business. [8] Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the benefits and limitations of a labour intensive production process for a business 7â€“ 8 3 Some analysis of the benefits and limitations of a labour intensive production process for a business 5 â€“ 6 2 Some application of the benefits and/or limitations of a labour intensive production process for a business 3 â€“ 4 1 Understanding of a labour intensive process 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers may include: â€¢ Operations managers must decide on the combination of factors of production â€“ one option is to use a high level of labour relative to capital equipment. â€¢ Obviously more appropriate and necessary for some businesses â€“ the nature of the business (say hotels and restaurants). â€¢ More labour may afford opportunity to give a more personal service â€“ or a more unique product (hand crafted furniture). â€¢ In certain situations, it is less expensive to use labour than expensive machines â€“ or where machines are not appropriate â€“ capital investment not required or not available. â€¢ Less fixed cost, particularly in recession times â€“ more flexible â€“ can 'lay off' staff. â€¢ However, there may be limitations in using a labour intensive production process â€¢ Opportunities for expansion â€“ economies of scale may be limited. â€¢ Craft/specialised labour may also die away/be in short supply. â€¢ Labour-intensive production may become uncompetitive. â€¢ Labour costs initially low may become too high. â€¢ More likely to get government grants due to increasing/creating employment. Page 5 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ October/November 2016 9609

mark_scheme_text: 7 (a) Analyse the benefits to a business of product portfolio analysis. [8] Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the benefits of product portfolio analysis 7â€“ 8 3 Some analysis of the benefits of product portfolio analysis 5 â€“ 6 2 Some application of product portfolio analysis in a business context (this could be related to product life cycle) 3 â€“ 4 1 Understanding of product portfolio analysis/product life cycle 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers may include: â€¢ Product portfolio analysis is the analysis of the elements of a company's product/service mix in order to determine the optimum allocation of resources. â€¢ It is a tool for a more informed marketing/corporate strategy. â€¢ It involves the examination of products/services with low margins and/or volatility and the removal of them from the portfolio â€“ remove risk to the business. â€¢ In the context of the product life cycle concept/model, you examine where each product/service is positioned. â€¢ An important tool to aid decision-making â€“ helps to steer resources towards the most productive areas. â€¢ Reveals the strengths and weaknesses of the product/service mix. â€¢ Some may refer to Boston matrix (but not required). NB Some candidates may adopt an approach in which they refer to â€˜one productâ€™ out of a product portfolio and then identify benefits to a business of analysing that product in the market. mark_scheme_text: 1 (a) Define joint venture [2] Two or more businesses/people agree to work together (1 mark) on a particular project/product or business enterprise (1 mark) (A distinct business unit/division may be set up or just informal collaborative arrangements). Sound definition given (2 marks) Partial definition (limited understanding) (1 mark) (b) Briefly explain two advantages of joint ventures to the businesses involved. [3] Advantages may include: â€¢ Share strengths, minimise risk and increases opportunity for competitive advantage in business ventures. â€¢ Given access to new resources, markets, or distribution channels. â€¢ Particularly useful for small businesses wanting to spread risks â€“ e.g. joint advertising, marketing, R&D. â€¢ Popular way of entering new, emerging markets. â€¢ May be used by large businesses to shut out the competition. â€¢ Loyal customers of both companies are likely to purchase the new product or service and the customer base is therefore widened. NB: do not accept economies of scale as an advantage unless it is linked to a new project/service coming out of the new venture. Two advantages for joint ventures soundly explained (3 marks) One advantage soundly explained or two partially explained (2 marks) One advantage partially explained or a list of two (1 mark) Page 3 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ October/November 2016 9609 12

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define â€˜leadershipâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two advantages of autocratic leadership. [3]
mark_scheme_text: 2 (a) Define leadership [2] Answers may include: â€¢ A popular textbook definition of leadership is: â€˜the art of motivating a group of people towards achieving a common objectiveâ€™ . This definition should be awarded 2 marks. â€¢ Other more specific definitions of leadership include reference to: giving a sense of direction, a vision or purpose, guidance, inspiration, long-term and strategic, for or within an organisation. Such definitions should also be given 2 marks. (This definition of leadership is clearly different to definitions of a manager.) â€¢ Definitions which list motivation as the activity of leadership with nothing else should be awarded 1 mark. â€¢ Definitions which only give a definition of management such as organising, controlling, coordinating = 0 marks. NB : reference to different style(s) of leadership without any relevant leadership definition is 0. This question is about the definition of leadership not styles of leadership. Sound definition given (2 marks) Partial explanation (limited understanding) (1 mark) (b) Briefly explain two advantages of autocratic leadership [3] Answers may include: â€¢ Leaders take decisions on their own â€“ no discussion â€“ set objectives themselves, issue instructions â€“ close control and monitoring. â€¢ Useful when orders/decisions need to be responded to quickly â€“ e.g. army/police. â€¢ Important in emergency situations â€“ accidents, disasters â€“ requiring a focused leadership and co-ordinated decisions. â€¢ Useful when workers are content to get the job done and have no desire for empowerment or for participation, consultation, Theory X. â€¢ Prevents drift and ensures a focus on objectives. Sound explanation of two advantages of autocratic leadership (3 marks) Sound explanation of one advantage or partial explanation of two (2 marks) Partial explanation of one advantage or a list of two (1 mark) Page 4 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ October/November 2016 9609 12 3 Explain why cost information is important for business decision making. [5] Examples will contribute to the quality of the explanation: â€¢ Costing information is an important factor for the following: â€“ key factor in determining profits â€“ informs pricing decisions â€“ informs product decisions â€¢ Costing information facilitates control/monitoring/comparisons. â€¢ Costing assists in setting budgets and targets â€¢ Assists in resource allocation decisions â€¢ Costing vital in decisions relating to business options â€“ different production methods, alternative location decisions. Costing is at the heart of business decision-making. Effective explanation of the importance of costing (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of the importance of costing (2â€“3 marks) Understanding of costing (1 mark)

question_text: Define â€˜secondary market researchâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two advantages of using â€˜focus groupsâ€™ as a method of market research. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only.
mark_scheme_text: 4 (a) Define 'secondary' market research [2] The collection of data from second hand sources. Sound definition given (2 marks) Partial definition (limited understanding) (1 mark) (b) Briefly explain two advantages of using 'focus groups' as a method of market research. [3] Definition: a form of qualitative research in which a group of people are asked about their perceptions/opinions/beliefs/attitudes/views towards a product or service . Answers could include: â€¢ Considered to be an important source of marketing data. â€¢ Used to secure feedback about new products/services. â€¢ Considered to be more accurate and reliable information than surveys. â€¢ More opportunity for views, ideas, comments than in individual interviews or questionnaires â€“ rich discussion. â€¢ Produces important qualitative information. â€¢ Can save time / cost. NB : focus groups can consist of general consumers or targeted consumers. Sound explanation of two advantages of the use of 'focus groups' as a method of market research (3 marks) Sound explanation of one advantage or partial explanation of two (2 marks) Partial explanation of one advantage or a list of two, or an accurate definition. (1 mark) Page 5 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ October/November 2016 9609 12

question_text: Analyse, using examples, why packaging could be important in the marketing mix. [8] (b) Discuss factors that could determine the success of a business that has decided to set up an online shop to sell beauty products. [12]
mark_scheme_text: 5 (a) Analyse, using examples, why packaging could be important in the marketing mix. [8] Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the importance of packaging in the marketing mix- with at least one example 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the importance of packaging in the marketing mix with at least one example 5â€“6 2 Some application of packaging to marketing 3â€“4 1 Understanding of packaging 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers could include: â€¢ Packaging is often advanced as an additional P to the 4P's â€“ the quality, design, colour of a product's packaging is often considered to be very important.(eg: perfume/fragrance as a luxury product) â€¢ Packaging can perform a number of functions â€“ protect and contain the product â€“ give instructions to the customer about the product (contents, ingredients, how to use) â€“ support and sustain the image of the product â€“ support the recognition/brand of the product. â€¢ If packaging is poor it can destroy the quality image that is sought for a product. â€¢ If packaging is seen to be too wasteful of resources, consumers may respond negatively in an environmentally concerned time. â€¢ Packaging has to support the overall objective of the business. â€¢ So packaging is functional (protects product) â€“ it attracts the customer â€“ it promotes the product â€“ it differentiates the product â€“ it encourages purchasing. NB : the question asks for examples; these can be product specific or examples that demonstrate application of packaging to the marketing mix. Page 6 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ October/November 2016 9609

[8] Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the advantages and disadvantages of performance related pay to an employer 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the advantages and disadvantages of performance related pay to an employer 5â€“6 2 Some application of performance related pay to a business context 3â€“4 1 Understanding of performance related pay 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Definition: Performance related pay has been defined as a way of linking salary progression to an assessment of individual performance usually measured against pre-set objectives . (Reference may be made to ways of giving PRP through piece rate, bonus, and commission payments.) Answers could include: â€¢ Advantages â€“ â€“ a bonus scheme to reward staff for above average performance will motivate those who seek increases in financial reward it is suggested. â€“ the targets set for PRP will focus workers. â€“ it helps to retain and attract new staff. â€“ recognises individual contribution. â€“ it may be an effective way of dealing with poor performance. â€“ it can lead to an increase in staff productivity. â€“ it can be an important element of a performance culture. â€¢ Disadvantages â€“ â€“ it is suggested that PRP inhibits team performance with its focus on individual performance â€“ it may be seen to reduce pay equity and increase disparities â€“ disputes can arise relating to how to measure performance. â€“ arguably too much relies on the judgement of the assessor. â€“ does PRP really motivate workers significantly? â€“ other incentives (non-financial may be more important. â€“ it may encourage unhealthy rivalry. NB : please note this question requires an analysis of the advantages/disadvantages of PRP to an employer . Page 9 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ October/November 2016 9609

question_text: Insert. DC (ST) 118108/2 [Turn over Cambridge International Examinations Cambridge International Advanced Subsidiary and Advanced Level * 0 5 7 8 9 0 5 2 4 3 * BUSINESS 9609/ Short Answer and Essay October/November 2016 1 hour 15 minutes No Additional Materials are required. READ THESE INSTRUCTIONS FIRST An answer booklet is provided inside this question paper. You should follow the instructions on the front cover of the answer booklet. If you need additional answer paper ask the invigilator for a continuation booklet. Section A Answer all questions. Section B Answer one question. You are advised to spend no more than 35 minutes on Section A. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/13/O/N/16 Section A (Short Answer) Answer all questions. Distinguish between external and internal recruitment. [2] (b) Briefly explain two advantages to a business of internal recruitment. [3]
mark_scheme_text: 1 (a) Distinguish between external and internal recruitment [2] External recruitment is where the HR department search the employee pool outside of its own employees to fill positions. Internal recruitment is where the HR department searches inside the organisation and appoints people from within to positions (can be horizontal/ sideways appointments or vertical promotion appointments). ONE mark for each accurate definition given (maximum 2 marks) (b) Briefly explain two advantages to a business of internal recruitment. [3] Answers could include: â€¢ Internal employees will understand the business/culture/processes. â€¢ No need for extensive training for assimilation. â€¢ Promotion from within may well be a significant motivator for staff â€“ improving morale. â€¢ Cheaper and quicker to recruit â€“ more cost effective. â€¢ Known past performance can be assessed. Two advantages of internal recruitment soundly explained (3 marks) One advantage soundly explained or two partially explained (2 marks) One advantage partially explained or a list of two (1 mark)

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define â€˜product life cycleâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two uses of the product life cycle to a business. [3]
mark_scheme_text: 2 (a) Define â€˜product life cycleâ€™ [2] The stages/cycle a product goes through from introduction to withdrawal from the market. Or, the marketing stages are traditionally broken down into 4 stages e.g. introduction/launch, growth, maturity and decline Sound definition (2 marks) Partial definition (limited understanding) (1 mark) (b) Briefly explain two uses of the product life cycle to a business [3] Answers may include: â€¢ The PLC concept assists with planning marketing mix decisions â€“ when/how to launch a new product â€“ at what stage do you reduce the price of a product â€“ when do you spend most of your advertising budget â€“ when do you make changes to the product? â€¢ Indicates the extent of the cash flows at different stages of the life of a product â€“ at introduction stage will be negative â€“ at maturity stage most positive. â€¢ Indicates the need for a balanced product portfolio â€“ when to replace a product. â€¢ An important analytical tool â€“ a check on the effectiveness of the marketing strategy of a business. Sound explanation of two uses of the PLC (3 marks) Sound explanation of one use or partial explanation of two (2 marks) Partial explanation of one use or a list of two (1 mark) Page 3 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ October/November 2016 9609 13 3 Explain why many new businesses fail within their first year. [5] Answers could include: â€¢ Insufficient capital â€“ run out of cash for day to day operational needs (working capital). â€¢ Flawed business plan/model â€“ inadequate information and too ambitious forward projections. â€¢ Poor management â€“ entrepreneurs without experience in finance, sales, hiring â€“ poor leadership and decision-making. â€¢ Failure to understand customers â€“ too â€˜product ledâ€™. â€¢ No real differentiated product/service and no real understanding of the competition. â€¢ Lack of business visibility â€“ website. â€¢ Over expansion â€“ too soon. â€¢ Too much red tape â€“ bureaucratic restrictions. â€¢ Unable to respond to external environmental changes â€“ recession. Effective explanation of the factors leading to early new business failure (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of the factors leading to early new business failure (2â€“3 marks) Understanding of new business and/or business failure (1 mark)

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define â€˜transformation processâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two objectives of an operations management department. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only.
mark_scheme_text: 4 (a) Define â€˜transformation processâ€™ [2] An activity (process) or group of activities that takes inputs and converts them into outputs. Sound definition (2 marks) Partial definition (limited understanding) (1 mark) (b) Briefly explain two objectives of an operations management department [3] Answers could include: â€¢ To design, create, produce goods and services for an organisation and its customers â€“ effectively. â€¢ To direct and control the transformation process â€“ so that it is efficient and effective and adds value. â€¢ To procure appropriate inputs in a cost effective way. â€¢ To effectively manage an appropriate inventory level. â€¢ To focus on quality, speed of response, flexibility, type cost of the production process. â€¢ Achieve an effective labour/capital production mix. â€¢ To incorporate latest technological approaches into the production process. Sound explanation of two operations management objectives (3 marks) Sound explanation of one objective or partial explanation of two (2 marks) Partial explanation of one objective or a list of two /definition of operations management (1 mark) Page 4 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ October/November 2016 9609 13

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Analyse the qualities of a successful entrepreneur. [8] (b) Discuss why senior managers leading large public limited companies might decide not to have corporate social responsibility (CSR) as a business objective. [12]
mark_scheme_text: 5 (a) Analyse the qualities of a successful entrepreneur. [8] Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the qualities needed by an entrepreneur to be successful 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the qualities needed by an entrepreneur to be successful 5â€“6 2 Some application of entrepreneurial qualities to a business context 3â€“4 1 Understanding of entrepreneurs 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers could include: â€¢ Have innovative and viable business ideas/proposals. â€¢ Willing to take risks. â€¢ Selfâ€“ confident and assertive. â€¢ Multi-skilled. â€¢ Committed and self-motivated. â€¢ Good leaders/motivators. â€¢ Ability to raise finance/convince investors. Perceptive answers may consider how to measure success and/or question the time element of success â€“ for how long will a business survive. (b) Discuss why senior managers leading large public limited companies might decide not to have corporate social responsibility (CSR) as a business objective. [12] Level Description Marks 4 Effective evaluation of why some PLC senior managers would be against CSR as a business objective 9â€“12 3 Limited evaluation of why some PLC senior managers would be against CSR as a business objective 7â€“8 2 Analysis and some application of arguments for not having CSR 3â€“6 1 Understanding of senior managers/CSR/public limited companies 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Page 5 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ October/November 2016 9609 13 Answers may include: â€¢ Some senior managers see CSR (that business should consider the interests of society in its decisions and activities over and above legal responsibilities) as fundamentally flawed and a dangerous distraction from profit seeking and shareholder satisfaction. â€¢ Companies that simply do all they can to boost profits will end up increasing social welfare. It is argued that, for example, producing fuel efficient cars is not about increasing the quality of the environment, but about responding to customer demand for fuel efficient cars. The profit motive will, therefore, lead to successful environmental situations. â€¢ Senior managers should relentlessly pursue profit maximisation â€“ the market response to consumer demand will maximise consumer satisfaction. â€¢ More important to make money than to give it away. â€¢ It is irresponsible to focus on wider community if the business is managed/ led successfully. â€¢ If the aim/objective is â€˜to do goodâ€™ companies may well fail. â€¢ The movement towards CSR is seen to be in direct opposition to the best interests of a business organisation. â€¢ Companies who sacrifice profit for the common good are imposing a tax on shareholders and other company stakeholders. â€¢ An awareness of social consequences of business activity is sufficient â€“ CSR is going too far. So some senior managers may see CSR simply as a financial calculation for the business and are not interested in CSR for PR purposes. This question is NOT a general question on CSR â€“ rather, it is a question about why some PLC senior ma

Nevertheless, quantitative measures of business performance are important and strong answers may well outline the value of financial measures before discussing the case for including qualitative performance measures. â€¢ Recognition of the limitations of financial data and often too little emphasis on the qualitative drivers of performance such as innovation, quality, employee relations, intangible assets. â€¢ Advantages of non-financial methods of performance measurement â€“ drivers to success are often intangible assets â€“ customer loyalty â€“ innovation, management capability, employee relations, brand value may well compose a significant proportion of the value of a business. â€“ non-financial measures are more closely linked to long term strategy of a business â€“ the progress made towards reducing competition, increasing customer loyalty â€“ while traditional measures are more short term. âˆ’ non-financial measures can be better indicators of the long term/future financial performance - R&D decisions made now may well produce longe

The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/22/O/N/16 Fire Fly Ebooks (FFE) FFE is a manufacturer of electronic book readers (e-readers). The product is used to read electronic books which are sold by other businesses. FFEâ€™s product is a simple and inexpensive e-reader with a retail price of between $20 and $30. FFE sells each e-reader to retailers for $10. There are many competitors in the market and FFE has calculated the price elasticity of demand to be very elastic. There are similar products on the market and FFE must try to maintain a Unique Selling Point (USP) for its product. In the past the USP has been its low price; but, as more brands become available, extra features have become more important. These features include: built-in lights, speakers for audio books and voice control to change page. FFE does not currently offer any extra features in its product. FFE has received an order from a new retailer. This order is to be delivered in Month 2. The retailer wants two months to pay in Month 4, so that it has chance to sell the e-readers before paying FFE. This might cause FFE cash flow problems. FFE has produced a cash flow forecast which includes the new order. Table 2: Cash flow forecast for FFE Month

question_text: Insert. DC (NH/SW) 125608/1 [Turn over * 5 1 0 9 4 7 0 2 7 2 * BUSINESS 9609/ Exam Success (ES) Amaya is a sole trader who owns and manages ES. Amaya was a teacher for 20 years. She decided to leave teaching and start up ES to offer individual tuition for students in their own homes. For the first year, Amaya was the only worker, tutoring students for Business qualifications. In the second year she used methods of selection to choose two teachers to join her team. This meant that ES was also able to offer individual tuition in Mathematics and Science. ES has now been trading for five years. Fig. 1 shows the revenue for ES from the first five years of trading. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Case Study October/November 2016 3 hours No Additional Materials are required. READ THESE INSTRUCTIONS FIRST An answer booklet is provided inside this question paper. You should follow the instructions on the front cover of the answer booklet. If you need additional answer paper ask the invigilator for a continuation booklet. Section A Answer all questions. Section B Answer one question. You are advised to spend no more than 40 minutes on Section B. The businesses described in this paper are entirely fictitious. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/31/O/N/16 Kaldi & Akbars (KA) KA was established in 2010 by Anne Styles as a private limited company. Anne identified a gap in the coffee shop market in country Y. KA now has many coffee shops in country Y. Changing perceptions The coffee shop market in country Y is highly competitive. There are many independent shops and several large chains. These large chains have a reputation for offering consistent quality products with excellent customer service. They also have a standardised layout with a clearly identifiable style of decoration and branding. Anneâ€™s vision was to try to change the way people think about coffee. KA sources coffee from an ethical supplier which is committed to promoting the interests of coffee bean farmers. KA provides intensive training for coffee baristas (employees that make the coffee) before they are allowed to make a cup of coffee for a customer. Each KA shop has a unique design. Employees are encouraged to get to know their individual customerâ€™s preferences for coffee. With a high class interior design, its relaxed atmosphere, quality coffee experience and locally sourced food, KA has targeted the premium end of the market. This is reflected in the prices charged for the product range. Digital marketing KAâ€™s approach to marketing has been to utilise digital technologies. KA has used social media to build customer interest and loyalty. In 2015 KA launched a mobile app (software program) that allows customers to use their mobile (cell) phone to pre-order and pay for coffee. This app has provided KA with valuable information about customer preferences and enables KA to target promotional offers at individual customers. Customers select their preferred purchase which can then be seen by the barista in the chosen shop together with the customerâ€™s name and photo. KA has achieved growth without any significant use of traditional forms of advertising. Business growth For the first three years KA pursued an aggressive growth strategy and did not make a profit. Growth was financed largely by borrowing from banks. By the end of 2013 KA had opened 20 shops and consequently gearing reached 80%. In 2014 Gayles, a major supermarket chain in country Y, took a minority shareholding in KA. This provided much needed finance for continued expansion and enabled KA to open coffee shops in Gaylesâ€™ supermarkets as well. Anne remains in f
