question_text: Insert. DC (NF) 133429 [Turn over Cambridge International Examinations Cambridge International Advanced Subsidiary and Advanced Level * 0 4 4 4 6 0 6 9 9 6 * BUSINESS 9609/ Short Answer and Essay October/November 2017 1 hour 15 minutes No Additional Materials are required. READ THESE INSTRUCTIONS FIRST An answer booklet is provided inside this question paper. You should follow the instructions on the front cover of the answer booklet. If you need additional answer paper ask the invigilator for a continuation booklet. Section A Answer all questions. Section B Answer one question. You are advised to spend no more than 35 minutes on Section A. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/11/O/N/17 Section A (Short Answer) Answer all questions. Define the term â€˜channel of distributionâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two factors a manufacturing business might consider in deciding on an appropriate channel of distribution. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term â€˜channel of distributionâ€™. It is the chain of intermediaries a product passes through (1) from producer to consumer. (1) A definition such as this merits 2 marks. A partial definition such as â€˜the way a business distributes its productsâ€™ or â€˜method used by a business to get products to customersâ€™ merits 1 mark. Sound definition (2 marks) Partial definition â€“ limited understanding (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

1(b) Briefly explain two factors a manufacturing business might consider in deciding on an appropriate channel of distribution. Answers may include: Â• cost of different distribution channels e.g. direct selling involves shipping costs and requires the manufacturer to hold inventory. Â• using intermediaries means reduced profits for the manufacturer. Â• efficiency of distribution channel; which will reach the customer the quickest and at the lowest cost to the manufacturer. Â• which channel gives the manufacturer the control they require over how the product is presented to the customer. Â• type of product â€“ industrial products tend to be sold more directly than consumer goods, perishable direct to retailers. Â• the nature of the chosen market â€“ if it is large and dispersed, intermediaries may well be needed. Â• level of service required or demanded by customers â€“ so some products will not be sold via internet (e.g. bespoke products). Â• technical complexity of the product â€“ may be direct selling, e.g. computers. Â• number of customers â€“ if high value product and few customers, direct selling might be used (luxury yacht). NOTE: Some answers refer to methods of transportation but not specific intermediaries. The question is â€˜channel of distributionâ€™ so methods of transportation only cannot gain marks. Sound explanation of two factors (3 marks) Sound explanation of one factor or partial explanation of two factors (2 marks) Partial example of one factor or a list of two factors (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 3 of 10 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜job re-designâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain the benefits to a business of job re-design. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the termâ€™ job re-designâ€™. The re-structuring / altering / changing of a job (1), usually with employee involvement and agreement, (1) to make the work / job more interesting / satisfying / challenging. (1) A definition that merits 2 marks will contain 2 of the 3 points listed above. A partial definition that merits 1 mark will contain 1 of the 3 points above. NOTE: Some answers refer to the role of an employee changing but do not refer to a â€˜jobâ€™ The question is â€˜job re-designâ€™ so explicit reference to a â€˜job/tasksâ€™ must be made for marks to be awarded. Sound definition given (2 marks) Partial definition given (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

2(b) Briefly explain the benefits to a business of job re-design. Answers could include: Job re-design is designed to: Â• make the job holder feel more valued by increasing their role. This leads to maximum output from satisfied workers. Â• increase motivation of workers and hence help to retain them so reducing costs of recruitment. Â• create a right person â€“ right job fit leading to efficient and effective workforce so increasing productivity. Â• adapt the business / job roles to suit changing economic conditions, therefore having the right staff and skills to deal with change so the business can survive/grow. NOTE: Candidates that do not refer to a â€˜jobâ€™ in question

2(a) may nevertheless give acceptable answers in question

2(b) by referring to the impact of job re-design on employees such as new skills and motivation, leading to increased productivity and should be rewarded accordingly. Sound explanation of the benefits of job re-design to a business (3 marks) Limited explanation of the benefits of job re-design (2 marks) Some general comment on job re-design (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 4 of 10 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain how a business might benefit from acting ethically. Answers may include: Acting ethically is regarded as doing the â€˜right thingâ€™ â€“ taking business decisions against a background of certain moral principles â€“ morally correct behaviour. In the short-term there may be a â€˜costâ€™ involved in acting ethically â€“ only doing things in a certain way or not doing certain things. In the long-term there could be substantial benefits: Â• avoid negative publicity. Â• retain customer loyalty â€“ retain/gain sales through high reputational perception. Â• attract ethical customers/investors. Â• attract staff/retain staff. Â• gives a competitive advantage. Â• improved brand and business awareness. Effective explanation of possible benefits to a business of ethical business activities (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of possible benefits of ethical business activities (2â€“3 marks) Understanding of ethical business activity (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 5

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜micro-financeâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two advantages of micro-finance for an entrepreneur. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only.
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) Define the term â€˜micro-financeâ€™. The provision of financial services for poor and low-income customers (1) who do not have access to banking services such as loans and overdrafts offered by traditional commercial banks. (1) Such a definition should be awarded 2 marks. A partial definition such as â€˜the provision of financial services to poor customersâ€™ should be awarded 1 mark. NOTE: Reference to â€˜poorâ€™ or â€˜low-incomeâ€™ must be made for 2 marks to be awarded. Sound definition (2 marks) Partial definition (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 5 of 10 Question Answer Marks

4(b) Briefly explain two advantages of micro-finance for entrepreneurs. Â• entrepreneurs use micro-finance to start businesses. Â• entrepreneurs use micro-finance to operate and grow businesses. Â• this provides an income and average incomes increase. Â• provide finance which otherwise would not be available to high risk entrepreneurs with no credit score. Â• such finance is likely to be at lower interest rates than traditional banks making it easier to pay back. Â• small capital sums in the form of loans to poor aspiring entrepreneurs (especially in rural areas) have encouraged small businesses to be set up where previously there was no access to such funds. Â• evidence that women, who traditionally have been denied financial services, have benefitted from micro-finance opportunities. Â• growth of local economies; incomes rise and families are able to give education to their families. Â• micro-finance institutions stimulate economic activity and development particularly through entrepreneurial activity â€“ the multiplier effect. Sound explanation of two advantages of micro-finance for entrepreneurs (3 marks) Sound explanation of one advantage or partial explanation of two advantages (2 marks) Partial explanation of one advantage or list of two (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 6 of 10 Question Answer Marks

5(a) Analyse the role of marketing in adding value to a product Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of how marketing can add value to a product. 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of how marketing can add value to a product. 5â€“6 2 Some application of how marketing can add value to a product or convince customers that a product is more desirable. 3â€“4 1 Understanding of value added and/or marketing. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers may include: Candidates may well use the 4Ps framework to answer this question, but must link to adding value to gain Level 2. Marketing is linking the production of goods and services to the consumer profitably and satisfying business and consumer objectives. Adding value is the difference between the cost of purchasing bought in materials and the price finished goods/services are sold for â€“ the greater the difference the greater the potential for more profit. Marketing / market research / market intelligence can add value by: Â• identifying how to get a product to the right customers more effectively/cheaply so reducing cost and adding value. Â• determining the right offer price to set for the target market so creating the optimal amount of added value for that target market. Â• creating a distinctive brand that becomes a â€˜must haveâ€™ name that consumers will pay a premium price for. Â• increase the perception of this product being more valuable so customers are willing to pay more for it. Â• positioning a business/product/creating a USP so that it stands out from the competition; customers likely to buy the product even if the price is higher than rivals. Â• creating an exclusive and luxurious retail environment to convince customers to pay more for a â€˜luxuriousâ€™ product â€“ cosmetic retailers, luxury cars. Â• using high quality packaging to differentiate a product â€“ perfume â€“ luxury chocolates and make it appear worth the higher price. Â• promoting the benefits of a product to achieve a result / outcome / emotion â€“ not just a commodity so higher price is justified. Â• including testimonials in advertising to suggest social proof that the product should be bought at the higher price. 8 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 7 of 10 Question Answer Marks

[2] (b) Briefly explain two aims of a social enterprise. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term â€˜social enterpriseâ€™. A social enterprise is a business that has mainly social objectives (1); reinvests profits to benefit society (1); a business whose main focus is not necessarily the making or maximising of profits (1); that pursues the triple bottom line objectives (1); that engages in activities/social objectives such as creating work for the unemployed or producing in an environmentally friendly way (1). A definition that merits 2 marks will contain 2 of the 5 points listed above. A partial definition that merits just 1 mark will contain only 1 of the 5 points above. Sound definition (2 marks) Partial definition â€“ limited understanding (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

1(b) Briefly explain two aims of a social enterprise. NOTE: Questions

1(a) and

1(b) may well attract similar relevant points/information. Repeated material in

1(b) is perfectly acceptable and should be rewarded as long as it is explained or developed as evidence for a brief explanation of distinctive social enterprise aims. Answers could include: Â• a social enterprise may not focus on making profits or maximising profits like a normal business but rather seek to secure enough resources to provide services to the community Â• a social enterprise may seek to achieve a triple bottom line set of objectives rather than simply generate profits or maximise sales, or grow the business Â• a social enterprise may have as its primary purpose the provision of jobs to local disadvantaged citizens who may have difficulty in securing employment in traditional ways Â• a social enterprise may engage in business activities such as sustainable production to support and protect the environment Â• a social enterprise may seek to give employees a â€˜living wageâ€™ and a supportive working environment at the expense of maximising profits Two distinct economic aims, two social aims or two environmental aims are worthy of full marks. Examples may be given but they are NOT required as part of an answer. Sound explanation of two social enterprise aims (3 marks) Sound explanation of one social enterprise aim or two partially explained (2 marks) Partial explanation of one social enterprise aim or a list of two (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 3 9609/12 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 3 of 10 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Define the term â€˜retained earningsâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two uses of income statements for senior managers. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜retained earningsâ€™. Retained earnings (profit) is the profit left after all deductions, including dividends, have been made (1); this is ploughed back into the business as a source of finance (1). Part of the profit that is reinvested in the business (1) rather than distributed to shareholders. (1) A definition that merits 2 marks will contain 2 of the 4 points listed above. A partial definition could be â€˜money put back in the businessâ€™ worth 1 mark or contains only 1 of the 4 points above. 2(b) Briefly explain two uses of income statements for senior managers. Answers could include: Â• income statements record the revenue, costs and profit (or loss) of a business over a given time Â• provides information on the gross and operating profit of a company and shows how the operating profit is split between dividends to shareholders and retained profits Â• can be used to measure and compare business performance over time and with other companies (ratios can be calculated to help with this analysis) Â• used to see where costs could be reduced to increase profits Â• actual profit data can be compared with expected levels of profit Â• provide evidence for a bank loan Â• all this information is useful for senior managers as they review the health of the organisation and make corporate decisions Â• as important internal stakeholders they may well use the information of a strong set of figures in the income statement to push for improved rates of remuneration and/or propose changes in direction for the business Â• the information in the income statement will provide vital management information as senior managers review and plan for the future of the business Sound explanation of two uses of income statements for senior managers Sound explanation of one use or partial explanation of two uses of income statements for senior managers (2 marks) Partial explanation of one use or a list of two uses for senior managers. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜product orientationâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two advantages of customer (market) orientation to a business. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only.
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) Define the term â€˜product orientationâ€™. Product orientation is said to be an inward-looking marketing approach (1) which has a focus on making products (1) and invests a high proportion of resources on product features rather than marketing (1). The product is made without carrying out market research (1) and then the business tries to find customers to buy it (1). A definition that merits 2 marks will contain 2 of the 5 points listed above. A partial definition worth 1 mark will contain only 1 of the 5 points above. Sound definition (2 marks) Partial definition (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

4(b) Briefly explain two advantages of customer (market) orientation to a business. Â• this is an outward-looking marketing approach where product decisions are based on customer demand as determined by market research. Â• it is said to be very important in fast-changing, volatile, consumer markets as it allows speedy response. Â• market research feedback allows production and marketing to be adapted to the needs of customers before competitors get there first. Â• it may well reduce number of market failures for newly-developed products. Â• it reduces high cost wastage of producing an unwanted product. Â• if business responds continually to customer preferences it is more likely to be a sustainable business. Sound explanation of two advantages to a business of customer orientation. Sound explanation of one advantage to a business of customer orientation or partial explanation of two advantages. (2 marks) Partial explanation of one advantage to a business or list of two (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 3 9609/12 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 6 of 10 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Analyse how a business might use a price discrimination strategy to increase profit. [8] (b) Discuss why both the finance department and the operations department of a car manufacturing company should be involved in new product development. [12]
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse how a business might use a price discrimination strategy to increase profit. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of how a business might use a price discrimination strategy to increase profit 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of how a business might use a price discrimination strategy to increase profit 5â€“6 2 Some application of price discrimination to business pricing decisions. 3â€“4 1 Understanding of price discrimination. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers may include: A definition of price discrimination â€“ the charging of different groups of consumers different prices for the same product giving opportunities to increase profit (as opposed to a single price for all these different markets). Conditions required for successful price discrimination policies may well be spelt out: Â• company must be able to identify different market segments. Â• different segments must have different price elasticities (PED). Â• market segments must be kept separate either by time, physical distance, or nature of use (e.g. Microsoft Office â€“ available at a discount only to educational institutions). Â• there must be no seepage between the segmented markets. Â• the company must have some degree of monopoly power. Examples of possible price discrimination policies to improve profit: Â• rail travellers sub-divided between commuter and casual travellers; Â• cinemas selling differently priced tickets for adults, children, students, seniors; Â• different prices for export market products from those sold in home country; Â• splitting a market between peak and off-peak use; Â• charging many different prices for airline travel. 8 9609/12 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 7 of 10 Question Answer Marks

5(b) Discuss why both the finance department and the operations department of a car manufacturing company should be involved in new product development. Â• major finance required for car design and development. Â• to support innovative technology, design and production. Â• cost and value management, financial planning for new product platforms. New product development clearly is a concern of operations management and the operations department will be invol

Answers may include: Â• ensure that each business unit is compatible with others in the business portfolio. Â• concerned with long-term business performance and priorities. Â• see that all sections of a business contribute to corporate success. Â• set the framework for departmental objectives. Â• corporate objectives in a single business company might include growth, profit maximisation, market share increase. Â• they are all overall strategic objectives. Departmental objectives : each department of a business will be constrained by the corporate objectives and will set departmental objectives that will specifically support and sustain the corporate objectives. Answers may include: Â• marketing may have a departmental objective to improve sales through more effective sales promotions. Â• finance may have an objective of reducing long-term borrowing by 5% or reducing costs by 10%. Â• operations may have an objective of introducing a new product each year. Each functional department will develop functional level strategies that make processes and the value chain more efficient through clearly defined objectives and thus support and sustain the corporate objectives and the success of the business. 8 9609/12 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 10 of 10 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Define the term â€˜induction trainingâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two benefits to a business of training employees. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜induction trainingâ€™. An introductory training programme for new business recruits (1) to provide awareness of the systems and practices used in the business (1). A definition such as this merits 2 marks. A partial definition that merits 1 mark will contain 1 of the 2 points above. Sound definition given (2 marks) Partial definition given (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

2(b) Briefly explain two benefits to a business of training employees Answers could include benefits such as: Â• the business chooses what skills to give to its employees. Â• you can target the skills needed now and for the future. Â• you give a message to employees that they are valued. Â• improves staff loyalty and retention. Â• may well improve performance/productivity/motivation. Â• business can get a competitive advantage with a skilled workforce. Â• strengthens the intellectual capital of the business. Â• an attractive recruitment tool. Â• increases the adaptability of the business and assists change. Â• potentially improves staff morale/performance and profits. Â• employees are said to be important business stakeholders and to have important responsibilities in respect of business performance. Â• employees are said to have specific responsibilities to the business that employs them: â€“ to meet the conditions and requirements of their employment contracts. â€“ to co-operate with management in all reasonable requests. â€“ to observe the ethical code of conduct or values in a mission statement. â€“ to be honest and respect the rights of fellow workers. â€“ to fulfil Health and Safety requirements. question_text: Define the term â€˜economies of scaleâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two economies of scale. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only.
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) Define the term â€˜economies of scaleâ€™. Defined as reductions in the unit costs of a business (1) resulting from an increase in the scale of operations (1). A definition such as this merits 2 marks. A partial definition that merits 1 mark will contain 1 of the 2 points above. Sound definition (2 marks) Partial definition (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

4(b) Briefly explain two economies of scale. Answers may include: Â• purchasing economies â€“ discounts for bulk buying. Â• technical economies â€“ use of flow production lines and sophisticated computer equipment; high cost equipment spread over large-scale production. Â• financial economies â€“ large firms advantaged in raising loan finance and at better rates. Â• marketing economies â€“ these costs (e.g. sales force) spread over a higher level of sales. Â• managerial economies â€“ large businesses able to employ more specialised managers. Sound explanation of two economies of scale. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one economy of scale or partial explanation of two . (2 marks) Partial explanation of one economy of scale or a list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 3 9609/13 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 6 of 11 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Analyse why primary market research could be more useful to a business than secondary market research. [8] (b) Discuss the advantages and disadvantages to a business of using focus groups to collect market information on a new product. [12]
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse why primary market research could be more useful to a business than secondary market research. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of why primary research could be more useful to a business than secondary research. 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of why primary research could be more useful to a business than secondary research. 5â€“6 2 Some application of why primary research could be more useful to a business than secondary research. 3â€“4 1 Understanding of primary research. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers may include: It may well depend on the type of business and its requirement. Definitions may well be given of both types of market research. Primary research and its advantages will be described. Advantages/disadvantages of primary market research Â• Up-to-date information. Â• Relevant information â€“ collected specifically for a business. Â• Examples such as evidence for pricing policy, extent and type of market/ customer demand. Â• A business has a higher level of control over how the information is collected. Â• Information can be kept hidden from competitors. Advantages/disadvantages of secondary market research Â• Information readily available. Â• Information relatively inexpensive. Â• Can be substantial information available. Â• Can be out-of-date information. Â• Information can be misleading. Â• Might not be specific to the businessâ€™ needs. 8 9609/13 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 7 of 11 Question Answer Marks

In this context, a new product, views gained on, for example, its price in relation to competition; its promotion/packaging; how it performs/might perform; pros/cons. Advantages of focus groups Â• Information considered to be more accurate than consumer surveys (interviews and questionnaires). Â• The discussion allows perceptions and opinions to be recorded. Â• More cost-effective than individual interviews. Â• Offer the opportunity to seek clarification of views. Â• Can provide ideas for improvement of a product. Â• Provide information about the reaction of consumers to design and message of the product. Â• Researcher can interact and stimulate the group. question_text: Data Response October/November 2017 1 hour 30 minutes No Additional Materials are required. READ THESE INSTRUCTIONS FIRST An answer booklet is provided inside this question paper. You should follow the instructions on the front cover of the answer booklet. If you need additional answer paper ask the invigilator for a continuation booklet. Answer all questions. The businesses described in this paper are entirely fictitious. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/21/O/N/17 Car Wash (CW) CW is a national chain of car wash businesses. Each car wash is a franchise of CW and is owned and managed by an entrepreneur who is local to the area where the car wash is based. As well as washing cars, each CW franchise offers customers the opportunity to have the interiors of their cars cleaned for an additional payment. Every entrepreneur who wants to set up a CW franchise must present a plan to CW for the business, including market research information. An entrepreneur called Palesh has recently submitted a proposal for a new franchise in a town that does not currently have a CW car wash. Palesh selected a sample of people on the main street in the town to carry out his market research. He asked them about how often they would use the car wash service and how much they would be willing to pay. Based on his market research, Palesh has forecast the cash flow for the first two months of trading (see Table 1). Palesh will have an opening balance of $10 000 at the start of Month 1. Table 1: Cash flow forecast Month ($) Cash inflow 20 000 20 000 Cash outflow 25 000 10 000 CW car washes are labour intensive businesses. Employees work outside and they often get very wet from the water used. Each car is washed by two employees, one for the wheels and one for the rest of the car. A third employee is responsible for cleaning the interior of the car if this is required by the customer. Palesh plans to employ 12 employees to work in shifts at the car wash. The opening hours will be 06:00 until 22:00, seven days a week. Palesh is aware that he will need to keep costs low, so he will pay each employee a low hourly wage. However, he is also aware that this may lead to human resource problems. (a) (i) Define the term â€˜franchiseâ€™ (line 1). [2] (ii) Briefly explain the term â€˜entrepreneurâ€™ (line 2). [3] (b) (i) Refer to Table 1 and any other relevant information. Calculate the forecast closing balance at the end of Month 2. [3] (ii) Explain one benefit for Palesh of producing a cash flow forecast. [3] (c) Analyse one advantage and one disadvantage to Palesh of how he carried out his market research. [8] (d) Recommend how Palesh could motivate his employees. [11] 4 9609/21/O/N/17 BLANK PAGE Permission to reproduce items where third-party owned material protected by copyright is included has been sought and cleared where possible. question_text: Insert. DC (ST) 134588/2 [Turn over Cambridge International Examinations Cambridge International Advanced Subsidiary and Advanced Level BUSINESS 9609/ Occasion Cards (OC) OC is a private limited company that sells greeting cards designed using mass customisation. Customers order their cards on the Internet and can add their own design and personal message to each card. OC allows customers to download a simple Computer Aided Design (CAD) system that customers use on their home computers. OC is owned by four sisters who are also the directors of the company. OCâ€™s employees benefit from the business being owned by a family. However, family ownership has limited the capital available for expansion. OC has increased its sales over the past few years using the Internet. Social media and viral marketing have been the basis for most of OCâ€™s promotion. This has worked well, but the directors are aware that future sales growth may require a different approach and an increase in capital. The current owners have decided to change the legal structure of OC to a public limited company. In preparation for this change, the directors have prepared some accounting information for OC (see Table 1). Table 1: Accounting information for OC 2015 ($m) 2016 ($m) Revenue 20

Nathan is a fully trained and qualified computer engineer. Nathan buys broken computers from large electrical retailers and repairs them. The repaired computers are then sold through NNâ€™s website. Nathan uses a cost-based pricing strategy and adds 50% to the cost of the computer and the cost of repairs. Nathan currently works alone and has no employees. Nathan has recently purchased, for $2000, a batch of 20 laptop computers all of which have broken screens. Nathan thinks that each will cost $50 to repair and he hopes to make a profit from selling them. One of Nathanâ€™s biggest problems is cash flow. Nathan pays for all of his inventory in cash and he must spend time repairing the computers before he can sell them. Customers expect a range of computers to choose from and expect next day delivery. This means that most of Nathanâ€™s working capital is tied up in inventory. Sometimes Nathan is offered large batches of computers and he cannot buy them because he does not have the cash available. Nathan wants to recruit an employee to allow him to increase the production at NN. He has interviewed two potential employees and he has made some notes about their suitability (see Table 2). question_text: Insert. DC (ST) 134596/2 [Turn over BUSINESS 9609/ Jones Sticky Labels (JS) JS is a private limited company that produces sticky labels for the industrial market. JS was set up by Lucy Jones 15 years ago. The market for sticky labels is very specialised and there are no competitors within the home country because of low demand. However, the majority of JSâ€™s customers are in other countries and the international market is much more competitive. JS owns one factory where all the sticky labels are manufactured. The business employs 300 workers on eight production lines. The sticky labels are designed using Computer Aided Design (CAD) and manufactured using batch production. When a customer wants sticky labels, JS will create a new design, send the design to the customer for approval and then order the materials required for production. Each design of sticky label is unique and will take different amounts of time and effort to produce. The typical lead time is four weeks. One of JSâ€™s customers is a supermarket chain which puts sticky labels on many of its products. These range from promotional sticky labels that are put on products that need to be sold quickly through to small sticky labels that are put on fruit to identify their variety. JS has recently received an order from the supermarket chain for 50 000 sticky labels at a total price of $2750. The costs of producing these labels is shown in Table 1. Table 1: Cost data ($) Variable Costs: Cost of materials (per 1000 sticky labels)

Cambridge International Examinations Cambridge International Advanced Subsidiary and Advanced Level 2 9609/23/O/N/17 Perfik Plumbing (PP) PP is a plumbing business owned and managed by Navpreet. She set up PP as a sole trader three years ago when she qualified as a plumber. The majority of Navpreetâ€™s work is repairing and fitting water pipes in customersâ€™ homes. However, there is also a growing market for fitting new appliances, such as washing machines and dishwashers, in customersâ€™ homes. Navpreet is the only female plumber in her town. This is her Unique Selling Point (USP). Her business has been reasonably successful. Navpreet has relied on recommendations from satisfied customers to promote her business. However, if PP is to grow in the future Navpreet will need to use new promotional methods. In the past, Navpreet has used a cost-based pricing strategy, but she believes she may have been setting her prices too low. Navpreet did a survey of her customers to find out how much they would be willing to pay per hour of labour (see Table 2). Table 2: Survey data Price per hour ($) Number of customers 24 10 30 8 36

question_text: Depreciation policy. In 2018 KKS plans to invest $20m in IT equipment. The current depreciation policy would be to depreciate these assets over a five year period, with an estimated residual value of $5m. The Finance Director is proposing a new depreciation policy which would reduce the period of depreciation to three years with a residual value for these assets of $8m. Marketing strategy in country X KKS used to promote its services only using trade journals and business fairs. However, three years ago KKS invested in a website and this now produces the majority of sales. This has contributed to KKS gaining a high market share in the B2B segment, supported by competitive pricing and good customer service. This segment is a low growth part of the total parcel delivery market, whilst B2C and C2C are growing rapidly. KKS is planning to integrate the marketing and distribution network IT systems. Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) software could be used to minimise the costs of moving vehicles to match customer requirements. Costs would then fall and more contracts gained. Market analysis: options A and B The Marketing Director and Finance Director have also researched the B2C and C2C segments. They have set out the following two options for expansion. 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 3 9609/31/O/N/17 [Turn over Option A : Enter the B2C segment. KKS would need to make some changes to existing marketing methods. The competition is very strong, customers are price sensitive and profit margins are small. Option B : Enter the C2C segment. Competition is weak. Staff training and operational costs will be high. Parcel collection locations will be needed. A different marketing approach will be needed. The directors have carried out decision tree analysis to compare the likely returns from expanding into the B2C and C2C segments. Appendix 2 shows some results of their analysis. The directors have assumed costs and revenue will remain constant for each of the first three years. Managing the workforce KKS directors view the workforce as a key component in being the most reliable delivery service. There are 8500 employees in 64 warehouses and two administration centres. Employees are placed in one of the following departments: Operations, Administration and Finance, IT, Marketing, Human Resources. Within each department every manager has a wide span of control. KKS invests in its employees through developing their skills and enabling clear career progression. Most supervisors have been promoted from inside KKS. New employees have a training needs analysis and their line manager discusses possible training opportunities with them. Employees are encouraged to contribute to decision making. They are given encouragement to gain experience through job rotation and there is a generous bonus for good performance. In return KKS expects commitment, enthusiasm and hard work. question_text: Dividend yield 4.3% Factory Q â€“ a case for closure? ADP has expanded through external growth over the last 20 years. It has acquired some factories that perform less satisfactorily than others. Factory Q is a good example. This factory produces small domestic refrigerators. As with all ADPâ€™s factories, it is operated as a profit centre. It has recorded an adverse profit variance for the last three years. Machinery and equipment in this factory was last updated in 2005. Some directors want to close the factory and outsource production. The Finance Director has prepared a variance analysis table for Factory Q for the last three months. See Appendix 1. Market development â€“ sell in the USA? ADP does not currently sell its products in the USA. The safety standards for electrical goods are higher, and consumer protection laws stricter, than in most of ADPâ€™s markets. Consumer expectations in the USA are for high quality products. The average size of domestic appliances is bigger than the rest of the world. However, the total size of the USA market is, according to Asif, the main reason why ADP should aim to launch its products there in 2018. The Marketing Director has the responsibility for preparing a marketing plan for the sale of ADP products in the USA. He suggested to Asif that: â€˜Long term sales forecasts will be the most important factor in our new marketing plan to break into the USA market.â€™ Country Z factory â€“ improving employee performance ADP is a multinational business that takes advantage of differences in wage rates and employment laws between the countries it operates in. For example, country Z is a low income developing country. It has few employment laws to protect workers. ADP benefits from this situation and pays its employees in its country Z factory less than 50% of the average ADP wage rate. This ADP policy of treating employees in different countries in different ways was defended by the Human Resources Director at a press conference. She held this press conference after an accident in the country Z factory that badly injured four employees. â€˜It is true that this factory does not have the safety equipment that we install in our other factories around the world. However, ADP meets all its health and safety obligations under the law in country Z. Our employees here are paid more than other similar workers in the country. Unemployment is high so they know that other opportunities are limited.â€™ The Operations manager of ADPâ€™s factory in country Z is aware that employee performance is a real problem for the company. Based on full costing Â• Variable feed costs per litre = 8 cents Â• Dairy farm labour costs per litre = 6 cents [9000/150 000] 1 Â• Overhead costs allocated to farm = 0.05 x Â£15.6m/52 = $15 000 per week = 10 cents per litre 2 Â• Total full cost per litre = 24 cents 3 Â• Cost of buying in milk = 20 cents Â• Total cost saving per week = 150 000 x $0.04 = $6000 4 Â• Annual cost saving [additional profit] = $6000 x 52 = $312 000 5 Marginal/contribution costing Â• Cost of buying milk 20 cents. Â• Variable cost of dairy farm = 0.08 + 0.06 = 0.14 2 Â• Thus, extra contribution of dairy relative to buying milk is 0.06 per lit 4 Â• So contribution of milk division is really $9000 per week 5 Â• So profit would fall by $468 000 per year if the farm/milk division was closed. 6 6

Analysis Â• Analysis of marginal costing approach to making the decision. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Short Answer and Essay May/June 2018 1 hour 15 minutes No Additional Materials are required. READ THESE INSTRUCTIONS FIRST An answer booklet is provided inside this question paper. You should follow the instructions on the front cover of the answer booklet. If you need additional answer paper ask the invigilator for a continuation booklet. Section A Answer all questions. Section B Answer one question. You are advised to spend no more than 35 minutes on Section A. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/11/M/J/18 Section A (Short Answer) Answer all questions. Define the term â€˜market researchâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two advantages to a business of primary (field) market research. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term â€˜market researchâ€™. The process of collecting data about customers or markets or competition (1) and analysing such data (1). Finding out what consumers want or need (1) before a product is made (1). Process of gathering primary or secondary data (1) using research methods (e.g. surveys, interviews and product testing) (1) Sound definition â€“ 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition â€“ 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

1(b) Briefly explain two advantages to a business of primary (field) market research data. The advantages of primary (field) market research data could include: Primary market research is first-hand data directly related to the needs of a business. Â• It is up-to-date and thus more useful than dated secondary research. Â• Relevant â€“ it is collected for a specific purpose and answers the questions a business asks. Â• The business has direct control over what and how information is collected. Â• The information collected is confidential and can be hidden from competitors. Â• Allows the targeting of customer groups. Sound explanation of two advantages of primary research data. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one advantage or partial explanation of two advantages. (2 marks) Partial explanation of one advantage or a list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 5 of 12 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜productivityâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two ways a manufacturing business could improve productivity. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜productivityâ€™. The effectiveness/efficiency of productive effort (1) the amount of output produced (1) against the inputs used to produce (1) a measure of efficiency (1) the contribution of a worker (1) in a certain time (1). Productivity could be expressed as a formula for 2 marks: Output Productivity Time Period (2) OR Output No. of employees (2) Sound definition â€“ 2 of the one-mark factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition â€“ 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

2(b) Briefly explain two ways a manufacturing business could improve productivity. Answers may include: Â• Improve the training of employees to raise skill levels. Â• Improve employee motivation â€“ intrinsic and extrinsic rewards. Â• Invest in more advanced technology. Â• More efficient management of employees. Â• Set clear goals and measure performance. Â• Recruit better employees. Â• Give better feedback. Â• Improve quality assurance/control. Â• Reduce waste. Â• Introduce Kaizen/cell production. Â• Improved communication. Sound explanation of two ways to improve productivity. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one way or partial explanation of two . (2 marks) Partial explanation of one way or a list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 6 of 12 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain how ethics may influence the objectives of a business. Answers could include the following: Â• The moral principles that guide the way a business behaves â€“ distinguishing between â€˜rightâ€™ and â€˜wrongâ€™ and then making the â€˜rightâ€™ choice. Â• The objectives of a business may include survival, growth, market domination, profit, and the pursuit of any of these may well involve ethical dilemmas. Ethics may influence business objectives in the following ways: Â• Ethics prevent a business pursuing negative objectives such as profit maximisation at any cost. Â• Prevents a business exploiting workers to maximise profits by the government setting minimum wage levels. Â• Prevents a business exploiting suppliers to maximise profits (preventing late or slow invoice payments). Â• Prevents a business deciding on a low cost location in a country that has few regulations e.g. child labour, lax pollution laws, poor health and safety, bribery, slavery. Â• Encourages CSR. Â• Encourages recycling. Â• Encourages employee welfare. Â• Encourages sustainable production. Effective explanation of how ethics may influence the objectives of a business. (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of how ethics may influence the objectives of a business. (2â€“3 marks) Understanding of ethics/objectives. (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 5 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 7 of 12 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜margin of safetyâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two limitations of break-even analysis. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only.
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) Define the term â€˜margin of safetyâ€™. The amount by which the sales level exceeds the break-even level of output (2) OR an indication of how much sales could fall without the business falling into loss (2) OR the difference between the actual output and the break-even level of output (2) OR actual/budgeted sales â€“ break-even sales (2). The amount by which a business has passed its break-even level (1) Sound definition is for any one of the 2â€“mark definitions shown above (2 marks) Partial definition is for the 1â€“mark shown above (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

4(b) Briefly explain two limitations of break-even analysis. Answers could include the following: Â• It is supply side only (costs). Â• It tells you nothing about what sales are actually likely to be for the product at various prices. Â• Not all costs can be conveniently classified into fixed and variable costs. Â• It assumes that fixed costs are constant/they can increase Â• Variable costs do not always stay the same. Â• Sales are unlikely to be the same as predicted output. Â• Break-even analysis assumes all output is sold. Â• There is likely to be some build-up of stock and/or wasted output. Â• Assumes external environment is stable. Â• Depends on accuracy of data â€“ it is only a forecast. Â• It is only useful for one product. Sound explanation of two limitations of break-even analysis. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one limitation of break-even analysis or partial explanation of two . (2 marks) Partial explanation of one limitation or a list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 8 of 12 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Analyse why accurate cost data is important for all businesses. [8] (b) Discuss why a new business should focus more on managing its cash than making a profit. [12] 6 â€˜A business mission statement is only important if it directly affects the strategy and tactics of that business.â€™ Discuss this view. [20]
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse why accurate cost data is important for all businesses. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of why accurate cost data is important for all businesses 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of why accurate cost data is important for all businesses 5â€“6 2 Some explanation/application of why accurate cost data is important for all businesses 3â€“4 1 Understanding of cost data 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers may include the following: Â• Accurate cost data is at the heart of current and future operations and decisions of business organisations. Â• If costs are not recorded accurately, then managers have incomplete /misleading information on which to make decisions. Â• Setting prices (e.g. cost-plus) requires accurate cost data. Â• Calculations of gross margin/gross profit will lead to misleading information in the Income Statement if cost data is not accurate. Â• The Balance Sheet will not be accurate unless inventory costs are correct. Â• The valuation of assets on the Balance Sheet will be inaccurate if inventory costs are incorrect. Â• Cost analysis is vital if costs are to be controlled and resources used efficiently. Â• Cash flow forecasts need to be accurate if they are to be useful. Â• Break-even estimates rely on accurate costing. Â• With accurate cost data a business can run more effectively â€“ e.g. sales prices can be set to a more competitive margin. Â• Helps with good decision-making â€“ e.g. investment of funds in new directions/cost cutting for savings both depend heavily on correct cost estimates. 8 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 9 of 12 Question Answer Marks

5(b) Discuss why a new business should focus more on managing its cash than making a profit. Level Description Marks 4 Effective evaluation of why a new business should focus more on managing its cash than making a profit 9â€“12 3 Limited evaluation of why a new business should focus more on managing its cash than making a profit 7â€“8 2 Analysis and some application of why a business should focus on managing its cash and/or making a profit 3â€“6 1 Understanding of new business/cash/profit 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers may include the following: Â• Cash flow is critical for new businesses. Â• It is common for profitable businesses to run out of cash. Â• It is vital to have sufficient cash in the short term â€“ profit may have to wait for the long term. Â• It is important that the distinction between cash and profit is recognised by the business. Â• New businesses are often given much less time to pay suppliers, given shorter credit periods. Â• Banks and lenders will require quick and agreed time repayment. Â• Finance may be difficult for new businesses to obtain, so they need to build up their own cash reserves. Â• So, cash flow management is critical for new business survival. Â• The process of generating revenue requires cash, so especially for a new business, cash is more important than profit. Â• A focus on cash in the early days of a new busin

mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term â€˜inventory managementâ€™. The activities involved in maintaining an appropriate/optimal amount/ level of inventory (1) to assist cash flow and keep costs low (1). To ensure uninterrupted production/service levels (1). The management/control/organisation of stock items/suppliers (1). Overseeing ordering of inventory / storage of inventory (1). The efficient control of stock levels (1). One type is JIT (allow other methods) (1). 1(b) Briefly explain two reasons why a business might decide to hold a high level of inventory. The reasons for holding a high level of inventory could include: Â• A buffer against anticipated high demand. Â• Respond to seasonal variations in demand. Â• To eliminate/reduce the risk of stock-out costs. Â• In anticipation of future supply problems. Â• To provide sufficient levels of inventory to meet customer demand. Â• To take advantage of discounts for bulk purchasing. Â• To respond to anticipated future price rises by suppliers. Â• To improve customer service with high levels of product/service availability. Â• The stock is not perishable/does not deteriorate. Â• To reduce transportation costs. Â• There is extra space available. Answers could relate to a specific business or a specific product within a business. Sound explanation of two reasons for holding a high level of inventory. Â• Work time can be spent on personal issues and/or socialising. Â• Labour turnover may increase (best workers may leave). Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of why a train company might use a strategy of price discrimination 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of why a train company might use a strategy of price discrimination 5â€“6 2 Some explanation / application of why a train company might use a strategy of price discrimination 3â€“4 1 Understanding of price discrimination / pricing strategy options 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers may include the following: Â• Price discrimination is a pricing strategy by a business to charge different prices to different customers for the same product. Â• Price discrimination is not price differentiation such as different prices for different services such as first and second class. Â• The train company may be able to segment its market into groups of passengers having similar characteristics. Â• If able to keep these segments apart it can sell different priced tickets according to their reasons for travel â€“ business and leisure. Â• Can be used to attract passengers at times when trains may not be filled to capacity e.g. off-peak. Â• Market conditions and operational opportunities provide a range of pricing options for the business. Â• The price elasticity of demand for a product (e.g. inelastic demand) may provide an opportunity for a business to adopt a price discrimination strategy. Â• The train company can sell different priced tickets and maximise revenue and potentially increase profits and profitability. 8 9609/12 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 9 of 12 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Short Answer and Essay May/June 2018 1 hour 15 minutes No Additional Materials are required. READ THESE INSTRUCTIONS FIRST An answer booklet is provided inside this question paper. You should follow the instructions on the front cover of the answer booklet. If you need additional answer paper ask the invigilator for a continuation booklet. Section A Answer all questions. Section B Answer one question. You are advised to spend no more than 35 minutes on Section A. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/13/M/J/18 Section A (Short Answer) Answer all questions. Define the term â€˜leadershipâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two styles of business leadership. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term â€˜leadershipâ€™. Leadership involves giving clear direction / vision for a business (1), setting an example and engaging others to follow (1), motivating / inspiring / guiding a group of people (1) towards achieving a common objective / goal / aim (1). Giving long term direction to an organisation / taking the business forward (1). Giving a new direction for a business (1). Definitions only of â€˜managementâ€™ such as setting objectives, organising resources, controlling, etc., that mention none of the above leadership definitional features should be awarded 0 marks. Sound definition 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

1(b) Briefly explain two styles of business leadership. Explanations could include: Â• Autocratic / authoritarian â€“ all decisions kept at top of the organisation or with the leader â€“ impact on employees / business operations. Â• Democratic â€“ workers encouraged to take part in decisions â€“impact on employees / business operations. Â• Paternalistic â€“ leader will listen and consult but will take decisions themselves as â€˜they know bestâ€™ â€“ impact on employees / business operations. Â• Laissez-faire â€“ â€˜hands offâ€™ approach â€“ opposite of autocratic â€“ most decisions left to workforce â€“ impact on employees / business operations. A â€˜soundâ€™ explanation of a style of business leadership (2 marks) should comment on the impact of that style on employees or the business. A â€˜partialâ€™ explanation will simply define a style of leadership with no reference to impact. Sound explanation of two styles of business leadership. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one style or partial explanation of two . (2 marks) Partial explanation of one style or a list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/13 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 5 of 12 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Define the term â€˜operations managementâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two ways in which changes in technology might affect the operations management of a business. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜operations managementâ€™. The process of managing resources e.g. labour / capital / stock at the operational level (1) to achieve efficient production (1) and provision of goods / services (1). Managing/planning the transformation process â€“ inputs into outputs (1) managing the production process (1). Deciding what to produce (1). Deciding on method of production e.g. using technology (1). The scale of production (1). What materials to use (1). The process of adding value (1). Quality control (1). Sound definition lists 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition lists 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

2(b) Briefly describe two ways in which changes in technology might affect the operations management of a business. Answers could include the following: Â• Technological change / advancement could mean greater efficiency in productive process. Â• However, failures in newly installed technology may cause disruption to production. Â• Might require business to purchase new technically advanced machinery â€“ new investment required. Â• Changes in technology will take time to implement. Â• Businesses that do not invest in and use new technology may fall behind competitors. Â• Impact on employees â€“ new training required to work new advanced machinery. Â• Might mean fewer staff in operations management. Sound explanation of two ways operations management might be affected. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one way or partial explanation of two . (2 marks) Partial explanation of one way or a list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/13 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 6 of 12 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain why the objectives of a business may change over time. Answers could include the following: Â• Business objectives are the stated measurable targets (tactical, strategic, corporate, departmental) that move an organisation to achieve its aims and purpose. Â• Objectives can include survival, growth, profit maximisation, sales growth, socially responsible aspirations. Â• Changes may take place in response to: â€“ â€“ Initial objectives achieved (survival). â€“ Competitive environment changes. â€“ New leadership and management. â€“ Technology changes â€“ new production possibilities. â€“ New opportunities arise â€“ international trade. â€“ Economic / external situation changes â€“ e.g. recession. â€“ Unique products are no longer unique. â€“ Customer demands change. â€“ Change of ownership. An effective explanation of why the objectives of a business may change over time will clearly identify specific objectives that may change and will give an explanation of at least two. (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of why the objective(s) of a business may change over time. (2â€“3 marks) Understanding of business objectives. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 5 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Define the term â€˜marginal costâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain, with examples, two other types of business costs. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only.
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) Define the term â€˜marginal costsâ€™. The cost / extra cost of producing one more unit of output (2). Or partial definitions such as â€“ Addition to total cost (1) Additional costs of production (1) Sound definition gives the full two-mark definition above (2 marks) Partial definition lists 1 of the partial factors above. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2 9609/13 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 7 of 12 Question Answer Marks

4(b) Briefly explain, with examples, two other types of business costs. Answers could include the following costs: Â• Fixed costs â€“ costs that do not vary with output in the short-term e.g. rent of premises. Â• Variable costs â€“ costs that change with output e.g. cost of materials used in making a computer. Â• Semi-variable costs â€“ these have a fixed and variable element, e.g. the basic wage of a salesperson (fixed) and commission earned (variable). Â• Direct costs â€“ costs that can clearly be identified with each unit of production and can be allocated to a cost centre, e.g. the wages of a garage mechanic. Â• Indirect costs â€“ costs that cannot be clearly identified with a unit of production and cannot be allocated directly to a cost centre â€“ e.g. the cost of cleaning a retail shop. Sound explanation of two other types of business costs, with an example of each. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one other type of business cost, with an example, or a partial explanation of two types of business cost (a cost explained without an example). (2 marks) Partial explanation of one other type of business cost or a list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/13 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 8 of 12 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Analyse the advantages of venture capital as a source of finance for a small business. [8] (b) Discuss the view that non-financial measures of business performance are just as important as financial measures of business performance. [12]
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse the advantages of venture capital as a source of finance for a small business. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the advantages of venture capital as a source of finance for a small business 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the advantages of venture capital as a source of finance for a small business 5â€“6 2 Some explanation / application of the advantages of venture capital as a source of finance for a small business 3â€“4 1 Understanding of venture capital / source of finance / small business 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers may include the following: Â• Venture capitalists are organisations or wealthy individuals who lend risk capital for start-up or expansion. Â• Small companies may find it difficult to raise capital even if they are profitable. Â• Venture capitalists supply essential financial backing for small businesses. Â• They are usually easy to locate and many available at a relatively low cost. Â• They are usually under strict supervision by regulatory bodies. Â• Angel investors operate on a smaller scale. Â• There is no repayment schedule â€“ it is an investment so there is no repayment of debt as a cost of doing business. Â• An immediate source of temporary finance unavailable elsewhere. Â• Venture capitalists can also provide important advice, guidance, and consultation â€“ can help with financial management. 8 9609/13 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 9 of 12 Question Answer Marks

5(b) Discuss the view that non-financial measures of business performance are just as important as financial measures of business performance. Level Description Marks 4 Effective evaluation of the view that non-financial measures of business performance are just as important as financial measures of business performance 9â€“12 3 Limited evaluation of the view that non-financial measures of business performance are just as important as financial measures of business performance 7â€“8 2 Analysis and some application of the importance of non- financial and/or financial measures of business performance 3â€“6 1 Understanding of non-financial / financial measures of business performance 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers may include the following: Â• Focus on quantitative financial data such as financial ratios seen as limiting and possibly misleading. Â• Financial data can be historic and manipulated. Â• Financial data gives only a partial picture of performance and misses out measures such as innovation, quality, employee relations, intangible assets â€“ the qualitative drivers of performance. Â• Non-financial factors such as customer loyalty, brand value, intellectual capital may well point to a significant part of the value of a business. Â• Financial factors take no account of such factors. Â• Non-financial measures may well be more closely linked to the long- term performance of a business while traditional financial measures may place too much emphasis on short term issues â€“ focus on strategy rather than just tactics. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the potential advantages to a community of a business with â€˜triple bottom lineâ€™ targets 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the potential advantages to a community of a business with â€˜triple bottom lineâ€™ targets 5â€“6 2 Some explanation / application of the potential advantages to a business with â€˜triple bottom lineâ€™ targets 3â€“4 1 Understanding of community / triple bottom line / business targets 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers may include the following: The â€˜triple bottom lineâ€™ approach goes beyond the traditional measures of profits, return on investment, and shareholder value to include social and environmental dimensions â€“ profits, people and the planet. A triple bottom line business may provide benefits to a community such as: Â• Economic â€“ make profits to reinvest back into the business and the local / national economy as well as to shareholders. Â• Social â€“ provide jobs or local support to disadvantaged sections of the community. Â• Environmental â€“ protect the environment and manage the business in an environmentally sustainable way. Â• These 3 benefits may be more valuable to a community than the traditional targets of a private sector profit driven business. The triple bottom line organisation (social enterprise) has the capacity to temper the extremes of a purely capitalist / profit-centred approach to business activity and offer much more socially responsible and valuable business performance. 8 9609/13 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 12 of 12 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Data Response May/June 2018 1 hour 30 minutes No Additional Materials are required. READ THESE INSTRUCTIONS FIRST An answer booklet is provided inside this question paper. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/22/M/J/18 Umpire Umbrellas (UU) UU is a private limited company that sells umbrellas with pictures it prints on them. All of the pictures on the umbrellas are printed using batch production. The umbrellas can be used to shelter from the rain or as a shade from the sun. The company has two main markets: consumer and business. UU sells individual umbrellas to consumers. These have pictures of famous people or places on them. They are mainly sold through UUâ€™s website. UU produces 20 000 of each design. The business market for UU is made up of businesses that want their logo printed on a large number of umbrellas. UU guarantees all business orders are delivered within 10 days. Recently, UU received an order from a large bank for 10 000 umbrellas with the bank logo printed on them. The bank gave an umbrella to any customer who opened a new account. The fixed cost for the order was $2000 and the variable costs were $0.75 per unit. UU made $3000 profit on the order. UUâ€™s Board of Directors has been considering two different options to grow the business. Option 1 : UU would sell franchise opportunities to entrepreneurs who can print and sell UU branded umbrellas to the consumer market in the entrepreneurâ€™s local area. These entrepreneurs would pay a fee to UU as well as 10% royalties on the profits. In return UU would provide the machinery needed to print the umbrellas. The franchisee would be required to purchase all of their inventory from UU. Option 2 : UU would increase its product portfolio by selling other goods to the business market, such as pens, USB sticks and clothing. This would require external finance to purchase the machinery needed to be able to print on these products. The market for these is growing but is very competitive. (a) (i) Define the term â€˜consumerâ€™ (line 4). [2] (ii) Briefly explain the term â€˜variable costsâ€™ (line 11). [3] (b) (i) Calculate the total revenue of the order from the large bank (lines 7â€“12). [3] (ii) Explain one reason why UU needs accurate cost data. [3] (c) Analyse two advantages to UU of using batch production. [8] (d) Recommend which of the two options for growth UU should use. Justify your answer. [11] 5 10 15 20

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Insert. DC (LK) 151772 [Turn over Cambridge International Examinations Cambridge International Advanced Subsidiary and Advanced Level BUSINESS 9609/ Short Answer and Essay October/November 2018 1 hour 15 minutes No Additional Materials are required. READ THESE INSTRUCTIONS FIRST An answer booklet is provided inside this question paper. You should follow the instructions on the front cover of the answer booklet. If you need additional answer paper ask the invigilator for a continuation booklet. Section A Answer all questions. Section B Answer one question. You are advised to spend no more than 35 minutes on Section A. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/11/O/N/18 Section A (Short Answer) Answer all questions. Define the term â€˜crowd fundingâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two advantages of crowd funding for a new business. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term â€˜crowd fundingâ€™. A source of business finance (1) for a unique project/new business/venture (1) when a business sets up a fund (1) to raise monetary contributions/donations from the public (1) usually on an on-line platform/website (1). Small contributions from (many) people / individuals (1). Each investor takes a small stake (1) by contributing towards an online fundraising target (1). Sound definition â€“ 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition â€“ 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

1(b) Briefly explain two advantages of crowd funding for a new business. Answers may include: Â• Gain capital for a business start-up. Â• Can be used to buy assets or improve cash flow. Â• Ideas that may not appeal to conventional investors can often get financed more easily. Â• Gain finance when it would not otherwise be possible (bank loan/no financial records). Â• Gain finance quickly and cheaply with no upfront fees or documents. Â• Easy to gain the finance using online websites. Â• Often no interest rate required or often lower than traditional borrowing. Â• May be a donation and money may not be paid back at all. Â• Crowd funding websites allow new business to promote their businesses to many thousands/millions of potential investors / customers. Â• This is a useful opportunity to explain the business. Â• Good way to test publicâ€™s reaction to product/idea. Â• Investors can often become loyal customers . Sound explanation of two advantages of crowd funding for a new business. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one advantage or partial explanation of two . (2 marks) Partial explanation of one advantage or a list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2018 Page 5 of 14 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜niche marketingâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two limitations of niche marketing. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜niche marketingâ€™. A focus on a small/subsection/group of a (larger) market (1) through specifically designed / specialised goods and/or services (accept example) (1) related to specific interests, which suit the customer (1). Sound definition â€“ 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition â€“ 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

2(b) Briefly explain two limitations of niche marketing. Answers may include: Â• Misses out on potential mass market customers. Â• If successful, larger firms / competitors may be attracted into the niche market. Â• Limited growth / expansion / profit opportunities as sales may be low. Â• Risk of over-dependence on a single product or market. Â• Production is expensive due to lack of economies of scale and high average costs of production. Â• Harder/more expensive to research a smaller niche market in order to determine the separate market segment. Â• Marketing / advertising / promotion expenditure may need to be high to preserve exclusivity of customer perception. Â• Higher overall costs leads to a higher price being charged. Â• Changes in consumer buying habits can have very significant effects in a small niche market, e.g. during a recession. Â• Labour tends to be specialised and expensive as no mass production possible. Â• Strong brand image needed to enter and succeed in a niche market. Sound explanation of two limitations of niche marketing. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one or partial explanation of two . (2 marks) Partial explanation of one way or a list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2018 Page 6 of 14 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain the costs and benefits to a business of holding low levels of inventory. Candidates may well use the JIT method of inventory management to answer this question. This is acceptable. Answers may include: Costs: Â• Insufficient inventory to meet demand will likely lead to lost sales and reduced revenue. Â• Loss of customer loyalty if items are not available. Â• Lack of raw materials can lead to idle production. Â• Exceptional / new orders may not be met. Â• Low levels of inventory will likely prevent bulk buy discount. Â• Needs good relationship with supplier, which may be hard to achieve. Benefits: Â• The opportunity cost of capital invested in stock is reduced. Â• More usable cash available. Â• Storage costs are reduced. Â• Reduced risk of wastage / obsolescence. Â• Easier organisation of inventory. Â• More space available. Effective explanation of the costs and benefits of holding low levels of inventory. (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of the costs and/or benefits of holding low levels of inventory. (2â€“3 marks) Understanding of inventory. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 5 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜motivationâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two ways autocratic leadership might affect the motivation of employees. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only.
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) Define the term â€˜motivationâ€™. Factors / reasons that stimulate employees / make them willing / encourage / drive them (1) to achieve a goal / objective (1) that affects the productivity of employees (1). To cause employees to be continually interested and committed to a job, role or business (1), to increase job satisfaction (1). Some candidates are looking at

4(b) and interpreting motivation in terms of a leadership skill. This is acceptable. Sound definition â€“ 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition â€“ 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2018 Page 7 of 14 Question Answer Marks

4(b) Briefly explain two ways autocratic leadership might affect the motivation of employees. Note: Autocratic leadership can affect employees positively or negatively. Answers may include: Â• An autocratic leader is likely to dominate decision-making, give instructions, not allow much participation or empowerment. Â• Autocratic decisions may be taken that employees disagree with causing bad relationships with managers. Â• Some employees will resent too much close control. Â• Allows little scope for employee discretion / creative thinking. Â• Employees not allowed to express their own ideas. Â• Employees are unable to reach their full potential. Â• Some employees will be frustrated and suffer low self-esteem and lack of sense of belonging. Â• Others, however, will welcome clear instructions and do not seek to be more involved. Â• Employees may welcome opportunity to simply obey instructions and will be motivated by a theory â€˜Xâ€™ approach. Â• Autocratic managers motivate employees by establishing confidence in the manager's ability to make accurate and productive decisions. Â• Employees become comfortable with the manager's knowledge of the industry and the company processes and gain motivation from the manager's ability to keep the department focused on achieving company goals. Sound explanation of two ways in which autocratic leadership might affect the motivation of employees. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one way or partial explanation of two .
