question_text: Analyse the qualities of a successful entrepreneur. [8] (b) Discuss why the shareholders of a public limited company might disagree with having corporate social responsibility (CSR) as a business objective. [12]
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse the qualities of a successful entrepreneur. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the qualities of a successful entrepreneur 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the qualities of a successful entrepreneur 5â€“6 2 Some explanation / application of the qualities of an entrepreneur 3â€“4 1 Understanding the role of an entrepreneur 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 An initial definition of an entrepreneur may be given as someone who sets up a business â€“ takes the risk â€“ manages the new business. Successful may be defined as establishing a sustainable / profitable business. Answers may include the following: Â• Ability to develop innovative and viable business proposals and projects. Â• Willingness to take calculated risks â€“ especially financial. Â• Persistent and determined. Â• Self-confident and able to promote the business idea. Â• Strong personality â€“ energetic and driven. Â• Self-motivated and multi-skilled. Good motivator. Â• Ability to lead and inspire employees. Â• Be persuasive and provide a vision for the future. 8 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2018 Page 9 of 14 Question Answer Marks

5(b) Discuss why the shareholders of a public limited company (PLC) might disagree with having corporate social responsibility (CSR) as a business objective. Level Description Marks 4 Effective evaluation of why the shareholders of a PLC might disagree with having corporate social responsibility (CSR) as a business objective 9â€“12 3 Limited evaluation of why the shareholders of a PLC might disagree with having corporate social responsibility (CSR) as a business objective 7â€“8 2 Analysis and some application of why the stakeholders/ shareholders/the business might disagree with having corporate social responsibility (CSR) as a business objective 3â€“6 1 Understanding of shareholders / PLC / corporate social responsibility (CSR / business objective 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers may include the following: Â• The distinction between ownership and control can lead to conflicts between shareholders and directors about the direction the business should take. Â• Shareholders may well prefer measures that aim at short term profits rather than long term growth. Â• Shareholders may well believe that the objective of the business should be to make profits not to do social good. Â• Some stakeholders may say that an awareness of the social consequences of business activity is enough â€“ there is no need to go as far as CSR. Â• The large institutional shareholders may be particularly concerned with short-term profits â€“ a recognition that there may be shareholders in a business with different motives but the large shareholders may dominate. Â• Lack of understanding by shareholders of longer-term benefit of CSR. Â• Stakeholders other than shareholders may prefer CSR as it could result in environmental preservation, better work practices/pay, higher quality/cheaper products. 7(a) Analyse the advantages and disadvantages for a new business of using an overdraft to help manage its cash flow. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the advantages and disadvantages for a new business of using an overdraft to help manage its cash flow 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the advantages and disadvantages for a new business of using an overdraft to help manage its cash flow 5â€“6 2 Some explanation/application of the advantages and/or disadvantages for a business of using an overdraft to help manage its cash flow 3â€“4 1 Understanding of overdraft / cash flow management 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers may include the following: Overdraft financing is provided when businesses make payments from their business current account exceeding the available cash balance. Cash flow management means delaying outflows of cash as long as possible while encouraging anyone who owes you money to pay it as rapidly as possible. All businesses and especially new businesses must carefully manage cash flows and there may, from time to time, be negative cash flows. The advantages of using an overdraft include: Â• Can usually be arranged quickly. Â• A very flexible short-term addition of cash. Â• Allows a business to give customers time to pay. Â• Can be planned for in advance. Â• Interest is paid only when overdrawn. Â• No need for collateral for small overdraft. However, the disadvantages include: Â• Can be very expensive â€“ high rates of interest, arrangement fee, high charges if an overdraft limit is exceeded. Â• May be a disincentive for arranging trade credit agreements with suppliers. Â• May be a disincentive to recoup money owning. Â• Very much a short-term solution â€“ unlikely to contribute to a long-term healthy cash flow position. Â• The amount loaned is repayable on demand by the bank. Overdraft in the short-term may be the only option for a new business. 8 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2018 Page 13 of 14 Question Answer Marks

Â• A business needs to maintain liquidity and that requires effective cash flow management. Â• Employees, vendors, suppliers, rent, and bills are paid with cash â€“ so without it a business is unable to survive. Â• Cash is a vital short-term requirement for all businesses â€“ profit needs to be secured but it is a longer-te

Answers could also include: Â• The suggestion is that the two models can be combined â€“ Product and Customer, Price and Cost, Promotion and Communication, and Place and Convenience. Â• Distinction between product /price/promotion/place and customer solution /cost to customer /communication with customer /convenience to customer. 1(b) Briefly explain one advantage and one disadvantage to a business of operating as a public limited company rather than as a private limited company. Advantages: Â• Easier to raise capital e.g. from existing/new investors. Â• To give company a more prestigious profile â€“ improved reputation. Â• Greater creditworthiness e.g. easier to obtain loans. Â• The opportunity to more easily make acquisitions e.g. by offering shares to the shareholders of the target firm. Â• plc status may reduce expenditure on marketing e.g. greater discounts. Disadvantages: Â• Short-term pressure for profit maximisation/share price may mean compromising overall business aims. Â• More media exposure â€“ potentially more accountability. Â• Greater transparency â€“ accounts must be audited/fuller information on performance provided. Â• No control over who buys shares/less control over business â€“ vulnerable to a takeover/a majority shareholder having a greater say how the business is run. Â• Amount of finance to set up plc is higher. Â• More documentation required to set up. Sound explanation of one advantage AND one disadvantage of operating as a plc. Â• It is important for a business to know when to launch a new product or update an existing one. Â• A balanced product portfolio is one in which the range of products is one that maximises efficiency in the allocation of resources. Â• The product life cycle (concept) is a mechanism for assisting with product portfolio analysis. Â• The product life cycle is the pattern of sales recorded by a product from launch to withdrawal from the market. Â• It is an analytical tool that examines product sales over a series of stages: introduction, growth, maturity, decline, extension. Â• This analysis assists in reviewing the product portfolio and suggests when changes may be needed. Â• Examples include: market share â€“ brand identity â€“ launch new products â€“ new advertising campaign. Â• They need to be clearly communicated to all departments in the organisation. Â• If clear, they can be progress monitored. Finance Â• Marketing will have to work within the budget agreed at corporate level and specified by finance â€“ e.g. the amount of money allocated to marketing to market a product â€“ and finance will use the sales forecasts of marketing to devise cash flow forecasts. question_text: Insert. DC (LK) 168733/1 [Turn over Cambridge Assessment International Education Cambridge International Advanced Subsidiary and Advanced Level * 7 7 5 7 5 0 7 8 2 7 * BUSINESS 9609/ Short Answer and Essay May/June 2019 1 hour 15 minutes No Additional Materials are required. READ THESE INSTRUCTIONS FIRST An answer booklet is provided inside this question paper. You should follow the instructions on the front cover of the answer booklet. If you need additional answer paper ask the invigilator for a continuation booklet. Section A Answer all questions. Section B Answer one question. You are advised to spend no more than 35 minutes on Section A. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/11/M/J/19 Section A (Short Answer) Answer all questions. Define the term â€˜competitive pricing strategyâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain why a business might use a price skimming strategy. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term â€˜competitive pricing strategyâ€™. A pricing strategy / method of pricing where a price for a product or service is set / made (1) Based upon the price set by competitors (1). A business offers to match the price of competitors (1). Set a price slightly below competitors (1). An example Mcdonalds v Burger King (1). Sound definition 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

1(b) Briefly explain why a business might use a price skimming strategy. Answers could include: Â• Price skimming is the setting of a high price for a product that is considered to be unique or having special features and thus has a low price elasticity of demand (inelastic). Â• May be a new product â€“ aim is to maximise short-term profits before competitors enter the market. Â• Create customer perception of high quality. Â• Customers can equate quality with high price. Â• Producers of luxury goods/designer goods focus on status conscious customers who will pay high prices. Â• Recoup high costs involved in production (e.g. pharmaceutical companies). Â• To maximise profit. Â• To exploit a product with a low elasticity of demand. Sound explanation of the advantages of a business using a price skimming strategy. (3 marks) Partial / limited explanation of the advantages of a business using a price skimming strategy. (2 marks) Explanation / reference to a price skimming strategy. (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2019 Page 5 of 14 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜retained earningsâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain the distinction between short-term and long-term sources of business finance. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜retained earningsâ€™. The profits made by a business that are not distributed to shareholders (1). Profits left after all deductions have been made / dividends have been paid (1). Profits re-invested in the business (1). Profits kept as a reserve (1). A source of internal finance (1). Sound definition 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

2(b) Briefly explain the distinction between short-term and long-term sources of business finance. Answers could include: Short-term financing . Â• Usually refers to financing that spans a period of less than a year but could extend according to the types of loan/debt being considered. Â• Loan requiring a short repayment period, probably at a lower interest. Â• Risk is lower so more accessible to smaller businesses. Â• Include short-term loans, overdrafts, short-term leases, accounts payable. Â• Used for day to day financial requirements / to cover any shortfall between income and expenditure. Long-term financing Â• Spans a longer period of time, from over 1 year â€“ 30 years. Â• Long-term financing is riskier, so banks often require collateral to prevent default on repayment. Â• Includes share issue, bonds, long-term bank loans, long-term leases, retained earnings, mortgages. Â• Restricted to large businesses, established businesses that can supply collateral. Â• Used for longer term/larger investment projects. Answers may make reference to features of short-term and long-term sources of business finance such as those listed above. Accurate references to these features are acceptable as explanations of the distinction between these two sources of business finance. Sound explanation of the features of short-term and long-term sources of business finance. (3 marks) Partial / limited of the features of short-term and long-term sources of business finance. (2 marks) Explanation / reference to sources of business finance. (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2019 Page 6 of 14 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain factors that could influence the scale of operations of a business. Answers could include: Â• The size of the operations of a business from small / limited to large / extensive. Â• The objectives of the owners of a business â€“ there may be reluctance to expand a small business. Â• Capital available may be limited â€“ growth will be restrained. Â• The size of the market that a business operates in may be relatively small â€“ or a deliberate choice for a niche market. Â• The extent of competition â€“ intensive competition may limit scale of operations. Â• Possibility / scope for economies of scale â€“ may offer opportunities for expansion. Â• State of the external economy may affect scale. Â• Legal restrictions may affect opportunities to expand. Â• Size of labour resource available. Â• Nature of product â€“ less standardised products such as handicraft

question_text: Define the term â€˜private limited companyâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two possible disadvantages to a sole trader of changing to a private limited company. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only.
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) Define the term â€˜private limited companyâ€™. A business owned by private shareholders (1). A small to medium-sized company (1). Owned by shareholders (1). Shareholders have limited liability (1). Shareholders are often members of the same family (1). The business is a separate legal entity (1). Cannot sell shares to general public / stock exchange (1). Sound definition 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

4(b) Briefly explain two possible disadvantages to a sole trader of changing to a private limited company. Answers could include: Â• Loss of 100% ownership of the business. Â• Loss of control of the business (might not be a majority shareholder). Â• Cost of changing to a private limited company. Â• May lose influence over key decisions such as distribution of profit, future growth policies. Â• Needs to produce end of year accounts â€“ more transparency of financial affairs. Â• Information disclosure could make a business competitively disadvantaged. Sound explanation of two disadvantages to a sole trader of changing to a private limited company. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one disadvantage or partial explanation of two disadvantages to a sole trader of changing to a private limited company. (2 marks) Partial explanation of one disadvantage or a list of two disadvantages to a sole trader of changing to a private limited company. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2019 Page 8 of 14 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Analyse the difference between autocratic and laissez-faire leadership. [8] (b) â€˜A democratic style of leadership is the most effective leadership style for a manufacturing business in a very competitive industry.â€™ Discuss this view. [12]
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse the difference between autocratic and laissez-faire leadership. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the difference between autocratic and laissez-faire leadership. 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the difference between autocratic and laissez-faire leadership. 5â€“6 2 Some application of the difference between autocratic and laissez-faire leadership. 3â€“4 1 Some understanding of autocratic/laissez-faire leadership. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers could include: Â• An autocratic leader has complete command over employees. Â• Leader takes all major decisions. Â• Views / ideas / suggestions are discouraged. Â• Criticism not allowed. Â• Can lead to speedy decision making and high productivity. Â• Can lead to poor employee motivation and high labour turnover. Â• Works best with unskilled, monotonous, routine work where leader is best performer. Â• A laissez-faire leader is one who trusts employees / team to perform the job themselves. Â• Armâ€™s length management. Â• Views are shared and suggestions welcomed. Â• Works best when employees are highly skilled, loyal, and experienced. Â• Works best with employees who are subject area specialists â€“ capable of accepting responsibility. Â• Should not be used to mask managerial incompetence. 8 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2019 Page 9 of 14 Question Answer Marks

5(b) â€˜A democratic style of leadership is the most effective leadership style for a manufacturing business in a very competitive industryâ€™. Discuss this view. 7(a) Analyse the advantages and disadvantages for a retail business of using Just in Time (JIT) to manage its inventory. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the advantages and disadvantages for a retail business of using JIT to manage its inventory. 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the advantages and disadvantages for a retail business of using JIT to manage its inventory. 5â€“6 2 Some application of the advantages and disadvantages for a retail business of using JIT to manage its inventory. 3â€“4 1 Some understanding of JIT / retail business. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers could include: Â• Definition of JIT â€“ avoid holding high levels of inventory to require supplies to arrive just as they are needed. Â• Advantages and disadvantages of JIT set out and analysed in the context of retail â€“ great variety of products â€“ need to consistently meet customer demand â€“ limited space for holding inventory â€“ products may be fragile, perishable. Â• Advantages â€“ capital investment in stock reduced as is opportunity cost of stockholding â€“ cost of storage reduced â€“ more space to display products â€“ danger of out-of-date stock reduced â€“ reduced risk of wastage â€“ quicker response to customer demand. Â• Disadvantages â€“ failure of supply delivery could mean empty shelves, product shortage â€“ delivery costs (frequent) may be high â€“ may not be able to benefit from bulk buying (depends on the size of the retail business) â€“ very dependent on quality of supplier (it may, of course, be its own delivery service in a large retail business). It is often difficult for retailers to accurately calculate customer demand. 8 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2019 Page 13 of 14 Question Answer Marks

7(b) Discuss the importance of flexibility and innovation to a multi-national furniture manufacturer. Â• Flexibility might be achieved by having a flexible and adaptable workforce, flexible flow-line production equipment, increase capacity by purchasing more equipment / logistics / supply chain management. Â• Innovation is the use of new or improved processes e.g. production / design / R&D / work practices / systems. Â• Innovation might be achieved by developing new products or new production processes / CAD and CAM / training and restructuring of the workforce. 1(a) Define the term â€˜supplyâ€™. The quantity of a product / service that businesses are prepared to supply / produce (1). 1(b) Briefly explain two non-price factors which could increase the demand for a product or service. Answers could include: Â• Increase in consumer income. Â• There is limited supply. Sound explanation of two non-price factors which could increase the demand for a product or service. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜business leadershipâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two styles of business leadership. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜business leadershipâ€™. Motivating / inspiring staff (1). To achieve objectives / goals / aims (1). Directing an organisation (1). Having a vision for a business / organisation (1). Having a strategy for a business (1). Â• Definitions that simply refer to management functions such as control, coordination, resource allocation should not be awarded marks. Â• If, however, such an answer includes the activity motivation then this should be regarded as a partial answer and be awarded 1 mark. Â• Listing types of leadership should not be awarded marks. Sound definition â€“ 2 of the factors listed above. (2 marks) Partial definition â€“ 1 of the factors listed above. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

2(b) Briefly explain two styles of business leadership. Autocratic / authoritarian: all decisions taken at the top of the organisation, or by the leader. Democratic: employees encouraged to participate in decisions. Laissez-faire: â€˜ hands offâ€™ approach, opposite of autocratic, most decisions left to employees. Paternalistic: leader may listen and consult but will take decisions as â€˜they know bestâ€™. Â• A sound answer may be one that (a) defines a style of leadership as above and then (b) briefly explains some aspect of that style such as impact on employees, impact on quality of decisions, speed of decisions, resulting empowerment, increased delegation. Such an answer should be awarded 2 marks (for each style). Â• A partial answer is one that perhaps gives a single definitional sentence with no additional explanation. Such an answer should be awarded 1 mark (for each style). Sound explanation of two styles of business leadership. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one style of business leadership or partial explanation of two . (2 marks) Partial explanation of one style of business leadership or a list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content. Â• Different stakeholders in a typical business â€“ employees â€“ management â€“ shareholders â€“ customers â€“ suppliers â€“ local economy â€“ local / national government â€“ pressure groups. Â• Business decisions of interest to stakeholders include: profit distribution â€“ expansion â€“ diversification â€“ supplier policy â€“ degree of social responsibility â€“ operating methods â€“ remuneration and motivating policies. Â• Different stakeholders will have views on such business decisions that may lead to conflict among the stakeholders or between some stakeholders, e.g. shareholders vs. employees with regard to profit distribution â€“ so decisions could be changed or overruled or supported with or without conflict. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜job productionâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain why a business might choose flow production as a method of operation. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) Define the term â€˜job productionâ€™. Production of a one-off / unique product or service (1) specially designed by / for a customer (1). One product made at a time (1). Labour intensive production process (1). Requiring a highly skilled work force (1). A method of production (1). A relevant example such as a piece of jewellery (1). Sound definition â€“ 2 of the factors listed above. (2 marks) Partial definition â€“ 1 of the factors listed above. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

4(b) Briefly explain why a business might choose flow production as a method of operation. Answers could include: Â• Producing items in a continually moving process might be the best production for the business, e.g. in production of identical standardised cars on an assembly line. Â• Can make use of high technology methods â€“ robot arms. Â• Can mass produce items to a consistent standard. Â• Offers economies of scale and low costs for a one-size-fits-all product â€“ possible price reductions and increased sales can result. Â• Can produce different content products on the same production line (e.g. Coke, Sprite). Â• The market demands high volume, capital is available, unskilled workforce available might suggest a choice of flow production. Sound explanation of the advantages of flow production which cause a business to choose this method of operation. (3 marks) Partial / limited explanation of the advantages of flow production which cause a business to choose this method of operation. (2 marks) Explanation / reference to flow production. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/12 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2019 Page 8 of 12 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Analyse the advantages of crowd funding as a source of finance for small businesses. [8] (b) â€˜Financial measures are the most important indicator of business performance.â€™ Discuss this view. [12]
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse the advantages of crowd funding as a source of finance for small businesses. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the advantages of crowd funding as a source of finance for small businesses. 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the advantages of crowd funding as a source of finance for small businesses. 5â€“6 2 Some application of the advantages of crowd funding as a source of finance for small businesses. 3â€“4 1 Limited understanding of crowd funding / source of finance / small businesses. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers could include: Â• Small businesses typically have significant problems in raising funds. Â• The practice of funding a project / venture by raising monetary contributions from a large number of people, typically via the internet. Â• Access to cheap(er) money for small and medium sized businesses. Â• May not have to pay interest or be repaid. (However, in some instances crowd funding may have interest and repayment.) Â• No need to sell an equity stake in the business. Â• Fast and flexible source of finance. Â• Allows many more speculative projects to be funded. Â• Supports business expansion. Â• Acts as a marketing / brand tool. Â• May give feedback and advice to project fund seeker. Â• Entrepreneurs do not have to risk own money for a new venture. 8 9609/12 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2019 Page 9 of 12 Question Answer Marks

5(b) â€˜Financial measures are the most important indicators of business performance.â€™ Discuss this view. Level Description Marks 4 Effective evaluation of the view that financial measures are the most important indicators of business performance. 9â€“12 3 Limited evaluation of the view that financial measures are the most important indicators of business performance. 7â€“8 2 Analysis and some application of the view that financial or non-financial measures are important indicators of business performance. 3â€“6 1 Understanding of financial / non-financial measures / business performance. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Answers could include: Â• In recent years, there have been moves towards supplementing financial measures of performance such as financial results data and ratio analysis. Nevertheless, quantitative measures of business performance are important and strong answers may well outline the value of financial measures before discussing the case for including the qualitative performance measures. Â• Financial measures could include the following: profit, cash flow, break even, liquidity (current ratio, acid test ratio), profitability (gross profit margin, net profit margin and return on capital employed). Reference may also be made to income statements and statements of financial position. Â• Recognition of the limitations of financial data and often too little emphasis on the qualitative drivers of performance, such as innovation, quality, employee relations, intangible assets. 2(b) Briefly explain two ways that a manufacturing business might improve the efficiency of its operations. Answers could include: Â• Concern to produce output at the highest ratio of output to input. Â• The business might be capital or labour intensive or a combination of both â€“ will offer different opportunities. Â• Improve / increase employee training / recruitment â€“ more skills. Â• Improve staff motivation / morale. Â• Job rotation to reduce monotony. Â• Invest in more advanced equipment â€“ e.g. robotics. Â• Change the actual system of production â€“ e.g. to flow production. Â• Decrease waste. Â• More efficient management / supervision. Â• Better communication channels. Â• More appropriate inventory management. Sound explanation of two ways that a manufacturing business might improve the efficiency of its operations. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one way or partial explanation of two ways that a manufacturing business might improve the efficiency of its operations. (2 marks) Partial explanation of one way or a list of two ways that a manufacturing business might improve the efficiency of its operations. (1 mark) No creditable content. Answers could include: Â• Important decisions are made in a business such as: â€“ what suppliers to use â€“ what price to set â€“ how much to invest â€“ when to expand etc. Â• Such decisions cannot realistically be made without accurate cost information. Â• Cost information informs the business decision-making process. Â• Cost information facilitates control / monitoring / benchmark comparisons. Â• All resource allocation decisions are dependent on cost information. Â• Vital for setting budgets and targets. Â• Cost information is at the heart of all important business decisions. Â• Credit examples of specific decisions. Â• Compare costs with competitor costs. Â• Know when the break-even point is reached. Effective explanation of why cost information is important for business decision-making. (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of why cost information is important to a business. question_text: Data Response May/June 2019 1 hour 30 minutes No Additional Materials are required. READ THESE INSTRUCTIONS FIRST An answer booklet is provided inside this question paper. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/23/M/J/19 Fuzz Buzz Ltd (FBL) FBL is a small private limited company with 10 shareholders. Its unique selling point is that its toys are handmade. They are aimed at children aged 4 to 10. Its latest product, a hand-held soft spinning toy, called Tommy Turner, has become a fashion item amongst young children. Demand for these toys exceeds the ability of FBL to supply them. Stores are desperate to secure more inventory in order to make the most of the high demand. FBL sells these toys for $6 each. It is considering how to respond to the excess demand. If FBL increases the price of the Tommy Turner then the price elasticity of demand is estimated to be â€“2. The quantity demanded at the current price is 120 000. If FBL increases the supply of Tommy Turners it would require investment. The new machinery would cost $100 000 and allow mass customisation. The concern is that this fashion may not last. The work will be a lot less skilled, although a design team will still be required to develop future toys. FBLâ€™s employees take great pride in their work and are very loyal to the company and their co-workers. Most of the employees have been with the company for over 10 years and enjoy good relationships with all the directors. Anindita, the Marketing Director, thinks FBL should make the most of the fashion for the Tommy Turner by extending the product life cycle. (a) (i) Define the term â€˜unique selling pointâ€™ (line 1). [2] (ii) Explain the term â€˜mass customisationâ€™ (line 10). [3] (b) (i) Calculate the new quantity demanded of the Tommy Turner if FBL increases the price by 10%. [4] (ii) Explain one factor influencing the supply of the Tommy Turner. [2] (c) Analyse two methods FBL could use to extend the product life cycle of the Tommy Turner. [8] (d) Discuss the likely impact on the morale and welfare of FBLâ€™s employees if supply of the Tommy Turner is increased. [11] 5 10 15

DC (JM) 169865/1 [Turn over Cambridge Assessment International Education Cambridge International Advanced Subsidiary and Advanced Level * 8 3 8 0 7 9 2 7 5 3 * BUSINESS 9609/ Short Answer and Essay October/November 2019 1 hour 15 minutes No Additional Materials are required. READ THESE INSTRUCTIONS FIRST An answer booklet is provided inside this question paper. You should follow the instructions on the front cover of the answer booklet. If you need additional answer paper ask the invigilator for a continuation booklet. Section A Answer all questions. Section B Answer one question. You are advised to spend no more than 35 minutes on Section A. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/11/O/N/19 Section A (Short Answer) Answer all questions. Define the term â€˜price elasticity of demandâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two reasons why price elasticity of demand might be useful to a business when making pricing decisions. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term â€˜price elasticity of demandâ€™. A measure of the responsiveness of demand of a product (1). Change in demand following a change in its price (1). Formula (2) % change in quantity demanded % change in price Accept formula without % Sound definition 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

1(b) Briefly explain two reasons why price elasticity of demand might be useful to a business when making pricing decisions. Answers could include: Â• Allows a business to decide whether to change price, if PED is known. Â• If the demand for a product/service is known to be price elastic, then a business can lower the price and anticipate significant extra demand and sales revenue. Â• If the demand is known to be price inelastic then the price of a product/service can be increased with more sales revenue generated, even though demand will fall. Â• It allows a business to practice price discrimination if it knows different segments of its market have different PEDs. Â• Can choose pricing strategies e.g. skimming, competition, penetration. Accept any other valid response. Sound explanation of two reasons why price elasticity of demand might be useful to a business when making pricing decisions. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one reason or partial explanation of two reasons why price elasticity of demand might be useful to a business when making pricing decisions. (2 marks) Partial explanation of one reason why price elasticity of demand might be useful to a business when making pricing decisions, or a list of two reasons. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2019 Page 5 of 15 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜cash flow forecastâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two limitations for a business of using a cash flow forecast. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜cash flow forecastâ€™. The cash inflows and outflows of a business/money moving into and out of a business (1). Anticipated/estimated/future/predicted (1). Sound definition 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

2(b) Briefly explain two limitations for a business of using a cash flow forecast. Answers may include: Â• Cash flow based on out of date information. Â• It is only an estimate/prediction so less chance of accuracy. Â• Internal and external factors can invalidate a cash flow forecast. Â• The forecasts themselves may have been inexpertly drawn up. Â• Inflows and outflows may be under-estimated or over-estimated e.g. unexpected costs may arise. Â• Revenue inflows can fluctuate significantly with demand. Â• Expense outflows can fluctuate with supplier price changes. Â• Incorrect assumptions can be made e.g. based on poor market research Â• Incorrect assumptions lead to inaccurate cash flow estimates. Â• Not done correctly leads to wrong decisions. Â• Ignores other methods of financial measurements. Â• There may be window dressing in order to inflate the cash flow forecast. Accept any other valid response . Sound explanation of two limitations for a business of using a cash flow forecast. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one limitation or partial explanation of two limitations for a business of using a cash flow forecast. (2 marks) Partial explanation of one limitation for a business of using a cash flow forecast, or a list of two limitations. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2019 Page 6 of 15 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain how human resource management (HRM) helps to achieve business success. Answers may include: Â• HRM is a major function of most business organisations. Â• Concerned with supporting all other functions of a business organisation through effective activities such as: - Recruitment and selection of appropriate employees - Training and developing staff - Workforce planning for the future - Redundancy of unnecessary employees - Dismissal of ineffective/disruptive employees - Advice and support on issues such as work-life balance, diversity, discrimination - Negotiation with trade unions - Policies on motivation and skill development. Â• HRM is specifically responsible for achieving business success with issues such as the reduction of labour turnover. Accept any other valid response . Effective explanation with specific example(s) of ways that HRM helps to achieve business success. (4â€“5 marks) Explanation of how HRM helps to achieve business success. (2â€“3 marks) Descriptive information about HRM/business success. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 5 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2019 Page 7 of 15 Question Answer Marks

4(b) Briefly explain two ways ethics might influence the activities of a business. Answers could include: Â• Not exploiting consumers â€“ high prices and/or low-quality goods/service. Â• Not exploiting low cost foreign labour. Â• Not taking advantage of child/slave labour. Â• Not expecting excessive hours/paying low wages. Â• Not using production methods that are potentially damaging to the health of employees. Â• Not using production methods that lead to environmental damage or suffering to animals/local residents. Â• Not exploiting suppliers with poor/delayed payments. Â• Using sustainable/Fairtrade products/production. Â• Not purchasing cheap, unethical raw materials. Â• Explanation of the disadvantages to the business of the above e.g. increasing costs/decreased sales/competitiveness. Â• Comment on short term versus long term effects. Accept any other valid response . Sound explanation of two ways in which ethics might influence the activities of a business. question_text: Analyse why making a profit is not the main objective of all private sector businesses. [8] (b) Discuss why national water supply businesses in many countries operate in the public sector. [12]
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse why making a profit is not the main objective of all private sector businesses. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of why making a profit is not the main objective of all private sector businesses. 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of why making a profit is not the main objective of all private sector businesses. 5â€“6 2 Some application of why making a profit is not the main objective of all private sector businesses. 3â€“4 1 Knowledge and understanding of profit/business objectives/private sector businesses. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers may include: Knowledge and Understanding Â• Clear understanding of profit. Â• Clear understanding of business objectives. Â• Clear understanding of private sector businesses. Application Â• Reference to the different objectives a private sector business may have. Analysis SPECIAL NOTE â€“ some candidates may choose to focus exclusively on one particular type of private sector business such as a social enterprise or a charity to answer this question. This is a valid approach which can access all levels of the mark scheme. Â• There is a range of objectives that private sector businesses may seek to achieve including - Profit - Profit maximisation - Growth - Market share - Survival - Global reach - Social good, etc. Â• It may well depend on the context, size and maturity of a private sector business as to which objective is a primary objective at any particular time. Â• A private sector business may focus on survival or increasing market share in the short-term, with profit as a long-term objective. Â• Cash is often more important than profit in the short-term. 8 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2019 Page 9 of 15 Question Answer Marks

mark_scheme_text:
7(a) Analyse the benefits of a labour intensive production process for a business. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the benefits of a labour intensive production process for a business. 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the benefits of a labour intensive production process for a business. 5â€“6 2 Some application of the benefits of a labour intensive production process for a business. 3â€“4 1 Knowledge and understanding of labour intensive/production processes. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers may include: Knowledge and Understanding Â• Clear understanding of a labour intensive/production process. Application Â• The benefits of a labour intensive production process for a business. . Analysis Â• More labour may afford the opportunity to give a more unique product in production processes e.g. hand-crafted furniture. Â• More labour may afford the opportunity to give a more personal service when dealing with the customer face-to-face. Â• Labour intensive production is more appropriate and necessary for some businesses e.g. hotels and restaurants due to the customer service nature of the business. Â• In certain situations, it is less expensive to use labour than expensive machines especially in countries where labour is inexpensive. Â• In some businesses, machines are not appropriate for the work being carried out, perhaps it involves a complex set of actions or decisions. Â• Capital investment can be expensive and finance may not be available. Â• There is less of a fixed cost associated with many types of labour and, particularly in recession times, labour is more flexible and costs can be cut by â€˜laying offâ€™ staff. Â• Businesses might get government grants to support new jobs, encouraging labour intensive approaches. Â• However, there may be limitations in using a labour intensive production process. Â• Repetitive and boring jobs are often done better using capital. Â• Some dangerous or heavy lifting jobs are better done using capital. Accept any other valid response . 8 9609/11 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2019 Page 14 of 15 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Insert. DC (NH) 169864/1 [Turn over Cambridge Assessment International Education Cambridge International Advanced Subsidiary and Advanced Level * 8 8 1 8 4 6 3 5 8 5 * BUSINESS 9609/ Short Answer and Essay October/November 2019 1 hour 15 minutes No Additional Materials are required. You are advised to spend no more than 35 minutes on Section A. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/12/O/N/19 Section A (Short Answer) Answer all questions. Define the term â€˜random samplingâ€™. [2] (b) Briefly explain two benefits to a business of using quota sampling as a method of collecting data. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term â€˜random samplingâ€™. Random sampling is a sampling method/technique/process (1) in which all members of a group or population (1) have an equal chance of being selected (1) Sound definition 2 of the factors listed above [2 marks] Partial definition 1 of the factors listed above [1 mark] No creditable content. [0 marks] 2

1(b) Briefly explain two benefits to a business of using quota sampling as a method of collecting data. Answers may include: Quota sampling is when the population is stratified and an interviewer selects a specified number of respondents from each stratum. The benefits of quota sampling: Â• It ensures that the sample is chosen to be representative of all the strata in the population. Â• Allows more accurate data to be collected as sample is targeted. Â• It is quicker to collect information from a quota sample as fewer individuals need to be contacted, therefore results can be processed more rapidly to give the company information to act on. Â• It is easier and more practical to collect information from a small number of individuals rather than a larger random sample, especially when the whole population is large and spread over wide geographic region, thereby keeping down costs and speeding up research. Â• It is cheaper to collect data from a smaller number of people as fewer researchers are needed, therefore the cost of wages is reduced. Â• It allows more detailed information to be collected and analysed in the time allowed because the quality of interviewing can be better as there are fewer people to question. Â• Accept any other valid response. Sound explanation of two benefits to a business of using quota sampling as a method of collecting data. [3 marks] Sound explanation of one benefit or partial explanation of two benefits of using quota sampling as a method of collecting data. [2 marks] Partial explanation of one benefit of using quota sampling as a method of collecting data, or a list of two benefits, or a limited explanation of quota sampling. [1 mark] No creditable content. [0 marks] 3 9609/12 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2019 Page 5 of 15 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Analyse the advantages to an entrepreneur of purchasing a franchise to start a business. [8] (b) Discuss factors that could lead to the failure of a small business which repairs cars. [12]
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse the advantages to an entrepreneur of purchasing a franchise to start a business. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the advantages to an entrepreneur of purchasing a franchise to start a business. 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the advantages to an entrepreneur of purchasing a franchise to start a business. 5â€“6 2 Some application of the advantages to an entrepreneur of purchasing a franchise to start a business. 3â€“4 1 Knowledge and understanding of a franchise/entrepreneur. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers may include: Knowledge and Understanding Â• Clear understanding of a franchise. Â• Clear understanding of an entrepreneur. Application Â• Reference to the advantages to an entrepreneur of purchasing a franchise or starting a business. Analysis Â• Lower risk of a new business failing â€“ as an established brand and product is used. Â• The franchisor normally provides training and business advice. Â• Access to national/international marketing may be available. Â• Supplies are normally acquired from quality-checked suppliers. Â• Sales area is usually ring-fenced, providing some degree of monopoly power. Â• These advantages need to be paid for and often incur considerable costs. Â• Accept any other valid response. 8 9609/12 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2019 Page 9 of 15 Question Answer Marks

5(b) Discuss factors that could lead to the failure of a small business which repairs cars. Level Description Marks 4 Effective evaluation of factors that could lead to the failure in the context of a small business which repairs cars . 9â€“12 3 Limited evaluation of factors that could lead to the failure in the context of a small business which repairs cars . 7â€“8 2 Application and analysis of factors that could lead to the failure of a small business. 3â€“6 1 Knowledge and understanding of a small business. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers could include: Knowledge and understanding Â• Understanding of issues that relate to a small business. Application Â• Reference to the factors that might explain why small businesses fail. Analysis Â• May run into cash flow problems as customer payments not made/or slow. Â• Quality of work may reduce if the business becomes too busy or a skilled member of staff leaves or has an accident â€“ so customers leave. Â• The business may grow too quickly resulting in pressure on cash flow. Â• Poor management of the business. Â• Fewer opportunities for economies of scale in a small business. Â• Reference to the particular issues that face small car repairers. Â• Issues such as the capital requirement for machines and tools/equipment to repair cars. Â• Issues such as the requirement for skilled mechanics and appropriate premises. Â• Suppliers may increase costs of car parts/oil/tyres. Â• Larger and more efficient car repair businesses may take trade away from a small business â€“ with special offers/more up-to-date machinery. Â• May lose an important contract to repair cars for other businesses

7(a) Analyse how break-even analysis might be used by a business when making a decision to introduce a new product. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of how break-even analysis might be used by a business when making a decision to introduce a new product. 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of how break-even analysis might be used by a business. 5â€“6 2 Some application of how break-even analysis might be used by a business. 3â€“4 1 Knowledge and understanding of break-even analysis/introducing a new product. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers could include: Knowledge and understanding Â• A clear understanding of break-even analysis / point is given or implied. Â• A clear understanding of introducing a new product. Application Â• The use of break-even analysis by a business. Analysis Â• A technique widely used by production management or management accountants. Â• Total variable and fixed costs are compared with sales revenue to determine the level of sales or production where the business makes neither a profit or loss. Â• The technique can be used to determine the point at which the sales volume reaches a pre-set profit level. Â• The technique can be used to help increase the odds of success for a new product. Â• The technique can be used to answer business questions, such as: - What is the potential size of the market? - How should the product be priced? - Where is the break-even point? Â• Can provide vital management information, such as is it worth going ahead with this new product? Â• Break-even charts are relatively easy to construct and interpret. Â• However, there are limitations as to the use of break-even analysis, as: - it is a simplistic, static model. Â• Accept any other valid response. 8 9609/12 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2019 Page 14 of 15 Question Answer Marks

This is a very competitive market. The business sells all of its products online. Customers use the FF website to choose the flowers they like and how they want them arranged. Once a customer has placed an order, a skilled employee will use job production to produce the flower arrangement. Employees are paid a commission based on the final selling price of the arrangements which they produce. The finished product is then delivered to any address within 50 kilometres of the FF premises. Most customers receive their order within 24 hours of placing it, apart from during busy times of the year. The directors of FF are concerned that the current product has reached maturity in the product life cycle. A suggestion has been made to produce low-price flower arrangements. There would be 10 different arrangements which would be made using batch production. These flower arrangements would be sold through local retailers. The Finance Director has suggested that the new venture may decrease FFâ€™s working capital, which is currently $18 000. The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 9609/22/O/N/19 Candy Planet (CP) CP is a public limited company which sells candy (sweets) to its national market and international markets. CP has benefitted from internal growth over the past eight years and has a good working capital position. The total market sizes are shown in Table 2.1. Table 2.1: Market data for candy 2017 2018 National market $550m $550m International market $60bn $61.5bn CP is a capital intensive business but it does employ 40 workers to operate and maintain the machines. These employees have part-time contracts. The candy is made using flow production. Each variety of candy is made on a separate production line. Some of the machinery is over 25 years old. CP has 20 separate production lines in its factory and there are diseconomies of scale. The Operations Director of CP has proposed that the company changes from having many production lines to a single production line using process innovation. The business has carried out research into new machinery that would allow for mass customisation and an increase in the number of products produced each month. This would mean CP could have just one production line which would make every type of candy which CP sells. CP could also increase its product portfolio. The new machinery would cost $75m and its installation would require CP to stop production for six months. Most of the current workforce would face redundancy. The Finance Director has concerns about how CP could finance this new machinery. CP does not have any retained earnings which it could use and the business has already been told that a bank loan is not possible. (a) (i) Define the term â€˜internal growthâ€™ (line 2). [2] (ii) Explain the term â€˜capital intensiveâ€™ (line 9). [3] (b) (i) Refer to Table 2.1. Calculate the percentage market growth in the international market for candy. [2] (ii) Explain two ways in which CPâ€™s marketing may differ between its national market and international markets. question_text: Insert. DC (JM) 169860/3 [Turn over Cambridge Assessment International Education Cambridge International Advanced Subsidiary and Advanced Level * 4 4 3 6 8 7 3 2 3 7 * BUSINESS 9609/ Better Bicycles Ltd (BB) BB is a family-owned small business. The firm manufactures bicycles which are supplied to independent retailers. Recently, Lia, the Sales Director, has reported a 20% fall in monthly sales. She has asked the other directors for ideas to increase revenue. The Managing Director, Moneeb, has suggested that BB should open its own retail shop. Moneeb believes that BB has poor inventory management. BB currently uses buffer inventory control methods. There has been a build-up of inventory that is using warehouse space that could be converted to a shop at a cost of $180 000. He estimates additional yearly costs of operating the shop of $40 000. Moneeb has provided the following information in Table 1.1. Table 1.1: Financial data for selling bicycles through BBâ€™s proposed shop Unit price Cost of sales per unit Forecast additional annual sales $300 $120 1250 units John, the Operations Manager, thinks it would be a mistake to open a shop as they have no experience in the retail sector. He thinks that sales could be increased through the existing channel of distribution. John believes that the salaried staff in the sales department are not motivated to find new independent bicycle retailers, as they rely on customers who have bought from BB for many years. Lia thinks that BB should produce a new design of foldable bicycle which would be lightweight and portable. Secondary research shows that this is a growth market with customers prepared to pay a premium price for a well-designed foldable bicycle. Lia estimates that an investment of $225 000 would be required but the gross profit margin is forecast at 50% with additional annual revenue of $500 000. Moneeb and Lia have been asked to present their proposals for increasing BBâ€™s revenue to the other directors, who will then make a decision. Total contribution = contribution per unit â‹… extra output for special order OR Contribution per unit = selling price â€“ variable cost per unit [1 if no relevant calculation] Variable unit cost = 4 + 1 + 1.50 = 6.50 [1] Contribution per unit = $7 â€“ 6.50 = $0.50 [2] Total contribution = $0.50 â‹… 10 000 = $5000 [4] If fixed costs included then â€“$0.50 â‹… 10 000 = â€“$5000 negative contribution [3] Profit for the year with special order $85 000 [2] Profit for the year without special order $80 000 [1] Some attempt [1â€“2 marks] 4 9609/33 Cambridge International AS/A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2019 Page 10 of 15 Question Answer Marks

4(b) Refer to your results from

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜opportunity costâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two reasons why many new businesses fail in their first year of operation. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜opportunity costâ€™. The value of the next best opportunity. (1) That is given up by taking a particular decision. (1) Sound definition (2 marks) Partial definition â€“ (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

2(b) Explain two reasons why many new businesses fail in their first year of operation. Answers could include: â€¢ The product or service being offered is not wanted by consumers. â€¢ A lack of business/management experience causes poor decisions to be made. â€¢ A lack of finance. A new business might have to finance its operations for some time before income is sufficient to cover costs. â€¢ Lack of market research or poor market research resulting in flawed information meaning that the expected demand does not happen. â€¢ A sudden change in the economy that causes consumers to change their spending patterns. â€¢ A reaction from established competitors â€“ perhaps competitors increase their advertising or reduce the prices of their products, making it more difficult for the new business to survive. Sound explanation of two reasons why many new businesses fail in the first year (3 marks) Sound explanation of one reason or partial explanation of two reasons (2 marks) Limited explanation of one reasons or a list of two (1 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 6 of 14 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain why it is important for a manufacturing business to manage its inventory effectively. Answers could include: â€¢ A definition of inventory/inventory management. â€¢ A need to hold sufficient raw materials or components to meet the needs of the manufacturing process. This might depend on whether or not the business uses JIT. â€¢ Failure to hold sufficient inventory could lead to the production process being halted until new stock arrives. This can cause resources to be idle i.e. labour and machines. â€¢ An inability to manufacture could result in a failure to meet customer delivery requirements. This could cause a loss of future orders for the business. â€¢ Too much inventory is also an added cost to any business e.g. storage costs, insurance, etc. Effective explanation of the importance of effective inventory management to a manufacturing business (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of the importance of effective inventory management (2â€“3 marks) Understanding of inventory management (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 5 Question Answer Marks

Break-even is where TR = TC. â€¢ New businesses often struggle to achieve a profitable situation in the short term; break-even analysis can allow the new business to calculate the level of output that would be necessary to achieve a given level of profit. â€¢ The business might be uncertain of what price can be charged for their product/service; break-even analysis will enable them to test different price options. â€¢ It can also indicate if costs are too high and might indicate that cheaper options should be explored. â€¢ Break-even analysis is a relatively simple technique that can be completed by more inexperienced owners/managers. â€¢ A new business might be inexperienced at managing costs and using break-even analysis can provide guidelines for the business to work to. â€¢ The desired level of profit, the level of output and the price are dependent on consumer demand; completing a break-even analysis is a forecast of these levels, but not a guarantee. 8 9609/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 13 of 14 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Analyse the disadvantages to a business of using debt factoring to improve its cash flow. [8] (b) Discuss the limitations of using accounting ratios to compare the performance of different businesses. [12] 6 â€˜Good leaders are more important for business success than good managers.â€™ Discuss the extent to which you agree with this view. [20]
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse the disadvantages to a business of using debt factoring to improve its cash flow. Answers could include: Debt factoring is when a business sells its debts to a third party. Disadvantages: â€¢ The full value of the debt is not paid by the debt factor (usually about 80% of the value of the debt is received). â€¢ Debt factors usually refuse to take the long-term bad debts so the business still has debts that it might struggle to recover. â€¢ Customers might not like having their debt sold to a third party because they will now have to deal with someone outside of the original business they dealt with. â€¢ Customers might not want to deal with the business again. â€¢ Use of debt factoring could send a signal to stakeholders that the business has liquidity problems. This could impact on the relationship of the business with its stakeholders. For example, if it is a public limited company, shareholders may begin to sell shares believing that the business is no longer a good investment. 8 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 8 of 13 Question Answer Marks

5(b) Discuss the limitations of using accounting ratios to compare the performance of different businesses. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the limitations of using accounting ratios to compare the performance of different businesses. 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the limitations of using accounting ratios to compare the performance of different businesses. 5â€“6 2 Some application of the limitations of comparing accounting ratios between businesses. 3â€“4 1 Some understanding of accounting ratios. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content 0 Possible answers might include: This can depend on why the ratios are being compared and by whom â€“ if it is a potential investor then they will need a lot more information. Comparisons can only be reasonably made between business in the same industry and of the same size. E.g. a large business is likely to have more purchasing power than a small one and is likely to receive discounts which reduces costs. This will be reflected in ratio analysis. Ratios can show trends over time and between businesses but should be used with caution because: â€¢ The accounting techniques used differ between businesses â€“ different methods of depreciation can affect the profitability ratios making it difficult to make like-for-like comparisons. â€¢ Ratios are calculated using past information. The present situations in different businesses might be quite different. One business might be researching and introducing a new product shortly which could alter its ratios significantly now and in the future. â€¢ Ratios are not a certain indication of what is happening now or in the future. â€¢ Ratios do not give any qualitative information such as the level of motivation of the employees. This can impact on future performance and therefore ratios. â€¢ The values used from the statement of financial position are taken on one specific day. The results of the ratios

mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse the possible problems that a new business might experience when trying to raise finance. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the possible problems that a new business might experience when trying to raise finance. 7â€“8 3 Some analysis of the possible problems that a new business might experience when trying to raise finance 5â€“6 2 Some application of the possible problems of a business when trying to raise finance. 3â€“4 1 Some understanding of new businesses/finance 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers could include: â€¢ Often very difficult to raise large amounts of finance as a new business. â€¢ Lack of financial history â€“ therefore a lack of evidence that the business will be able to repay any finance loaned. â€¢ Lack of experience of the owner may lead to a lack of confidence in the financial management of the business. â€¢ Lack of security (collateral) to offer any potential lender. This can lead to higher rates of interest being charged due to perceived risk to the lender. â€¢ Bank overdrafts can often be negotiated but usually at high rates of interest â€“ this would impact on the costs of the business. â€¢ Credit purchases â€“ a new business might try to arrange to buy its goods on credit, but suppliers might be reluctant to do this due to the lack of credit history and knowledge of the new business. â€¢ If the new business is selling goods on credit and has to wait for its customers to pay before it pays its suppliers, this could add significant delay to the payment to suppliers â€“ this might mean that suppliers will not extend credit to the business until it is more established. â€¢ Family and friends might offer financial support, but this is likely to be limited and might cause some interference in the business from those friends and family. â€¢ Problems can be reduced by producing a realistic and well-thought out business plan. 8 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 8 of 12 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Short Answer and Essay October/November 2020 1 hour 15 minutes You must answer on the enclosed answer booklet. You will need: Answer booklet (enclosed) INSTRUCTIONS â— Section A: answer all questions. â— Section B: answer one question. â— Follow the instructions on the front cover of the answer booklet. INFORMATION â— The total mark for this paper is 40. â— The number of marks for each question or part question is shown in brackets [ ]. 2 9609/12/O/N/20 Section A (Short Answer) Answer all questions. You are advised to spend no more than 35 minutes on Section A. Define the term â€˜brandingâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two ways branding may help a business. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term â€˜brandingâ€™. â€¢ a marketing practice / value adding process (1) â€¢ that creates a name, symbol, image, trademark or description (1) â€¢ that distinguishes or differentiates products/services (1) â€¢ creating an identifiable image, design or expectation (1) â€¢ the identity, personality or status of a product (1) â€¢ that customers relate to (1) â€¢ creating a name for a family of products (1) â€¢ creates customer awareness (1) â€¢ credit example e.g. KFC (1) Sound definition: 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition: 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

1(b) Explain two ways branding may help a business. Answers could include: â€¢ a focus on a differentiated feature of a product such as â€˜qualityâ€™ could build loyalty and build reputation â€¢ gains competitive advantage through a USP â€¢ attracts more customers to the product â€¢ saves on advertising costs (a form of indirect advertising) â€¢ the promotion of brand characteristics such as â€˜high cost, high qualityâ€™ presents a promise and an expectation to customers which, if kept, will strengthen the position and reputation of the product in the eyes of the customer â€¢ branding will convey to the customer that value has been added to products and services in a distinctive way and so the reputation of the business is enhanced â€¢ branding will underpin and support all other marketing activities â€¢ will support the application of a premium price for a product â€¢ reduces competition in the market / gives competitive advantage â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Sound explanation of two ways branding may help a business (3 marks) Sound explanation of one way or partial explanation of two ways branding may help a business (2 marks) Limited explanation of one way or a list of two ways branding may help a business (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 3 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2020 Page 5 of 15 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜gross profit marginâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two ways a business might improve its profit margin. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜gross profit marginâ€™. â€¢ a profitability ratio that compares gross profit (profit before deduction of overheads) (1) â€¢ with revenue (1) â€¢ gross profit margin = Gross Profit Total Revenue (Sales) Ã— 100 (2) Sound definition of two of the factors listed above or the formula above (2 marks) Partial definition of one of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

2(b) Explain two ways a business might improve its profit margin. Answers may include: â€¢ reduce direct costs â€“ cheaper raw materials â€“ cut labour costs â€“ more capital intensive â€“ outsource production â€¢ reduce overhead costs â€“ cut rent, management costs â€“ increase organisational efficiency â€“ reduce promotional expenditure â€¢ increase price of product â€“ (assuming level and type of competition allows, and costs are kept the same) â€¢ reduce any discounts given â€¢ increase worker productivity â€¢ reduce inventory costs â€¢ focus on products with higher gross profit margin (reduce number of unprofitable products) â€¢ reduce turnaround time - time from order to delivery â€¢ process innovation - cut out operation steps â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Do not accept sell more or increase sales Sound explanation of two ways to improve its profit margin (3 marks) Sound explanation of one way or partial explanation of two ways to improve its profit margin (2 marks) Partial explanation of one way or a list of two ways to improve its profit margin (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 3 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2020 Page 6 of 15 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain why a business might remain a private limited company rather than change to a public limited company. Answers may include: â€¢ a definition of a private limited company might be given (probably a small/medium sized business) â€“ owned by shareholders â€“ often in same family â€“ unable to sell shares to the general public â€¢ concerned not to lose control by the current management team â€¢ want to retain the distinctive values and culture â€¢ have sources of finance â€“ e.g. venture capitalists â€“ so no need to go public â€¢ more likely to have shareholders more willing to invest and postpone dividend payments â€¢ may not be ready to float the business â€“ may be making losses â€¢ may have limited aspirations â€¢ may not want the expense and hassle of going public â€¢ do not want expense and scrutiny e.g. publishing accounts â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Effective explanation of why a business might remain as a private limited company rather than change to a public limited company. (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of why a business might remain as a private limited company rather than change to a public limited company (2â€“3 marks) Understanding of a private limited company (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 5 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Define the term â€˜effectivenessâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two ways a business might raise productivity levels. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only.
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) Define the term â€˜effectivenessâ€™. â€¢ the capability of management to achieve business objectives/targets/ goals (1) â€¢ in a specified time (1) â€¢ at a specified cost (1) â€¢ the capability of satisfying customer needs (1) â€¢ e.g. producing quality goods (1) â€¢ â€˜doing the right thingâ€™ (effectiveness) rather than just â€˜doing the thing rightâ€™ (efficiency) (1) Sound definition: 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition: 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2020 Page 7 of 15 Question Answer Marks

4(b) Explain two ways a business might raise productivity levels. Answers could include: â€¢ a definition of productivity â€“ the ratio of outputs to inputs during production â€“ the output per worker during a time period â€¢ raise skill levels of workers so they become more efficient â€¢ raise employee wages and increase motivation â€¢ more training for workers â€“ increase confidence and interest â€¢ increase motivation and wellbeing of workers â€¢ invest in more technology/capital equipment and so increase output with fewer staff â€¢ better maintenance schedules for capital equipment â€¢ increase the effectiveness of management â€¢ more leadership vision and inspiration â€“ share objectives â€¢ Accept any other valid response. mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term â€˜market researchâ€™. â€¢ the process of collecting data about customers / products / markets / competition (1) â€¢ analysing such data (1) â€¢ identifying aspects of a market to aid decision making (1) â€¢ identifying customer needs / demand of customers (1) â€¢ before a product is made (1) â€¢ collecting primary and/or secondary data (1) â€¢ using research methods such as surveys, interviews, internet records, government publications, product testing (1) Sound definition of 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition of 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

1(b) Explain two advantages to a business of using primary (field) market research data. (0 marks) 3 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2020 Page 5 of 15 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Define the term â€˜liquidity ratioâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two limitations of using accounting ratios. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜liquidity ratioâ€™. â€¢ it is a financial ratio (1) â€¢ assesses the short-term financial strength of a business (1) â€¢ ability to pay its short-term debts (1) â€¢ measures the liquidity of business assets (1) â€¢ a good liquidity ratio is greater than 1 (1) â€¢ formula given for either current ratio or acid test ratio (2) CR = Current assets Current liabilities ATR = âˆ’ Current assets inventory Current liabilities Sound definition of 2 of the factors listed above. (2 marks) Partial definition of 1 of the factors listed above. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

2(b) Explain two limitations of using accounting ratios. Answers may include: â€¢ may give a misleading picture of business performance if not combined with other data â€¢ ratio results on their own are of limited value â€“ need to be viewed over a period (trend) or compared with results from similar businesses â€¢ need for caution when comparisons made with other businesses â€“ there may be different ways of valuing assets â€“ may be window dressing â€¢ poor results only identify and highlight a potential business problem â€“ still need to analyse problems and devise solutions â€¢ only represent quantitative measures of performance â€“ other qualitative measures may be just as important â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Sound explanation of two limitations of using accounting ratios (3 marks) Sound explanation of one limitation of using accounting ratios or partial explanation of two limitations (2 marks) Partial explanation of one limitation of using accounting ratios or a list of two limitations (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 3 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2020 Page 6 of 15 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain why emotional intelligence is an important quality of an effective business leader. Explanations may include: â€¢ a definition of EI â€“ the ability of leaders/managers to understand their own emotions and those of the people they work with to achieve improved performance â€¢ Colemanâ€™s competencies â€“ self-awareness â€“ self-management â€“ social awareness â€“ social skills â€¢ what do leaders do â€“ motivate employees â€“ provide a vision for the successful achievement of goals/objectives â€¢ intellectual ability alone said to be insufficient â€“ EI is needed to empower employees â€¢ a leader with EI more likely to succeed than a cold, clinical IQ leader / manager â€¢ long-term business effectiveness requires leaders with IQ and EI. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: UPlane Components (UC) UC is a private limited company providing engine parts for commercial aircraft. It uses batch production in factory A and flow production in factory B. As part of UCâ€™s commitment to corporate social responsibility (CSR), it provides a training scheme for the long-term unemployed, based in factory A. UCâ€™s products are sold to aircraft engine manufacturers. Demand for aircraft engines has increased by 45% over recent years. The aircraft engine manufacturers want inventory just when needed and are demanding a reduction in prices. In 2017, UC opened factory B which is 5 km away from factory A. Factory B has a high level of automation, resulting in low unit costs for the parts produced there. Production is capital intensive. UC has a plan to automate factory A. The production workers are not happy about this proposal and have asked for more details. The workersâ€™ representatives have asked for a meeting with the human resource manager. In December 2019 a fault was discovered in one of the engine components supplied by UC and produced in factory A. UC had to recall 2000 parts at a cost of $200 000. This has had an impact on part of its triple bottom line and UC is unlikely to meet its targets. Table 1.1 shows some financial data for UC. Table 1.1: Financial data for UC Year ending
