subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Energy Solutions (ES) ES is a public limited company in country X. The business was set up in 1980. For 25 years most of ESâ€™s revenue came from coal mining. Although ES still owns many coal mines, the business now specialises in hydraulic fracturing, known as fracking. This is a process used to extract gas from underneath the ground. The government of country X encourages firms like ES to grow. The growth of ES has led to economies of scale and lower unit costs. ES considers the effects of fracking on all the stakeholders of the business. Fig. 1.1 is an extract from a recent newspaper article about fracking. Fracking is not liked by everyone. On the positive side it could produce enough gas to mean that country X can produce its own energy for the next 100 years. This is also likely to mean lower energy prices for both businesses and consumers. Competing companies in the market have taken full advantage of fracking and are expecting to increase their revenue and profit substantially in the future. However, people who live near the fracking sites have reported many minor earthquakes. These have not damaged any buildings but the price of houses in those areas has decreased significantly. There has also been a concern that fracking could lead to pollution and a loss of wildlife. Fig. 1.1: Extract from a recent newspaper article about fracking Despite the complaints from some external stakeholders, ES plans to increase the number of fracking sites in country X. This will require ES to buy licences from the government of country X. Each licence costs $50m and ES will require both internal and external sources of finance to fund this purchase. ES employs over 1000 people. Every worker benefits from a profit-sharing scheme (see Table 1.1) as well as their basic pay. [2] (ii) Explain the term â€˜unit costsâ€™ (line 6). [3] (b) (i) Refer to Table 1.1. Calculate the difference in dollars received by each director and each manager from the profit-sharing scheme. question_text: Aashnaâ€™s Pies (AP) AP produces and sells high quality pies. Aashna set up AP five years ago when she spotted a gap in the market. AP is a private limited company and Aashna is the majority shareholder. All of the pies use the AP branding which is growing in popularity. AP owns a factory where all the pies are produced. Pies are packaged and sold individually. The range of pies produced by AP is shown in Table 1.1. 1(b) Explain two disadvantages to employees of autocratic leadership. Answers could include: â€¢ The strict supervision might be demotivating. â€¢ A lack of ideas from the employees; they do not feel valued or trusted. â€¢ One-way communication; the ideas of employees are not heard. â€¢ A lack of motivation / job satisfaction / morale. â€¢ Less chance for employees to ask questions. â€¢ Less chance/opportunity for employee creativity. â€¢ Lack of trust between managers and employees. â€¢ Decisions only taken by hierarchy, do not reflect the business as a whole. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. question_text: Define the term â€˜market segmentationâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two benefits to a business of market segmentation. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜market segmentationâ€™. â€¢ an approach to marketing (1) â€¢ identifies different sub-groups within a market (1) â€¢ targeting different products to each market segment / type of customer (1) â€¢ segmentation might be due to age / social factors / income levels / geographic / sociological factors. (1) â€¢ customers with similar needs/characteristics (1) Sound definition 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2 9609/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 7 of 18 Question Answer Marks

2(b) Explain two benefits to a business of market segmentation. Answers could include: â€¢ better able to target potential customers. â€¢ target only segments that have high demand to maximise profits. â€¢ marketing costs can be lowered due to more specific targeting. â€¢ marketing personalised to maintain brand loyalty. â€¢ different strategies can be used for different segments. â€¢ products/services can be designed/developed to satisfy very specific needs of a group of consumers. â€¢ businesses can possibly use price discrimination by charging different prices to different sub-groups/segments. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Sound explanation of two benefits to a business of market segmentation. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one benefit to a business of market segmentation or a partial explanation of two benefits. (2 marks) Partial explanation of one benefit to a business of market segmentation or a list of two benefits. (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 8 of 18 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain the advantages to a manufacturing business of using sale and leaseback of non-current assets as a source of finance. Answers could include: â€¢ Where a business sells an asset in order to raise finance and leases the same asset back from the new owner. â€¢ The business will have an inflow of money which could be large if the non-current asset is, for example, a building. This can be used to fund projects or equipment. â€¢ The business will still have access to and use of the asset after the sale, therefore the sale should not cause any disruption to the operation of the business. â€¢ The payment of regular lease payments can allow for ease of budgeting for a business. â€¢ The maintenance of the asset is usually taken over by the new owner, therefore reducing ongoing maintenance costs for the business when it leases the asset back. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Effective explanation of the advantages to a manufacturing business of using sale and leaseback of non-current assets as a source of finance. (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of the advantages to a manufacturing business of using sale and leaseback of non-current assets as a source of finance. (2â€“3 marks) Explanation of sale and leaseback. (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 5 Question Answer

question_text: Define the term â€˜inventory reorder levelâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two advantages to a business of holding buffer inventory. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only.
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) Define the term â€˜inventory reorder levelâ€™. â€¢ trigger amount / pre-set level of inventory (1) â€¢ where / at which point a business would place a new order for inventory (1) â€¢ allows time for new inventory to arrive before current stock runs out (1) Sound definition 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2 9609/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 9 of 18 Question Answer Marks

4(b) Explain two advantages to a business of holding buffer inventory. Answers could include: â€¢ Allows a business to meet increases in demand. â€¢ It prevents shortages in the case of a late delivery or disruption of production of work in progress. â€¢ It allows a business to take advantage of bulk buying discounts that can significantly reduce the costs of inventory. â€¢ Reduces the effect of price fluctuations. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜job productionâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two advantages to a business of changing from job production to batch production. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜job productionâ€™. â€¢ production of one-off items, one-by-one (1) â€¢ specialised/unique items designed by/for customers (1) â€¢ hand-made/limited supply (1) â€¢ each item finished before next one produced (1) â€¢ correct example e.g. designer dresses, watches (1) Sound definition 2 of the factors listed above (2 marks) Partial definition 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

2(b) Explain two advantages to a business of changing from job production to batch production. Answers could include: In batch production, groups of identical products are produced together. â€¢ economies of scale/ cost reduction as the scale of production increases. â€¢ speed of production will be increased due to use of machinery and less skilled workers due to repetition of tasks. â€¢ less capital because the same equipment can be used for multiple products unlike the often very specialised equipment used in job production. â€¢ customer demands can still be met but in the production of a batch of several products rather than one individual product. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Sound explanation of two advantages to a business of changing from job production to batch production. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one advantage to a business of changing from job production to batch production or a partial explanation of two advantages. (2 marks) Limited explanation of one advantage to a business of changing from job production to batch production or a list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 9 of 19 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain the importance of working capital to a new business. Answers could include: â€¢ working capital is used to finance the day to day running of a business. â€¢ A new business has to cover its current liabilities with its current assets if it is to remain viable. â€¢ it can take some time for a new business to become known and to attract sufficient customers to generate profit. Working capital is essential to help the business survive these early days. â€¢ a new business had to finance its start-up and then needs a flow of money e.g. to be able to pay for labour and inventory. â€¢ if the premises are rented/mortgaged then regular payments will have to be made. â€¢ resources are likely to be purchased and so money needs to be available to pay for this. â€¢ suppliers are less likely to offer credit terms so all supplies will have to be paid cash on delivery. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Effective explanation of the importance of working capital to a new business. (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of the importance of working capital to a business. (2â€“3 marks) Explanation of working capital. (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 5 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 10 of 19 Question Answer Marks

4(a) Define the term â€˜contract of employmentâ€™. â€¢ a formal/legal document/deed/agreement (1) â€¢ between a business and an employee (1) â€¢ with reference to employment responsibilities / tasks / working hours / method and/or rate of pay (1) Sound definition 2 of the factors listed above (2marks) Partial definition 1 of the factors listed above (1 mark) No creditable content (0 marks) 2

4(b) Explain the difference between redundancy and dismissal. Answers could include: â€¢ redundancy is a form of dismissal when a particular job or role is no longer required by a business so an employeeâ€™s contract is terminated. â€¢ it is the job/role that is no longer required rather than the person. â€¢ the employee is not at fault in the case of redundancy. â€¢ dismissal is when an employeeâ€™s contract of employment is terminated, because of misconduct of the employee e.g. violent conduct, theft, continual absenteeism or lateness. â€¢ legally, redundancy is a form of dismissal â€“ an employee ends up without a job but through no fault of their own. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Sound explanation of the difference between redundancy and dismissal. (3 marks) Limited explanation of the difference between redundancy and dismissal. (2 marks) Understanding of redundancy or dismissal. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 11 of 19 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Analyse how product portfolio analysis can help a business to achieve its marketing objectives. [8] (b) Discuss the extent to which a new entrant in the computer manufacturing market will need to rely on penetration pricing to gain market share. [12] 6 â€˜The success of any manufacturing business will depend on the productivity of its workforce.â€™ Discuss the extent to which you agree with this view. [20]
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse how product portfolio analysis can help a business to achieve its marketing objectives. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of how product portfolio analysis can help a business to achieve its marketing objectives. 7â€“8 3 Limited analysis of how product portfolio analysis can help a business to achieve its marketing objectives. 5â€“6 2 Application of how product portfolio analysis can help a business. 3â€“4 1 Knowledge and understanding of product portfolio analysis / marketing objectives. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers could include: Knowledge and understanding 2 marks â€¢ Clear understanding of product portfolio analysis â€¢ Clear understanding of marketing objectives Application 2 marks â€¢ Reference to the use of product portfolio analysis â€¢ Reference to the use of marketing objectives Analysis 4 marks â€¢ Product portfolio analysis â€“ assesses the range of products/services offered by a business. This analysis is often integrated with the product life cycle. â€¢ Marketing objectives should be in line with the overall business objectives and might be: â€“ to increase market share â€“ to become the market leader â€“ to increase sales volume â€“ to increase the product range â€“ to improve customer satisfaction â€“ to increase brand loyalty â€“ to enter new markets. 8 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 12 of 19 Question Answer Marks

5(a) â€¢ An increase in market share might require the business to reassess the range or products or services being offered. Perhaps a competitor offers a wider range or a better quality. â€¢ An increase in market share often needs a business to stand out and therefore the business might need to look at whether or not its products are outdated and perhaps there is a need to introduce extension strategies or to introduce new product(s). â€¢ Linking to the product life cycle, if the business has too many products in the maturity or decline stages this would indicate that new products are required if the marketing objectives are to be achieved. â€¢ New markets might require products to be adjusted/changed to meet the needs of the customers in that market. â€¢ Successful businesses often have products in all stages of the product life cycle; new products being developed to take the place of ones in decline or to meet the changing needs of the market. â€¢ The range of products offered will have to be adjusted to meet the marketing objectives and if the objectives change the products might have to change too. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 13 of 19 Question Answer Marks

5(b) Discuss the extent to which a new entrant in the computer manufacturing market will need to rely on penetration pricing to gain market share. 7(a) Analyse the benefits to a business of accurate cost information. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the benefits to a business of accurate cost information. 7â€“8 3 Limited analysis of the benefits to a business of accurate cost information. 5â€“6 2 Application of the benefits of cost information. 3â€“4 1 Knowledge and understanding of cost information. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers could include: Knowledge and Understanding 2 marks â€¢ Clear understanding of cost information Application 2 marks â€¢ Reference to the benefits of cost information Analysis 4 marks â€¢ Managers need cost information to be able to assess and monitor the expenses of the business. â€¢ Without cost information the price of products/services might be set too low and costs would not be covered by the selling price. â€¢ The level of costs determines the level of profit. If the cost information is inaccurate the business might be expecting to make a profit that does not happen: the business might even make a loss. â€¢ Inefficiencies can be highlighted by looking at the cost information for different parts of a business and actions can be taken to correct any problems. If the information is not available or is inaccurate the inefficiency might go unnoticed. â€¢ Inaccurate cost data might incorrectly indicate an efficiency or inefficiency and the wrong action might be taken. â€¢ Managers can compare trends over time and monitor any changes and take corrective action. â€¢ The important element is that cost information needs to be accurate. Having lots of cost data that is not accurate will be of little use to managers and might lead them to make decisions that are damaging to the business. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. 8 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 18 of 19 Question Answer Marks

7(b) Discuss the usefulness of liquidity ratios to potential investors in a business. Level Description Marks 4 Effective evaluation of the usefulness of liquidity ratios to potential investors in a business. 9â€“12 3 Limited evaluation of the usefulness of liquidity ratios to a business. 7â€“8 2 Analysis and application of the usefulness of liquidity ratios to a business. 3â€“6 1 Knowledge and understanding of liquidity ratios / investors 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers could include: Knowledge and Understanding 2 marks â€¢ Clear understanding of liquidity ratios â€¢ Clear understanding of investors Application 2 marks â€¢ Reference to liquidity ratios â€¢ Reference to the role of business investors Analysis 2 marks â€¢ Liquidity ratios measure the ability of a business to pay its current liabilities. â€¢ Two key definitions: The acid test ratio and the current ratio. Acid test = current assets inventory current liabilities âˆ’ Current ratio = current assets current liabilities â€¢ For most businesses, the current ratio is an adequate measure of liquidity but for a business that holds a high level of inventory then a safer measure might be the acid test

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜economies of scaleâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two possible causes of diseconomies of scale. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜economies of scaleâ€™. â€¢ reduction in costs of production/operations. (1) â€¢ due to increase in size/scale of production / output. (1) â€¢ correct diagram showing economies of scale. (2) Sound definition 2 of the factors listed above. (2 marks) Partial definition 1 of the factors listed above. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

2(b) Explain two possible causes of diseconomies of scale. Answers could include: â€¢ Communication diseconomies: increased output leading to an increase in the workforce, making it more difficult to communicate directly with employees. Less direct methods of communication might need to be used which might be less effective. Feedback from employees/departments is likely to be more difficult to obtain. â€¢ Co-ordination diseconomies: more people, more departments and possibly more functions within a business make the monitoring and managing of individual performances or processes more difficult to achieve, making co-ordination of activities more difficult. Timing of activities â€“ it might become more difficult to ensure that activities are completed at the same/required time. The values of the business might not be employed to the same extent in each section of the business e.g. ethical values, environmental values. â€¢ Control diseconomies: employees can feel more alienated in a larger organisation and the quality standard might fall. More output can make it more difficult to check each item for quality. It is more difficult to control several departments on numerous sites than to monitor and control the functions within a smaller business. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Sound explanation of two possible causes of diseconomies of scale. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one possible cause of diseconomies of scale or partial explanation of two causes. (2 marks) Partial explanation of one possible cause of diseconomies of scale or list of two . (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 9 of 19 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Explain why cost-based pricing might be an effective pricing strategy for a small business. [5]
mark_scheme_text: 3(a) Explain why cost-based pricing might be an effective pricing strategy for a small business. Answers could include: â€¢ Cost-based pricing is determined by the costs of production of a product/service. â€¢ Cost plus pricing - the full costs of a product are calculated and a margin is added to achieve the desired profit level. â€¢ Contribution cost pricing â€“ in a small business, it is likely that the fixed costs per product are known due to the small scale of the business, making it easier to include a proportion of them in the price of each item produced. â€¢ A small business needs to ensure that all the costs of production are covered by the price charged to its customers. â€¢ The fixed costs together with all variable costs will be included and a percentage added to give a profit. â€¢ This can ensure that the business recovers all costs when goods are sold. â€¢ The amount of profit added on to the costs of the business can be adjusted to make sure that the business is able to compete on price with its competitors. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Effective explanation of why cost-based pricing might be an effective pricing strategy for a small business. (4â€“5 marks) Limited explanation of why cost-based pricing might be an effective pricing strategy for a business. (2â€“3 marks) Explanation of cost-based pricing. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 5 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 10 of 19 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜labour turnoverâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two ways in which the recruitment and selection process of a business could help to improve labour turnover. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only.
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) Define the term â€˜labour turnoverâ€™. â€¢ The rate at which employees leave a business (2) â€¢ Correct formula Number of employees leaving (in a year) 100 Number of employees in the business (in a year) Ã— (2) â€¢ Employees leaving the business. (1) â€¢ In a given time period. (1) â€¢ Through dismissal, resignation, redundancy, new employment. (1) Sound definition will be either the first bullet point or the formula or 2 of the other 3 bullet points. (2 marks) Partial definition 1 of bullet points 3, 4 or 5. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 2

4(b) Explain two ways in which the recruitment and selection process of a business could help to improve labour turnover. Answers could include: â€¢ be sure that the job description accurately describes the tasks and responsibilities of the job to recruit the most suitable people. â€¢ be sure that the person specification is focused on the qualities and qualifications required when recruiting staff. â€¢ Investigate/analyse previous employeesâ€™ reasons for leaving. â€¢ use an appropriate medium for advertising any posts e.g. social media, recruitment sites or agencies, local newspapers, depending on the role. â€¢ check that applicants have the relevant essential and desirable qualities. If not, then reject the application. â€¢ use an appropriate selection technique or a combination of methods e.g. an aptitude test followed by an interview. It is possible that using only one technique might not give the most reliable results. For example, some people are able to interview well but might not perform as well in an aptitude test. â€¢ the more thorough/appropriate the recruitment and selection process is, the more likely it is that the best candidate is selected. If the person is right for the job, it is less likely that they will leave, therefore labour turnover will improve. â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Sound explanation of two ways. (3 marks) Sound explanation of one way or partial explanation of two ways. (2 marks) Partial explanation of one way or list of two ways. (1 mark) No creditable content. (0 marks) 3 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 11 of 19 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Analyse the possible disadvantages to a business of changing from batch production to flow production. [8] (b) Discuss the benefits to a clothing business of holding buffer inventory rather than using a Just in Time (JIT) inventory system. [12]
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse the possible disadvantages to a business of changing from batch production to flow production. Level Description Marks 4 Good analysis of the possible disadvantages to a business of changing from batch to flow production. 7â€“8 3 Limited analysis of the possible disadvantages to a business of changing from batch to flow production. 5â€“6 2 Application of the possible disadvantages to a business of changing from batch to flow production. 3â€“4 1 Knowledge and understanding of batch/ flow production. 1â€“2 0 No creditable content. 0 Answers could include: Knowledge and Understanding 2 marks â€¢ Clear understanding of batch production. â€¢ Clear understanding of flow production. Application 2 marks â€¢ Reference to change from batch to flow production. Analysis 4 marks â€¢ Batch: the production of a limited number of identical products. Each stage of production is completed before the next stage commences. â€¢ Flow: the production of identical products in a continuous flow. Disadvantages can include: â€¢ The high cost of setting up a flow production method. More equipment might be involved that adds to the costs of production in the short-term. â€¢ Fewer employees might be needed, causing some redundancies, leading to demotivated workforce. â€¢ High levels of output usually required to make flow production a profitable option. Is demand of the business high enough to justify the change? â€¢ If there is a fall in demand it is more difficult to switch to the production of different products because flow production usually has dedicated equipment for the production on one product. â€¢ If the business produces a wide variety of products, batch production might be more efficient. They have formed a co-operative called JTG which is based in city M. JTG is planning to hire a theatre in city M so that the co-operative members can act in a play. The theatre has 300 seats but Juliet has set a target of 200 customers to break even. This should ensure a margin of safety. City M is a large city with many theatres and music venues. Juliet estimates that they can sell 150 tickets in advance to friends, family and on the internet. Unsold tickets will be available to buy on the night of the performance. JTG is planning to perform the play at the hired theatre in city M for one night only. Financial information is shown in Table 2.1. Table 2.1: Financial information ($) Theatre hire per night (paid in advance) 600 Costume hire 200 Printing tickets 100 Variable costs per ticket Juliet has proposed going to different cities with the play, putting on performances for one night in each city. She thinks that JTG should put on performances only in city M and has given the following reasons: â€¢ there is a suitable venue in city M â€¢ it is easier to build customer loyalty â€¢ minimal costs. Juliet disagrees. She thinks that JTG could: â€¢ build up a larger customer base by going to other cities â€¢ perform the same play in different cities so no need to rehearse other plays â€¢ access government grants for travelling acting groups. question_text: Hot Bubblez (HB) Karen is a sole trader who operates a service business called Hot Bubblez (HB) from her own home. It is a laundry service in town P. Each day Karen spends the morning collecting laundry from customersâ€™ homes. Karen then washes, dries and irons the laundry and then delivers it back to customersâ€™ homes in the evening. She owns two washing machines, two drying machines and one iron. Each batch of washing takes approximately two hours to wash, dry and iron. Karen has completed some market research about which method of laundry households use in town P (see Fig. 1.1). There are 60 000 households in town P. Use a laundry service 20% Do laundry at home 65% Go to a laundry shop (launderette) 15% Fig. 1.1: The percentage of households using each laundry method in town P Karen has 300 households that use HBâ€™s laundry service. Karen would like to employ someone to do the collections and deliveries. Karen has always relied upon customer recommendations to promote HB. However, she has not been able to gain any new customers and she thinks it is time for new promotion methods to be used. She would like to target the hotel laundry market. Table 1.1 shows some data about the local hotel market. Table 1.1: Data about the local hotel market Number of hotels in town P

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜overdraftâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two reasons why a business might use an overdraft. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜overdraftâ€™. â€¢ bank agrees to lend up to a set amount of money (1) â€¢ when an account reaches zero/as and when needed (1) â€¢ a short-term extension of credit (1) â€¢ a flexible borrowing facility (1) â€¢ source of finance/current liability/interest charged/no need for collateral (1) Sound definition â€“ two of the points listed above. 2 Partial definition â€“ one of the points listed above. 1 No creditable content. 0 2

2(b) Explain two reasons why a business might use an overdraft. Answers could include: â€¢ allows short-term funding for businesses to make payments to improve working capital, e.g. day-to-day expenses â€¢ use overdrafts to facilitate cash flow management, e.g. payment to suppliers â€¢ enables the handling of emergency cash flow events â€¢ more convenient to arrange/less paperwork â€¢ a business might use an overdraft facility as an alternative to factoring â€¢ may use an overdraft as an alternative to a loan â€“ only borrow what is needed at a particular time â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Do not accept â€˜start upâ€™ or â€˜growth/expansionâ€™ or â€˜capital/machineryâ€™ for the use of an overdraft. Sound explanation of two reasons for a business using an overdraft. 3 Sound explanation of one reason or partial explanation of two reasons. 2 Partial explanation of one reason or a list of two reasons . 1 No creditable content. 0 3 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2021 Page 7 of 16 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain why businesses invest in the training and development of employees. Answers could include: â€¢ T&D is seen as an important investment in the support and maintenance of employee skills and abilities â€¢ specific T&D may be identified â€“ e.g. induction training/new skills training/re-deployment â€¢ can lead to higher productivity and increased motivation â€¢ can lead to employee loyalty and lower labour turnover â€¢ can lead to employer becoming the employer of choice â€¢ can mean lower recruitment and selection costs â€¢ can lead to higher customer service â€“ a USP â€“ competitive advantage â€¢ more important in some businesses than others â€¢ Accept any other valid response. Effective explanation of reasons for investment in training and development. 4â€“5 Limited explanation of reasons for investment in training and development. 2â€“3 Understanding of training and development. 1 No creditable content. 0 5 Question Answer Marks

Answers could include: Knowledge and understanding â€¢ Clear understanding of debt factoring Application â€¢ Reference to a relevant business context, e.g. a business using debt factoring Analysis â€¢ The selling of invoices to a third party at a discounted price in order to bypass often long waiting times for payment, although it reduces overall profit for businesses â€“ usually between 1%â€“3% of the invoice value is charged â€¢ Especially popular with small businesses in that it provides instant access to working capital, but less control over sales and less relationship with customers Advantages â€¢ Greatest advantage is that it can improve cash flow â€“ discounted cash value of invoices instantly released â€¢ Can use this cash to operate the business and re-invest in the business â€¢ Good cash flow is vital as it is the key to day-to-day operations and growth â€“ can take on more work and complete more jobs, gain more revenue â€¢ Saves time in managing and chasing up invoices, frees up management time and this contributes to overall efficiency â€¢ Debt factoring can lead to accelerated growth and eventual building up of funds that means debt factoring is no longer required â€¢ Accept any other valid response. 8 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2021 Page 15 of 16 Question Answer Marks

mark_scheme_text:

Level 4 â€“ Good analysis of the advantages of a Just in Time (JIT) system of inventory management. 7â€“8 Level 3 â€“ Some analysis of the advantages of a Just in Time (JIT) system of inventory management. 5â€“6 Level 2 â€“ Some explanation/application of the advantages of a Just in Time (JIT) system of inventory management. 3â€“4 Level 1 â€“ Understanding of JIT. 1â€“2 Level 0 â€“ No creditable content. 0 Answers could include: Knowledge and understanding â€¢ Clear understanding of JIT Application â€¢ Reference to a relevant business context, e.g. business use of JIT Analysis â€¢ JIT â€“ avoids holding high levels of inventory â€“ requires supplies to arrive just as needed â€¢ Manufacturing likely to require significant amount of supplies and flow production very dependent on regular and timely supplies â€¢ JIT system, if effective, will reduce capital investment in stock â€“ opportunity for alternative capital investment â€¢ Cost of storage reduced â€“ warehouses reduced/eliminated â€¢ JIT adds value by increasing efficiency â€“ costs are lowered by intelligent working that eliminates waste â€¢ A JIT model allows a manufacturer a better level of control over the entire manufacturing process â€¢ Workers can focus on primary tasks â€“ manufacturing rather than stocking material â€¢ JIT makes the entire workflow of a manufacturing company more efficient â€¢ Accept any other valid response. 8 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2021 Page 10 of 16 Question Answer Marks

â€¢ Clear understanding of break-even analysis â€¢ Clear understanding of an entrepreneur â€¢ Clear understanding of a new business Application â€¢ Reference to a relevant business context: an entrepreneur starting a new business Analysis â€¢ Entrepreneurs strive to establish businesses through innovation and risk â€¢ In order to succeed, entrepreneurs must minimise risk of failure through effective planning and research before launching a business â€¢ Break-even analysis (formula) helps an entrepreneur determine the sales quantity (volume) of a product that must be sold to generate enough revenue to pay costs over a given period of time â€“ this information will allow an entrepreneur to avoid incurring losses â€¢ It allows an entrepreneur to estimate how much profit can be earned at different sales levels â€“ this helps to set sales targets that will achieve desired profit levels â€¢ Setting the right price for a product is a major decision to be made â€“ setting different price levels and evaluating break-even analysis allows an entrepreneur to review the effect of that price on the profitability of the business â€“ will allow an entrepreneur to study the effect of each price level in relation to other factors such as price competition and consumer affordability â€¢ After studying break-even analysis, an entrepreneur can set inventory management levels The number of cafÃ©s in country S has increased by more than 50% over the last 5 years. This has meant that QF has been able to expand and achieve internal economies of scale. Inventory QFâ€™s inventory includes raw materials, work in progress and finished tables and chairs. QF buys 80% of its materials from country T and there is a long delivery lead time. This means that QF holds a high level of buffer inventory which has a high value. Human resources QFâ€™s production employees are unhappy that they have not received any benefit from the companyâ€™s expansion. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Snappy Box (SB) SB is owned by Ralph who is a sole trader. The business prints photographs. Ralph has one shop on the main street of city D. Customers bring their saved digital photographs into the shop and these are printed on high-quality paper. SB uses a large printing machine that can print on almost any size of paper to produce different sized photograph prints. The process is very capital-intensive and most customers request a batch of photographs to be printed. SB is the only shop in city D that prints photographs. However, recently a number of online competitors have started to offer low-priced photograph prints to customers. Ralph has noticed that his sales have decreased significantly because of this competition. Ralph estimates the demand for his photograph prints has a price elasticity of demand of â€“4. SB already has a low profit margin and Ralph is struggling to compete with the online retailers. However, Ralph has an idea to introduce job production into his shop. He could stop printing photographs and instead focus on framing individual photographs for customers. These frames will be made for any sized photograph or picture and can be made from a variety of materials chosen by the customer. Ralph will need specialised equipment to allow him to make the frames. The equipment would cost $10 000. He has identified two possible sources of finance for this equipment. The first possible source of finance is for Ralph to lease the equipment from the company that produces it. The lease would be for five years at a fixed cost of $400 per month. The second possible source of finance is for Ralph to sell the photograph printing machine for at least $10 000, to purchase the equipment to make frames. 5 10 15

4(b) Knowledge â€¢ Quality relates to whether a product meets customer expectations. â€¢ Quality control uses quality inspectors at end of production to identify faulty output. â€¢ Quality assurance is a system of agreeing and meeting quality standards at each stage of production to ensure customer satisfaction. â€¢ TQM is an approach to quality that aims to involve all employees in quality improvements. TQM includes: âˆ’ quality chains, quality circles, zero defects, concept of internal customers and kaizen. Application Linking points to TQM. â€¢ Removes need for quality control inspectors. â€¢ Cost savings from reduced rework of faulty goods. $10 710 (OFR) is current monthly cost of substandard output requiring rework. â€¢ AC workers are skilled. Other factors causing problems. â€¢ Current employee concerns over lack of empowerment. â€¢ Pay currently 90% of industry average â€“ workers do not feel valued. â€¢ Labour turnover is 15%. â€¢ Production stopped due to supply chain issue. Analysis â€¢ Quality chains and internal customers will reduce rejects as next stage employees will be unwilling to accept substandard items. â€¢ TQM makes explicit the need for quality assurance throughout the process so decreases likelihood of poor work. â€¢ Greater responsibility for workers may increase motivation, thus reducing labour turnover. â€¢ Not all employees will embrace having greater responsibility and may be demotivated, leading to other production problems. â€¢ Employe

mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term â€˜social enterpriseâ€™ . A business with mainly social objectives (1) such as environmental or social projects (1) A business which reinvests profits into the business (1) A business that reinvests profits to benefit society/community (1) rather than maximise returns to owners (1) A business that focuses on triple bottom line or corporate social responsibility (1) Often described as not-for-profit businesses/organisations (1) such as charities (1) Clear definition of 2 of the factors listed above 2 marks Partial definition of 1 of the factors listed above 1 mark No creditable content 0 marks 2

1(b) Explain two advantages to a business of being a social enterprise . Answers could include: ï‚· A social enterprise might attract more customers just because of its aim of benefitting the community ï‚· The business might be seen as more ethical than other businesses which again can increase demand for its product/service ï‚· People might be willing to pay a higher price because the business regards people and the planet as more important than profit ï‚· Employees might be easier to recruit as people seek to work in social enterprises ï‚· Employees might be easier to retain leading to lower labour turnover ï‚· Employees are committed and motivated by the values and aspirations of a social enterprise ï‚· Government support/grants/subsidies might be available unlike in businesses with purely a profit motive Accept any other valid response Clear explanation of two advantages to a business of being a social enterprise 3 marks Clear explanation of one advantage or a partial explanation of two advantages to a business of being a social enterprise 2 marks Partial explanation of one advantage to a business of being a social enterprise or a list of two advantages 1 mark No creditable content 0 marks 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 8 of 18 Question Answer Marks

2(b) Explain two benefits to a business of autocratic leadership . Answers could include: ï‚· Employees know clearly that they are expected to follow orders ï‚· There is close supervision of employees so fewer mistakes ï‚· Quicker decision-making. Less time spent/wasted consulting employees before decisions are made ï‚· Productivity may improve due to no time wasted on discussion ï‚· Less conflict due to no discussion ï‚· Clear communication channel from the owner/leader (clear who gives instructions and who follows them) ï‚· Non-participating employees are more focused ï‚· More experienced/qualified/skilled leaders take decisions Accept any other valid response . Clear explanation of two benefits to a business of autocratic leadership. 3 marks Clear explanation of one benefit to a business of autocratic leadership or a partial explanation of two benefits of autocratic leadership. 2 marks Partial explanation of one benefit to a business of autocratic leadership or a list of two benefits 1 mark No creditable content 0 marks 3 9609/11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 9 of 18 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain how price elasticity of demand influences the pricing decisions of a business . Answers could include: Price elasticity of demand measures the response of demand to any change in the price of a product. ï‚· Behaviour will depend on whether the PED of products is elastic or inelastic ï‚· Price elastic â€“ a business wishing to increase market share can reduce price knowing that demand for its products will increase by a greater percentage than the percentage decrease in price. This achieves an increase in total revenue ï‚· Price inelastic â€“ if an increase in profit is a business aim, a business might decide to increase the price of its products knowing that the percentage fall in demand will be lower than the percentage increase in price = increase in total revenue. ï‚· If a business wants to increase market share, a decrease in price would possibly not be as effective as for a price elastic product â€“ the percentage increase in demand for price elastic products would be smaller than the percentage decrease in price = reduced revenue and only a small increase in demand ï‚· PED will influence the establishment by a business of an ideal price for a product in the marketing mix ï‚· The way in which PED might influence businesses can depend on the objectives of the business ï‚· A business that wishes to maximise its revenue might behave differently to a business that wishes to maximise sales to gain market share Accept any other valid response. Answers could include: Time based payments are made to employees based on the number of hours worked within a given time period. ï‚· A business only pays for the hours worked, unlike for an employee who receives a fixed payment per week or month regardless of the length of time worked ï‚· This method can avoid some of the quality issues that can arise when using the e.g. piece rate payment method â€“ fast work can lead to poor quality ï‚· It is easy for a business to calculate â€“ it reduces administrative costs/time for a business ï‚· A business can set the number of hours per week that should be worked and can therefore organise the number of workers in terms of when and how long they are required and know plan its costs ï‚· Productivity may increase when a business requires it if workers are willing to work overtime to get more money ï‚· Productivity may increase because it improves motivation in employees; encourages them to work harder for a business Accept any other valid response. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜franchiseâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two advantages to a business of buying a franchise. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term â€˜franchiseâ€™ . A franchise is a business that uses the name/logo of an existing business (2) A legal contract between two businesses for one business to operate as a franchise (2) The process of a franchisor selling the rights of its business to a franchisee (2) Example of a franchise, e.g. McDonaldâ€™s, KFC (1) Clear definition 2 marks Partial definition 1 mark No creditable content 0 marks 2

2(b) Explain two advantages to a business of buying a franchise . Answers could include: ï‚· The business can benefit from the reputation of the franchise giving a higher chance of success ï‚· Marketing/advertising costs may be spread between all franchisees and have national/wider coverage ï‚· Help and advice from the franchisor can be useful to a business new to the franchise (industry) or new to business ownership of any type ï‚· Increased chance of success because the product/service is established (known by customers) ï‚· Competition might be limited due to the franchisor restricting the setting up of another outlet in the same area ï‚· Opportunity for the franchisee to expand their business ï‚· Relations with suppliers already established Accept any other valid response. Clear explanation of two advantages to a business of buying a franchise 3 marks Clear explanation of one advantage or a partial explanation of two advantages 2 marks Partial explanation of one advantage or a list of two advantages 1 mark No creditable content 0 marks 3 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 10 of 20 Question Answer Marks 3 Explain why a business manager needs to understand the difference between capital expenditure and revenue expenditure. Answers could include: ï‚· Capital expenditure is money spent on the purchase of fixed assets such as premises and machinery. Items that will be in use for a long period (more than 1 year) which managers must plan for ï‚· Revenue expenditure is money used to pay for everyday running costs of a business, e.g., raw materials or components, tea or coffee for the canteen or paper for the offices. These items will be used in the short term and, even though lower value, must still be planned for ï‚· They should normally be funded differently; working capital / retained capital ï‚· Revenue expenditure recorded correctly allows a manager to accurately measure the cost of the day-to-day running of a business which is vital information in the early days of a business. ï‚· They are recorded on different financial documents: revenue expenditure is recorded in the Income Statement; capital expenditure is recorded in the Statement of Financial Position â€“ managers use these documents to assess the financial situation of a business Accept any other valid response. Effective explanation of why a business manager needs to understand the difference between capital expenditure and revenue expenditure 4â€“5 marks Explanation of why a business manager needs to understand the difference between capital expenditure

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term â€˜price elasticity of demandâ€™. [2] (b) Explain two benefits to a business of knowing the price elasticity of demand of its products. [3] Section B (Essay) Answer one question only.
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) Define the term â€˜price elasticity of demandâ€™ . Correct answers must refer to the responsiveness of the demand for a product. Price elasticity of demand (PED) measures the degree of responsiveness of demand (1) to a change in the price of the product/service. (1) % change in quantity of demand for a product PED = % change in price of the product (2) Clear definition of 2 of the factors listed above or formula 2 marks Partial definition of 1 of the factors listed above 1 mark No creditable content 0 marks 2 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 11 of 20 Question Answer Marks

4(b) Explain two benefits to a business of knowing the price elasticity of demand of its products. Answers could include: ï‚· Allows a business to anticipate the reaction of its customers to a change in price and to forecast the level of sales following a change in price ï‚· Pricing strategies (e.g. price discrimination) are influenced by an understanding of how demand might be affected by price changes ï‚· Maximises revenue, i.e. price inelastic â€“ revenue rises when price of product increases; price elastic â€“ revenue rises when price of product falls ï‚· Helps a business to decide on its pricing policy, e.g., a product with a PED greater than 1; a change in the price of the product would result in a percentage change in the level of demand which is higher than the percentage change in price. This might lead a business towards competitive pricing ï‚· A business can use the knowledge that demand for their product is inelastic to know that an increase in the price would lose proportionately fewer customers than the price increase. Businesses that provide goods that customers view as essential know that an increase in price will not harm demand significantly Accept any other valid response. Clear explanation of two benefits to a business of knowing the price elasticity of demand of its products 3 marks Clear explanation of one benefit to a business of knowing the price elasticity of demand of its products or a partial explanation of two benefits 2 marks Partial explanation of one benefit or a list of two benefits 1 mark No creditable content 0 marks 3 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 12 of 20 Question Answer Marks

2(b) Explain two disadvantages to a business of having CSR as a business objective. Application 2 marks ï‚· Reference to the disadvantages of changing from a sole trader to a partnership business. Analysis 4 marks ï‚· Loss of ownership. The sole trader might have been the sole owner of their business for some time and this change would be difficult to take, knowing that they no longer fully own their business. ï‚· Shared decision-making. New partners might not agree with the approach of the original owner. Previously all decision-making was done by the sole owner of the business. Agreement would need to be reached with the new partner(s) which would take time. Any advantage of quick decision-making might be lost ï‚· Shared profit. Previously any profit made was for the sole owner but after taking in partners any profit would have to be shared between them ï‚· Responsible for the actions of the partner(s). The actions of one partner are binding on all other partners. If one partnerâ€™s actions result in a large debt, then all partners are liable for that debt whether they agreed with the action or not. Accept any other valid response. 8 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 12 of 18 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Farm Produce (FP) FP is a primary sector co-operative made up of six farms in country G. Each farm grows a range of fruit and vegetables. FP employs 26 workers across the farms and distribution centre. Each farm transports its fruit and vegetables to the distribution centre where they are packaged and sent to customersâ€™ homes. Table 1.1 contains data about the farming industry in country G. Table 1.1: Data about the farming industry in country G â€¢ Farms producing fruit and vegetables are given an annual government grant. â€¢ Most farms are labour intensive. â€¢ The government promotes the importance of eating fresh fruit and vegetables. â€¢ Most farms are small family businesses. â€¢ Minimum wage for farm workers will increase by 10% next year. FPâ€™s customers pay for a box of seasonal fruit and vegetables that is delivered each week. Data about the different box sizes sold by FP is shown in Table 1.2. It believes it should stop selling this product. (a) (i) Define the term â€˜labour intensiveâ€™ (line 8). [2] (ii) Explain the term â€˜co-operativeâ€™ (line 1). [3] (b) (i) Refer to Table 1.2. Calculate the profit made by FP in April 2022. [4] (ii) Explain one problem FP may have when deciding how to allocate fixed costs to each box size. Knowledge Marks A correct definition 2 A partial, vague or unfocused definition 1 No creditable content 0 Content: Production based on a high level of employee input compared with capital equipment. A correct definition will include the following points: ï‚· use of a high number of employees/manual/workers ï‚· as opposed to a high level of capital (no/limited machinery involved) Exemplar Marks Rationale Using a higher proportion of employees than capital. 2 Both elements covered When something is made by people instead of capital. 2 Both elements covered A hand made product. 1 One element Where more labour than capital is used. 1 One element â€“ â€˜labourâ€™ is a tautology. The intensity of the labour used. 0 A tautology. ARA 2 9609/22 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 7 of 30 Question Answer Marks

1(a)(ii) Explain the term â€˜co-operativeâ€™ (line 1). Award one mark for each point of explanation: C Example or some other way of showing good understanding, e.g. bulk buying, common in agriculture, motivational, slow decision-making, allows the achievement of economies of scale, mutual benefit, types of co-operative (worker and customer) etc. 1 mark B Knowledge of the operation of a co-operative: ï‚· Responsibilities are shared ï‚· Owners may contribute to the running/operation/control of the business ï‚· Shared decision-making ï‚· All members have one vote ï‚· Profits are shared 1 mark A Knowledge of the ownership of a co-operative: ï‚· Owned / financed by workers / customers / members 1 mark Note: Using the case as an example is repetition and not rewardable as the C mark A and B must be awarded before C mark can be given. Content: A co-operative is a business structure where the workers are the owners. The profits are shared with workers. Workers (owners) do not have to manage the business: they may vote for Directors. In smaller co-operatives, such as worker co-operatives, workers may also manage the business. Exemplar Mark Rationale Members (A) own a co-operative and share the running (B) of the business. Co-operatives can be slow when making decisions (C). 3 A, B and C A co-operative can be owned by customers or workers (A) who will each have a vote (B). Because they own it, they are motivated for it to do well (C). 3 A, B and C A co-operative has shared ownership by workers (A) and responsibilities (B) 2 A and B 3 9609/22 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2022 Page 8 of 30 Question Answer Marks

Each person has a vote, and the decision-making is shared. 1 Three points but all from B, so only one mark. question_text: Drone Development (DD) DD produces a range of drones, which are unmanned aircraft flown by remote control. These drones are used to help farmers identify whether their fields have enough water. They can also help farmers to see how fast crops are growing on large farms. The drones are individually designed using Computer Aided Design (CAD). DDâ€™s mission statement is: â€˜To be the leading company in drone technologyâ€™. DDâ€™s employees are skilled and highly motivated. This has enabled DDâ€™s managers to use a laissez-faire style of leadership. Secondary research indicates that these drones can be used for taking aerial photographs and videos of sporting and other events, such as cycle races and outdoor parties. The drones will send photographs and videos directly to a userâ€™s smartphone. question_text: Friendly Fit (FF) FF is a public limited company that owns a popular social media and fitness app. The app was launched five years ago and now has over three million users worldwide. The app allows users to upload and share pictures and short films of their favourite exercises. There are many other features which have been developed as the app has gained popularity. The app can be downloaded for free and users can access basic features without paying any money. However, to access all the features of the app, customers must pay a monthly fee of $10. FF also receives income from advertising revenue. Table 1.1 shows cost and revenue data for the app for the last two years. Knowledge Definition of network analysis: this is part of project management to ensure that resources are used efficiently. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Great Resources (GR) GR is a business partnership that creates educational resources. It sells direct to schools and teachers via its own website. Sanjay, Rukmal and Boris are entrepreneurial teachers who formed the GR partnership. One year ago, they identified a gap in the market to supply interactive, digital resources. GRâ€™s website is subscription only. An online marketing campaign, which used penetration pricing, attracted 250 subscribers in the first six months of operation. The start-up costs were financed with a $5000 bank overdraft, which is GRâ€™s only debt. Reviews for GRâ€™s products in teaching journals are positive but cash flow is poor. Many customers have taken advantage of a recent sales promotion for one monthâ€™s free membership and posted positive reviews. Unfortunately, few have then taken out a regular subscription. As revenue has not increased as much as the entrepreneurs had hoped, they must now consider alternative promotion methods. They have researched possible promotion methods and decided to advertise in an educational newspaper. The newspaper has a readership of half a million people. Expert Materials (EM) is a large national company that also advertises in the newspaper. EM is GRâ€™s closest competitor. The EM brand is well-known and trusted in the educational resources market. Table 1.1 shows some marketing data. Table 1. question_text: Office Furniture Designs (OFD) Markus is an entrepreneur and is planning to set up a private limited company called OFD in four weeksâ€™ time. OFD will be an online retailer selling office furniture directly to business customers. Products will be sent directly to customers from OFDâ€™s warehouse. OFDâ€™s product range will include office chairs, desks and filing cabinets. Markus will have limited start-up funds. OFD will operate in a competitive market and Markus is keen to offer high-quality customer service. He also will have to think carefully about opportunity cost. As part of his business planning, Markus has prepared a cash flow forecast (see Table 1.1). Table 1. This is a new and expanding market in country J, targeting the urban youth market. MXBâ€™s business objectives are reviewed regularly by Rani, the Managing Director. Rani is concerned that MXBâ€™s sales of mountain bikes have declined by 12% over the past year. Demand for mountain bikes in country J has remained static. 1(a)(ii) Exemplar and annotations Mark Rationale The next best alternative that has been given up . For example, if Markus chooses to spend money on customer services and not marketing then the opportunity cost would have been the loss of the marketing benefits . 3 An answer which starts with the knowledge and then applies it to Markus. The next best thing forgone when making a decision . A business could choose to buy some desks , but they would miss out on being able to buy chairs . 3 Again, knowledge first, followed by application to a decision (choose to buy some desks) and to the lost benefit (not being able to buy chairs). If a business has Â£100 and they choose to buy inventory instead of fuel , then the opportunity cost is the lost benefit of the fuel because it has been lost . 3 A different way of answering the question. There is application of both elements of opportunity cost (the choice and the lost benefit) as well as clear knowledge of the concept by the end of the response. The next best opportunity forgone . For example, when you choose to buy a chocolate bar . 2 Clear knowledge. Some application of a choice, but not the lost benefit. What you miss out on when a decision is made . 1 Not a perfect definition, but enough to award the knowledge mark. No attempt to apply. Indicative content AO1 Knowledge and understanding. Just-in-time production minimises the time, labour, and materials in a manufacturing process . question_text: Define the term capital intensive . [2] (b) Explain one benefit to a business of labour intensive operations. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term capital intensive . Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding â€¢ involves a high quantity / proportion of machinery / equipment / technology compared with labour input / other resources. Partial understanding â€¢ uses machinery / equipment / technology (1) â€¢ involves a high quantity of machinery / equipment / technology (1) Accept all valid responses. 2

2(b) Explain one benefit to a business of labour intensive operations. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Benefits of labour intensive operations include: â€¢ good quality / customer service. â€¢ can be inexpensive. â€¢ assists job production / one off items. â€¢ skilled / flexible employees. â€¢ motivated workforce. â€¢ good for small businesses. 3 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 13 of 30 Question Answer Marks

2(b) AO2 Application â€¢ used in personal services e.g. hairdressing, where good customer interaction and service is needed. â€¢ can be better at meeting customer needs and wants. â€¢ in some countries human labour is relatively inexpensive, so costs can be minimised. â€¢ no costly investment in machinery or maintenance / repair costs. â€¢ better suited to job production where specialised / individually designed / one â€“ off products are made. â€¢ labour intensive production can be very flexible, using similar skills for many personalised / hand â€“ crafted products. â€¢ small business may not need high levels of production so labour intensive production is better than having large fixed costs. Accept all valid responses. 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 14 of 30 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term margin of safety . [2] (b) Explain one way a business can decrease its break-even level of output. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
3(a) Define the term margin of safety . Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding â€¢ the amount / quantity by which a firmâ€™s current level of output / sales / revenue exceeds the break â€“ even level of output / sales / revenue. â€¢ current level of output / sales / revenue âˆ’ break â€“ even output / sales / revenue. â€¢ difference between current output / sales / revenue and break - even output / sales / revenue â€¢ margin of safety = e current level of output / sales / revenue â€“ break-even output current level o / f output s e / s e ale r s n / s l r v e / v e e u enu a Ã— 100 â€¢ correct diagram with margin of safety labelled (2) Partial understanding â€¢ break - even output / sales / revenue (1) â€¢ current level of output / sales / revenue (1) â€¢ above break - even point (1) â€¢ partially correct diagram with margin of safety shown (1) Accept all valid responses. 2 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 15 of 30 Question Answer Marks

3(b) Explain one way a business can decrease its break â€“ even level of output. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding â€¢ Ways to decrease break â€“ even level of output include: â€¢ increase the price per unit. â€¢ reduce fixed costs of business. â€¢ reduce variable costs per unit. â€¢ increase contribution per unit. AO2 Application â€¢ increases revenue per unit, especially if demand is price inelastic â€“ shown as revenue line being steeper. â€¢ can add value and / or increase promotion to increase price. â€¢ cheaper fixed costs e.g. premises / utilities â€“ reduces total costs â€“ shown by fixed costs line being lower. â€¢ variable costs reduced e.g. cheaper inputs / components / materials / backward vertical integration â€“ reduces total costs â€“ shown as variable costs line being less steep. â€¢ the break â€“ even point is achieved with less units produced. â€¢ relevant calculation(s). â€¢ relevant diagram(s). Accept all valid responses. 3 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 16 of 30 Question Answer Marks 4 Analyse one way that a business may be affected by a dynamic business environment. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Effects of a dynamic business environment include: â€¢ increased / decreased demand. â€¢ increased / decreased costs. â€¢ increased / decreased revenue. â€¢ increased / decreased profit. â€¢ increased / decreased competition. â€¢ increased / decreased market share. â€¢ increased uncertainty / instability. AO2 Application â€¢ changing customer needs for products. â€¢ technology advances / production possibilities. â€¢ government policy e.g. treatment of employees, type of product, ethical behaviour. â€¢ competitor actions e.g. pricing / promotion / product development. â€¢ external environment â€“ inflation, interest rates. â€¢ difficult to forecast future inventory needs. â€¢ must change and adapt. 5 9609 / 11 Cambridge Internation

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Analyse two advantages to a business of mass marketing. [8] (b) Evaluate whether primary sector businesses should use product differentiation to increase sales. [12] OR
mark_scheme_text:
5 (a) Analyse two advantages to a business of mass marketing. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Advantages of mass marketing include: â€¢ reduces risk. â€¢ large customer base. â€¢ little price discrimination. â€¢ no need for product differentiation. â€¢ little market research. â€¢ no targeted advertising. â€¢ brand easily recognised. â€¢ can use flow production methods. â€¢ economies of scale. AO2 Application â€¢ one product or a limited range is supplied to the whole market e.g. rice. â€¢ little or no differentiation e.g. bottled water. â€¢ example e.g. Coca â€“ Cola with large customer base. 8 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 19 of 30 Question Answer Marks

5(a) AO3 Analysis â€¢ reduces the risk of failure if product is guaranteed to remain necessary / popular â€“ more chance of business success. â€¢ large number of customers wanting a standardised product at low price so no need to consider different pricing methods. â€¢ mass market means high total sales and high revenue even with a lower price. â€¢ gain cost leadership over smaller businesses and can set lower prices. â€¢ usually leads to high profits even if profit margins are low. â€¢ little product development reduces spending on R and D. â€¢ requires little market research if market is stable â€“ saves money and time. â€¢ advertising is not targeted so can use expensive but wide â€“ coverage methods â€“ gives marketing economies of scale. â€¢ strong brand creates barrier to entry for smaller businesses and keeps market share high / allows high prices. â€¢ flow production usually capital intensive which can be cheaper in long run than labour intensive operations. â€¢ high volumes of production lead to lower unit costs due economies of scale â€“ this leads to a lower price for consumers. Accept all valid responses. 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 20 of 30 Question Answer Marks

5(b) Evaluate whether primary sector businesses should use product differentiation to increase sales. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding â€¢ product differentiation is making a product distinctive so that it stands out from competitorsâ€™ products in consumersâ€™ perception. â€¢ product differentiation could be achieved by branding, product design, ease of use, USP. Do not reward selling different products e.g. wheat and tomatoes. AO2 Application â€¢ primary sector business is one which extracts natural resources / raw materials. â€¢ primary sector products are used and processed by other firms e.g. strawberries / milk to yoghurt manufacturer. â€¢ primary sector business examples could be fishing, mining, farming, oil extraction, logging businesses. â€¢ primary sector products could be coffee, wheat, coal, wood. 12 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 21 of 30 Question Answer Marks

5(b) AO3 Analysis â€¢ primary sector prod

6(a) Analyse two ways a business could use employee participation in the management of business activity. AO2 Application â€¢ examples of use of type(s) of employee participation in business contexts â€“ meetings, quality circles, work councils, worker directors, autonomous work groups, laissez faire / democratic management. â€¢ might not name specific methods of participation but describe how they work. 8 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 24 of 30 Question Answer Marks

Will seek actions to benefit employees and prolong decision â€“ making. question_text: October/November 2023 1 hour 15 minutes You must answer on the enclosed answer booklet.
