Define the term job description . [2] (b) Explain one advantage to an employer of using a person specification. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term job description. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding (2 marks) â€¢ A statement/document outlining/defining the job profile â€“ key tasks, responsibilities, duties of a job, pay, hours of work Partial understanding (1 mark) â€¢ General statement about the work involved in a job Accept all valid responses. 2 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 9 of 24 Question Answer Marks

1(b) Explain one advantage to an employer of using a person specification Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding A person specification is a profile of an ideal candidate that a business wants to interview for a job. It sets out the exact skills and knowledge needed to perform the job such as education, qualifications. experience, skills, training Advantages to an employer include: â€¢ Makes the interview process efficient â€“ recruits applicants that meet the requirements of the job â€¢ Prevents unsuitable applicants applying for a job â€¢ Defines the personable qualities/qualifications required of an applicant â€¢ Ensures equality of treatment of applicants â€¢ Presents important information to applicants â€¢ Used to assess applicants and select for a specific job â€¢ Assists shortlisting of applicants Advantages to an employer of a person specification may include: â€¢ It streamlines the interview process â€“ job seekers are matched to the same job profile and matched against certain requirements â€¢ It clarifies the two types of personal qualifications important to the employer â€“ essential and desirable â€¢ Helps to remove bias and discrimination â€“ supports equality of treatment â€¢ Saves time in the recruitment process â€“ gives clear information of candidatesâ€™ qualifications, skills, experience to perform the job â€¢ Allows employer to profile the ideal person to fill the job â€“ and use it to assess candidates â€¢ Provides a basis for shortlisting applicants â€“ reduces the number of applicants to be interviewed to manageable proportions. Accept all valid responses. 3 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 10 of 24 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term business enterprise . [2] (b) Explain one reason why a business might fail. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term business enterprise Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Business enterprise is often defined as someone who spots a business opportunity and sets up a business â€“ an entrepreneur Business enterprise is also defined as any business activity that makes/sells products that make profits and contributes to economic development â€“ job creation, GNP in a city or country â€“ both interpretations are acceptable Clear understanding (2 marks) â€¢ Risk taking ability that brings together all factors of production to set up a business â€¢ Entrepreneur activity that sets up businesses to make profits/ products/sells to customers â€¢ Any organisation that sells products to satisfy customers â€¢ The skill and ability to start up/manage a business â€¢ A business with the aim of making profit rather than meeting social objectives Partial understanding (1 mark) â€¢ One of the factors of production â€¢ A business that makes profits â€¢ A partnership or limited company/an organisation â€¢ An entrepreneur Accept all valid responses 2 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 11 of 24 Question Answer Marks

2(b) Explain one reason why a business might fail Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Business failure is when a business ceases to operate â€¢ Lack of cash â€¢ Insufficient working capital â€¢ Poor business model â€¢ Poor managers â€¢ Competition â€¢ Too few customers â€¢ Poor marketing â€¢ Business inflexible. AO2 Application Reasons for business failure may include: â€¢ Lack of financial control â€“ cash flow forecasts are inadequate or not used â€“ unable to negotiate cash shortages â€¢ Poor cash flow management â€“ insufficient working capital to survive â€¢ Business model is defective â€“ demand for niche product falls â€“ no alternative product â€¢ Management and leadership inadequate â€“ lack key skills and experience to make appropriate decisions â€¢ Competition too strong â€“ business fails to respond to aggressive competition - offer on price or quality is inferior to competition â€¢ Failure to attract and retain customers â€“ may be dependent on a few big customers, or a limited niche market segment â€¢ Marketing is weak â€“ poor market research at outset â€“ marketing is under resourced â€¢ Economic, legal, social, market changes â€“ business unable to respond â€“ lacks agility and flexibility Accept all valid responses. 3 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 12 of 24 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term bankruptcy . [2] (b) Explain one advantage of using retained earnings as a source of finance. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
3(a) Define the term bankruptcy Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding (2 marks) â€¢ A legal procedure when a business is unable to repay outstanding debts/loans Partial understanding (1 mark) â€¢ A business runs out of cash/is insolvent / no financial capital to operate /assets used to pay creditors Accept all valid responses. 2 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 13 of 24 Question Answer Marks

3(b) Explain one advantage of using retained earnings as a source of finance Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Retained earnings are the part of business profits not distributed to shareholders as dividends but instead reserved for re - investment back into the business. â€¢ Inexpensive way of raising finance â€¢ Not repaid â€¢ No interest â€¢ Internal source of finance â€¢ Flexible way of raising finance â€¢ Deal with unpredictable situations â€¢ Not using the ownerâ€™s capital â€¢ No loss of control â€¢ Available/sufficient finance â€¢ Instant increase in liquidity AO2 Application Advantages of using retained earnings as a source of finance may include: â€¢ Retained earnings may be inexpensive â€“ compared to other sources of finance (though not free) there is an opportunity cost for shareholders â€¢ Retained earnings provide flexibility â€“ business has complete control as to how they are used â€¢ They do not dilute the ownership of the business â€“ unlike share issues or venture capital (in shares or loans) â€¢ Readily available â€“ when other sources of finance may not be available â€“ banks and investors may be unwilling â€¢ Increases liquidity â€“ provides liquid assets â€“ funds for emergencies â€“ gives stability to a business â€¢ Can be used to stimulate growth Accept all valid responses. 3 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 14 of 24 Question Answer Marks 4 Analyse one way a business might improve the efficiency of its operations. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Efficiency is the ratio between an output gained from an input during business operations â€“ seeking to produce a product with minimum resources â€“ ways to improve efficiency of operations may include: â€¢ Improve production process â€¢ More capital investment â€¢ Motivation of employees â€¢ Waste reduction measures â€¢ Improve productivity of employees â€¢ Cheaper supplies â€¢ More efficient inventory â€¢ More efficient HRM/skilled managers â€¢ Better training of employees â€¢ Workforce planning AO2 Application â€¢ Business aims to have cost - efficient operations â€“ reducing waste while retaining quality and service â€¢ Business can improve efficiency â€“ with more cost â€“ efficient operations, â€“ use new and better equipment, waste reduction, use more machines, training for workers, worker involvement, review inventory cost. AO3 Analysis â€¢ Invest in new or improve machines/technology â€¢ Cost efficient operation

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Analyse two methods that a business could use to help employees satisfy their self- actualisation needs, as defined by Maslow. [8] (b) Evaluate whether an ability to motivate others is the most important role of a hotel manager. [12] OR
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse two methods that a business could use to help employees satisfy their self - actualisation needs, as defined by Maslow Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Maslowâ€™s hierarchy of needs is a model of human motivation. The final fifth stage of the Maslow hierarchy is self - actualisation needs. If these needs are met at work the employee can realise full potential â€“ a higher order need (other 4 are physical, safety, belonging, and esteem needs) Self - actualisation needs are related to an employee need for self - growth, creativity and advancement and looks to a business to satisfy these needs â€¢ Businesses can create appropriate working environments for self - actualisation â€¢ New job roles and responsibilities â€¢ Involve employees in decision - making AO2 Application Business can employ methods to support employees to achieve Maslow higher order needs including: â€¢ Opportunities to be an intrapreneur â€“ Method to meet creative needs â€¢ Training/self - development â€“ methods to meet career progression needs â€¢ Job enrichment/enlargement â€“ a method to meet responsibility needs â€¢ More management responsibilities â€“ method to meet leadership needs â€¢ Promotion opportunities â€“ method to meet accomplishment needs AO3 Analysis â€¢ Business can provide creative work â€“ creative work projects that allow innovation and will inspire employees â€¢ Business can provide challenging work â€“ that demonstrates the skills of employees and stretches them â€“ and provides progression/promotion opportunities â€¢ Business can increase responsibility of employees â€“ provide project leadership opportunities â€¢ Business can place employees in positions of leadership â€“ responsibility and accountability that gives them increased self - worth and allows them to operate at a senior management level â€¢ Business can create tasks/projects that allow employees to accomplish significant tasks â€“ lots of delegation and minimal interference â€¢ Business can ensure employees benefit from satisfying the other 4 Maslow needs and create a platform for self - actualisation with opportunities to work in teams. Accept all valid responses. 8 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 16 of 24 Question Answer Marks

6(a) Analyse two reasons why there should be a close link between the marketing objectives of a business and its corporate objectives Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Marketing objectives are objectives set by a business and its marketing department to engage in marketing activities that will support corporate objectives. Specific marketing activities include increasing product awareness, strengthen customer relationships, and strengthen the business brand â€“ activities that would normally be supportive of corporate goals â€¢ To stop any conflict that might arise between corporate and marketing objectives â€¢ Ensure that there are appropriate strategies and tactics for the business â€¢ Will ensure that corporate objectives are more likely to be achieved â€¢ Marketing objectives and activities can make a significant contribution only if they are closely linked to the corporate objectives. â€¢ Harder to achieve corporate objectives without marketing objectives. â€¢ Would waste resources AO2 Application â€¢ Conflict might arise when the marketing department decides to follow its own objectives , e.g., lower prices when dynamic pricing is needed â€¢ Corporate objectives require all departments, particularly marketing department, to pursue supportive objectives. To increase market share the marketing department could increase sales through a social media advertising campaign â€¢ If marketing objective is domestic growth and corporate objective is expanding into overseas markets â€¢ Increased spending on irrelevant/unnecessary marketing activities â€“ spending money on market research when corporate objective is minimising costs 8 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 19 of 24 Question Answer Marks

6(a) AO3 Analysis Reasons for close links between marketing and corporate objectives â€¢ If marketing plans for new products â€“ involves research and development expenditure â€“ must have approval of corporate management and support the corporate objective on the product portfolio â€¢ If marketing decides that market share of some products should be increased â€“ using product life cycle approach â€“ should not conflict with stated corporate intent as stated in the latest corporate plan â€¢ A focus on the existing brand by marketing â€“ should align with latest mission statement of the business â€¢ Targeting new overseas customers â€“ could conflict with corporate goals to expand the business which may seek to concentrate on the domestic market â€¢ Marketing proposals to strengthen/change the customer relationship â€“ may conflict with corporate pans to explore a new customer relationship model â€¢ Marketing initiatives to increase sales and revenue â€“ with more aggressive advertising and promotion may conflict with a new corporate proposal to strengthen the existing corporate social responsibility approach â€¢ More use of online marketing activities may not be in the objectives and plans of corporate manage

1(b) Explain one reason a business may choose to remain small Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Reasons why a business may remain small include: â€¢ Personal choice of business owner â€¢ Unwilling to take risks of growth â€¢ Lack of finance â€¢ Unable/unwilling to meet costs of growth â€¢ Unwilling to lose control â€¢ Concern for quality â€¢ Lack ambition â€¢ Unwilling to accept increases management responsibility â€¢ Determined to remain flexible â€¢ Committed to the small business model â€¢ Lack of demand â€¢ Form better relationships with employees/customers. 3 9609 / 13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 9 of 24 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Define the term curriculum vitae . [2] (b) Explain one benefit to an employee of having an employment contract. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term curriculum vitae. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding (2 marks) â€¢ A curriculum vitae (CV) is a document of a personâ€™s education, qualifications, and previous occupations/experience Partial understanding (1 mark) â€¢ A CV is a record of a personâ€™s achievements / used for a job application. Accept all valid responses. 2

2(b) Explain one benefit to an employee of having an employment contract. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding â€¢ Employees know exactly what is required of them as employees â€¢ Rights and responsibilities are clearly set out â€¢ Pay and benefits are made explicit â€¢ Protection from arbitrary action by employers â€¢ Provides certainty AO2 Application â€¢ Employees have a clear statement of what is required of them in a period of employment â€” there is no ambiguity â€“ the employment status of an employee is clear â€¢ Provides job security to an employee â€¢ A clear statement of conditions of employment â€“ that an employer must honour â€“ employee has written protection of rights â€¢ Gives clarity about remuneration and benefits â€“ protects employees at times of conflict and disputes with employers â€¢ Benefits relating to sick pay, redundancy, and hours of work are among the basic rights set out in the contract â€“ these and other basic rights protected from actions of rogue employers â€¢ Prevents unauthorised deductions by the employer â€“ employee protected from arbitrary employer action â€¢ Contract provides consistency and stability for an employee â€“ consultation is normally required before any changes can be made in the contract Accept all valid responses. 3 9609 / 13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 11 of 24 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Analyse two qualities an intrapreneur should possess to be successful. [8] (b) â€˜Ethics should significantly influence the marketing activities of all retail clothing businesses.â€™ Evaluate this view. [12] OR
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse two qualities an intrapreneur should possess to be successful. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Intrapreneur is an employee tasked with developing an innovative idea or project within a business Someone who behaves like an entrepreneur within a business but does not face the same risks or reap the same rewards Qualities of an intrapreneur include: â€¢ Innovator â€¢ Risk taker â€¢ Creative thinker â€¢ Problem solver â€¢ Project leader â€“ matrix organisation â€¢ Lateral thinker â€¢ Politically astute/networker â€¢ Ability to communicate ideas â€¢ Ability to convince others to accept ideas â€¢ Determination/perseverance AO2 Application â€¢ Innovator and risk taker â€“ challenges the status quo â€¢ Creative thinker â€“ thinks new and big ideas â€¢ Problem solver â€“ enjoys seeking new solutions â€¢ Passionate and hard working â€“ inspires/motivates others â€¢ Lateral and holistic thinker â€“ thinks outside of the box â€¢ Politically adept â€“ forms coalitions/multi â€“ functional teams AO3 Analysis â€¢ Intrapreneur acquires knowledge of the internal and external business environment â€“ uses it to remove opposition and blocks to innovation and change â€¢ Uses knowledge and experience to set out a vision for an idea/project â€“ challenges the current way of doing things â€¢ Sees problems as opportunities â€“ willing to take risks and make mistakes â€¢ Inspires others to think as an intrapreneur/entrepreneur â€“ sets an example of energy, enthusiasm â€¢ Builds and leads cross â€“ functional teams â€“ more ideas and perceptions contributed â€¢ Skilled in internal politics â€“ ignores or overcomes opposition â€“ seeks and gets support from senior management â€” builds coalitions â€¢ Always seeking ways to refresh business activity â€“ seeks transformational change not marginal change Accept all valid responses. 8 9609 / 13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 14 of 24 Question Answer Marks

5(b) `Ethics should significantly influence the marketing activities of all retail clothing businesses`. Evaluate this view. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding â€¢ Ethics is an approach to business activity that incorporates a set of moral principles including a clear understanding of what is right and what is wrong, and a clear commitment to keep to that distinction in all business decisions and all business behaviour â€¢ Marketing activities are those that aim to bring customers and business together so that customers are attracted to purchase products and so contribute to the revenue and profits of the business. marketing activities include all those from market research to the packaging of a product â€“ over all elements of the marketing mix â€¢ Retail clothing business will use marketing activities to persuade the customer to purchase their brands and will mount campaigns to inform and convince customers of the value of the retail offer 12 9609 / 13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED

6(a) Analyse two ways in which operations contributes to added value in a business. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding â€¢ Operations is the harvesting of value from the assets owned by a business â€“ the concept can refer to the production and transformation process in a business or in a broader sense to all the operations in a business â€¢ Added value is the difference between the price of a finished good and the cost of the inputs in making it â€“ added value â€“ can be achieved in different ways â€“ building a car in an innovative way or building the brand of a business The ways operations contributes to added value in a business include: â€¢ To satisfy customers â€¢ Produce quality goods â€¢ Ensure production process is efficient â€¢ Ensure supply chain is efficient â€¢ Ensure all functional business areas contribute AO2 Application â€¢ The goal of operations is to add value â€“ essentially be strengthening the relationship between the organisation and its customers â€“ the aim of all the functional areas in a business â€¢ Main aim is to ensure the business produces quality products that can satisfy the needs of customers on a regular basis â€“ the production process is involved as is marketing and human resources â€¢ Production process in a manufacturing business â€“ converting raw materials into a cell phone â€¢ Marketing operations â€“ promote the quality of a product and the customer prepared to pay the price â€¢ Human resources â€“ the right employees are hired, trained, and equipped to add value with front line superb service in a service business 8 9609 / 13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 19 of 24 Question Answer Marks

6(a) AO3 Analysis â€¢ Manufacturing business adds value through its production process â€“ raw materials into finished products â€“ sources quality materials from suppliers â€“ new cars come off the production line assembled by robots â€¢ Production takes copper, plastic, lithium â€“ converts into smart phones â€“ software specialists increase functionality with up â€“ grades â€¢ Marketing operations â€“ promote the business reputation for quality and establish a USP to differentiate and add value â€¢ Marketing operations â€“ improve the supply chain to deliver excellent delivery systems to attract customers â€¢ Inventory operations â€“ focus on appropriate order and storage system to make the transformation system more efficient â€¢ Lean management / right first time efficiency systems â€“ used to reduce waste and add value Accept all valid responses

question_text: Plasshape (PS) PS manufactures plastic packaging for industrial markets (B2B). Its customers include food manufacturers and cosmetics firms. PS has 500 employees in each of two countries with a similar factory in each country. Employee payment methods and nonâ€‘financial motivators vary in each factory. In country Z, PS uses timeâ€‘based payment methods, offers employee development and uses many nonâ€‘financial motivators. In country V, PS uses piece rate payment methods, but there are fewer opportunities for employee development and nonâ€‘financial motivators are not used. Some employees are demanding equal pay and opportunities for employee development in both PS factories. Technology is changing rapidly in the packaging industry. PSâ€™s research and development (R&D) team has developed an environmentally friendly type of food packaging. However, this costs more to make and it will need investment in new machinery. This unique new packaging product is expected to increase added value. Table 1.1 shows a summary of PSâ€™s financial data if it does not launch the new packaging product. Table 1. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Gold Theme Park (GT) Gold Park was a mine where gold was extracted until 2012. Although there was still some gold in the mine, the direct costs of extracting it became too high. The area around Gold Park has become a tourist destination. There has recently been development of hotels and other accommodation. A new marina complex has also been built which attracts boat owners to use the local lakes and rivers. Kayo is an entrepreneur who purchased the mine in 2015. She converted the mine into GT, a theme park where customers can learn about gold mining and have fun. GT now includes a museum, guided underground tours of the mine and a roller coaster ride around the park. There is also a restaurant and gift shop. Kayo employs some of the people who previously worked in the gold mine to work as tour guides in the theme park. She also employs some younger workers, who require training, as customer service representatives. Kayo believes that developing intrapreneurship is important to the ongoing success of GT. Table 1.1 shows some data about GT over the past three years. Table 1. Data about GT 2020 2021 2022 Revenue ($m) 3.4 3.2 2. question_text: Jakeâ€™s Cakes (JC) Jake started baking cakes while at university. He baked cakes for friendsâ€™ birthdays and other celebrations. He posted pictures of his cakes on social media sites where they received very positive feedback and were widely shared. In 2021 Jake started JC, a small business, using his parentsâ€™ kitchen. JC bakes cakes and sells them online. Jake created a website where customers design and order unique cakes. Jake uses digital promotion as the main method of promoting his products. JC has only used retained earnings to invest in marketing and new equipment. In 2023, Jake made a cake for a celebrity who shared JCâ€™s social media posts with their social media followers. As a result of this an investor approached Jake. The investor offered to provide a $25 000 capital investment to help Jake grow the business. However, Jake does not want to take on an equal partner. Jake is considering a different plan for growth. His parents have agreed to let him convert their garage into his commercial kitchen. He has prepared a business plan and applied for a bank loan of $15 000 to finance this growth. He has produced a cash flow forecast for the first quarter of 2024. Define the term takeover . [2] (b) Explain one disadvantage to a business of external growth. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term takeover . Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding 2 marks ï‚· A takeover is when one business/investor buys / acquires / controls / absorbs / integrates another business, often by buying a majority stake in the target company. Partial understanding 1 mark ï‚· Purchasing a business Accept all valid responses. 2 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 11 of 30 Question Answer Marks

1(b) Explain one disadvantage to a business of external growth. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one disadvantage to a business of external growth ï‚· May require considerable additional expenditure ï‚· May lead to increased debt ï‚· May cause problems for existing management ï‚· Might lead to loss of control of core business ï‚· Disruptive to original business â€“ culture clash ï‚· Growth may be too rapid â€“ diseconomies of scale ï‚· May be more regulations/different management culture in a different market AO2 Application 2 marks for developed application/explanation of one disadvantage to a business of external growth 1 mark for limited application/explanation of one disadvantage to a business of external growth ï‚· Can be very expensive to take over another business â€“ which could raise business costs. ï‚· Managers may lack the experience to deal with a different business â€“ which could lead to poor decision making and inefficiency. ï‚· Can lead to loss of control of the business â€“ especially if it is a hostile takeover. ï‚· Can lead to culture clashes â€“ whereby employees may find it difficult to adapt and leave. ï‚· Different management styles may be incompatible â€“ which can make decision making difficult. ï‚· May need to adapt different marketing strategies â€“ which can be very expensive. ï‚· Can lead to an increase in long-term debt â€“ which can lead to a higher gearing ratio. ï‚· Can lead to regulatory problems â€“ whereby the government may prevent a merger or takeover from happening. Accept all valid responses. 3 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 12 of 30 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term job description . [2] (b) Explain one benefit to a business of internal recruitment. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term job description . Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding 2 marks ï‚· A document/list/explanation/statement/indication setting out the tasks/duties/features/role/responsibilities/requirements of a job NOT the qualifications/skills/attributes of a person doing a job. Partial understanding 1 mark ï‚· What the job involves/requires Accept all valid responses. 2 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 13 of 30 Question Answer Marks

2(b) Explain ONE benefit to a business of internal recruitment. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one benefit to a business of internal recruitment ï‚· Applicants well known to the business / know how the business operates ï‚· Applicants already integrated into working culture ï‚· Internal recruitment takes less time / more efficient ï‚· Internal recruitment likely cheaper than external recruitment ï‚· Acts as an incentive to employees in a business / increases motivation ï‚· May mean less disruption to existing employees AO2 Application 2 marks for developed explanation of one benefit to a business of internal recruitment 1 mark for limited explanation of one benefit to a business of internal recruitment ï‚· Applicants may already be known to the selection team â€“ so they know they would be able to do the job, therefore reduced risk. ï‚· Culture of the organisation would already be well understood by the applicants â€“ so they know what is expected of them â€“ reduced need for induction training or time to adjust. ï‚· It is often quicker than external recruitment/advertising â€“ so the business does not have to wait excessively long for the candidate to start contributing. ï‚· Likely to be cheaper than using recruitment agencies â€“ therefore saving costs ï‚· Gives internal staff a career structure and a chance to progress â€“ which might reduce labour turnover. ï‚· Reduces the likelihood that staff will have to adapt to the new recruitâ€™s style of management â€“ as they are already familiar with the person. A ccept all valid responses. 3 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 14 of 30 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term job production . [2] (b) Explain one benefit to a business of capital intensive operations. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
3(a) Define the term job production . Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding 2 marks ï‚· The production process where a one-off/unique/bespoke product is made/for a customer/product completed before the next one is started Partial understanding 1 mark ï‚· Where only one item is produced Accept all valid responses. 2 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 15 of 30 Question Answer Marks

3(b) Explain ONE benefit to a business of capital intensive operations. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding â€“ 1 mark for identifying one benefit to a business of capital intensive operations ï‚· May well lead to economies of scale ï‚· Machines may well guarantee greater conformity of quality ï‚· Machines more efficient than labour ï‚· Production costs reduced ï‚· Reduces need for labour resource ï‚· Increases output/productivity AO2 Application 2 marks for developed application/explanation of one benefit to a business of capital intensive operations 1 mark for limited application/explanation of one benefit to a business of capital intensive operations ï‚· Substantial opportunities for scale economies â€“ therefore unit cost reductions ï‚· Quality can be standardised so products are the same every time â€“ which reduces human errors and customer complaints ï‚· Machines can work continuously 24/7 â€“ therefore increased output enabling large orders to be met ï‚· Less employees are needed â€“ therefore reduced labour/wage costs ï‚· Higher productivity â€“ more effective utilisation of resources Accept all valid responses. 3 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 16 of 30 Question Answer Marks 4 Analyse one reason why product differentiation may be important to a business. Note: no marks can be awarded if a relevant reason why product differentiation may be important to a business has not been identified Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one reason why product differentiation may be important to a business ï‚· May give a product a USP/distinctive/unique feature ï‚· May increase customer demand for the product ï‚· May lead to an increase in market share ï‚· May strengthen the brand ï‚· Target niche market ï‚· Allows premium prices to be set ï‚· Increases morale/motivation of employees 5 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 17 of 30 Question Answer Marks 4 AO2 Application 2 marks for a developed application of one reason why product differentiation may be important to a business 1 mark for limited application of one reason why product differentiation may be important to a business ï‚· Increased competitiveness ï‚· A product is made distinctive so it stands out from competitorsâ€™ products

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Analyse two limitations to a business of using contribution costing. [8] (b) Evaluate whether working capital is the most important source of finance for a start-up furniture manufacturer. [12] OR
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse two limitations to a business of using contribution costing. There are 8 marks in total for Q5(a) â€“ 4 marks for each of the two limitations to a business of using contribution costing: 1 mark for 1 mark for 2 marks for Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one limitation to a business of using contribution costing ï‚· It allocates only direct costs to cost/profit centres ï‚· Overhead costs not apportioned at all ï‚· May provide a misleading picture of profitability ï‚· It overlooks the impact of fixed costs ï‚· Not acceptable for external financial reporting ï‚· Difficult to allocate costs accurately across a full product range ï‚· Not an appropriate method for price determination ï‚· Unsuitable for capital intensive operations/multi-product operations 8 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 19 of 30 Question Answer Marks

5(a) AO2 Application 1 mark for application of one limitation to a business of using contribution costing ï‚· Difficult to analyse as not all costs can be easily split into fixed and variable costs. ï‚· Poor method for setting prices â€“ as prices are based on contribution which does not cover fixed costs. ï‚· It violates the principle of matching costs with revenues ï‚· Has limited scope â€“ so businesses with high automation have high levels of fixed costs, so not suitable to capital intensive industries. ï‚· Can lead to unrealistic financial statements â€“ as exclusion of fixed overheads in valuation of stock. ï‚· Firms might find it difficult to cover all costs and earn a fair return on capital employed AO3 Analysis 2 marks for developed analysis of one limitation to a business of using contribution costing L2 1 mark for limited analysis of using contribution costing L1 ï‚· Not acceptable for stock valuation â€“ if stock is undervalued it could lead to government investigation/fines â€“ affect business image ï‚· Unrealistic financial statements â€“ could lead to investors withdrawing from the business or government fines. ï‚· Under-pricing of products â€“ which leads to poor return on capital employed and low profits â€“ shareholder dissatisfaction ï‚· Costs can be difficult to allocate â€“ which leads to inaccurate pricing decisions â€“ lose customer loyalty ï‚· Can lead managers to maintain the production of goods just because of positive contribution â€“ perhaps a brand new product should be launched instead which makes a greater contribution. ï‚· If lower prices are offered to gain customers as contribution can still be earned â€“ may lead to existing customers demanding lower prices â€“ lower profits. ï‚· If high prices are a key feature that has established brand â€“ then lowering prices could destroy brand image. Accept all valid responses. 9609/12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 20 of 30 Question Answer Marks

1(b) Explain one method of improving cash flow for a business. 1(b) ï‚· An overdraft is where a bank agrees to a business borrowing up to an agreed limit as and when required â€“ therefore increase cash inflow ï‚· A short-term loan allows a fixed amount to be borrowed for an agreed length of time â€“ which increases cash inflow ï‚· Sale of assets allows cash to be obtained from selling off redundant assets â€“ which will boost cash inflow ï‚· Sale and leaseback allows an asset to be sold then leased back â€“ increase cash inflow ï‚· Reduce credit terms to customers i.e. from 2 months to 1 month â€“ will enable cash to be brought into the business sooner ï‚· Debt factoring enables customersâ€™ bills to be bought â€“ means an immediate cash boost ï‚· Make more sales â€“ reduce price to encourage customers to buy more, more advertising ï‚· Delay payments to suppliers i.e. pay a bill after 3 months instead of 2 months â€“ cash outflows will fall in short term ï‚· Delay spending on capital equipment â€“ means money is kept in the business that might have gone out ï‚· Use leasing â€“ no large cash outlay is made â€“ so less money going out of the business ï‚· Cut overheads (that do not directly affect output) â€“ i.e. promotion costs â€“ so less money going out of the business ï‚· More motivated staff â€“ make less mistakes so less wastage of materials ï‚· Improved productivity â€“ higher output so potential for higher

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term added value . [2] (b) Explain one quality an entrepreneur needs for success. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term added value Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding 2 marks ï‚· The difference between the cost of purchasing raw materials and the price the finished goods are sold for. ï‚· Economic value added to an existing service. ï‚· Output worth more than input. Partial understanding 1 mark ï‚· The impact of a production process. Accept all valid responses. 2 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 14 of 34 Question Answer Marks

2(b) Explain one quality an entrepreneur needs for success. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one quality an entrepreneur needs for success Successful entrepreneurial qualities include: ï‚· Risk taking ï‚· Multi-skilled ï‚· Leadership skills ï‚· Self-confidence/ability to bounce back ï‚· Business knowledge ï‚· Good motivator ï‚· Resilience ï‚· Hard-working ï‚· Good manager ï‚· Creative/innovative ï‚· Good /effective communicator ï‚· Independent ï‚· Initiative ï‚· Intelligent ï‚· Networker ï‚· Optimistic ï‚· Organised ï‚· Resourceful ï‚· Results driven ï‚· Self-motivated / determined /focused/committed ï‚· Team player 3 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 15 of 34 Question Answer Marks

2(b) AO2 Application 2 marks for developed explanation of one quality an entrepreneur needs for success 1 mark for limited explanation of one quality an entrepreneur needs for success ï‚· Risk taking â€“ entrepreneurs must be willing to take risks to see results â€“ this can often be a financial risk. ï‚· Innovation â€“ means they can carve a niche in a market â€“ which will attract customers. ï‚· Commitment and self-motivation â€“ as it requires long hours and hard work to be successful. ï‚· Multi-skilled â€“ as they will have to promote and sell a product or service, manage the finances, deal with different customers, possibly hire new people. ï‚· Leadership â€“ has to lead by example â€“ and have a personality that encourages employees to follow them. ï‚· Self-confidence and ability to bounce back â€“ as many businesses fail â€“ so the strength not to be discouraged â€“ self- belief. ï‚· Creative thinking â€“ to identify gaps in the market. ï‚· Knowledgeable â€“ entrepreneurs will often have in-depth knowledge of a particular industry or market â€“ helps when dealing with customers. A ccept all valid responses. 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 16 of 34 Question Answer Marks

mark_scheme_text:
3(a) Define the term primary research. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Analyse two benefits to the workforce of McGregorâ€™s Theory X management. [8] (b) Evaluate whether cooperation between management and the workforce is the most important factor in the success of a car manufacturer. [12] OR
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse two benefits to the workforce of McGregorâ€™s theory X management. There are 8 marks in total for Q5(a) â€“ 4 marks for each of the two benefits to the workforce of McGregorâ€™s Theory X Management: 1 mark for 1 mark for 2 marks for Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one benefit to a workforce of McGregorâ€™s theory X management ï‚· Theory X takes the position that employees are reluctant to change and that they require significant external motivation from their managers to complete their work. ï‚· Employees dislike work and avoid it where possible. ï‚· Employees will avoid responsibility and are not creative ï‚· When staff are motivated only by money ï‚· Employees know exactly what is required of them ï‚· Some employees thrive under an authoritative management style ï‚· Employees can focus on achieving goal ï‚· Some employees may not have the skills ï‚· No room for ambiguity ï‚· Benefit new starters or people with lack of experience ï‚· Employees may not want to make decisions in a crisis ï‚· Close supervision can motivate some workers 8 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 21 of 34 Question Answer Marks

5(a) AO2 Application 1 mark for application/explanation of one benefit to a workforce of McGregorâ€™s theory X management ï‚· Can benefit employees who are motivated by money â€“ to achieve targets ï‚· Employees know exactly what is required of them â€“ so can complete work set ï‚· Some employees thrive under an authoritative management style â€“ perhaps if new to a job ï‚· Employees can focus on achieving goal â€“ so they achieve it ï‚· Some employees may not have the skills - to take on more responsibility ï‚· No room for ambiguity â€“ as roles are clear ï‚· Can benefit new starters or people with lack of experience â€“ who need a lot of guidance ï‚· Employees may not want to make decisions in a crisis â€“ and can rely on the manager to do this ï‚· Close supervision can motivate some workers to work more quickly â€“ as they may naturally be lazy AO3 Analysis 2 marks for developed analysis of one benefit to a workforce of McGregorâ€™s theory X management L2 1 mark for limited analysis of one benefit to a workforce of McGregorâ€™s theory X management L1 ï‚· If employees are motivated by money â€“ theory X may enable targets to be met and subsequent financial rewards. ï‚· Lazy employees are more likely to be kept on track â€“ so less likely to face disciplinary/performance issues ï‚· Removes the stress of having to make decisions (in a crisis) â€“ so better able to cope at work ï‚· Being able to complete work set â€“ means they meet targets set by managers ï‚· Newer employers are able to settle into job faster â€“ might not leave ï‚· Not given additional responsibility â€“ so donâ€™t feel overburdened Accept all valid responses. 9609/13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 22 of 34 Q

question_text: Samiraâ€™s Whiteboards (SW) Samira left school at the age of 18 in country H. She had a small amount of savings and an idea to create a flexible, removable and reusable whiteboard. Samira created a prototype and received small orders from local retailers. A local manufacturer batch produces stock when required. There is a two-week lead time for a minimum order of 500 units. Samira started to sell her whiteboards on her website and at trade shows. She has been trading for seven months. She has good cashflow but little working capital as production costs are high. Samira has heard that OS, a large business that sells office equipment, is planning to sell their own version of Samiraâ€™s whiteboard. She is keen to increase production quickly to take advantage of being first to market. However, Samiraâ€™s manufacturer cannot supply enough product to meet the growth in potential demand for whiteboards. SW Inventory control chart 2023 Lara, a venture capitalist with experience of manufacturing, has approached Samira about making an investment. She would invest $100 000 to build a local mass production facility for SW. The facility would have a maximum output of 50 000 units per month. Lara wants to own 40% of the business. question_text: Analyse two limitations to a business of using a business plan. [8] (b) â€˜A chocolate manufacturer should use external methods of growth to successfully achieve its objectives.â€™ Evaluate this view. [12] OR

There are 8 marks in total for Q5(a) â€“ 4 marks for each of the two reasons given : 1 mark for 1 mark for 2 marks for Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for giving one limitation to a business of using a business plan â€¢ plan is often set up before the business starts to run â€¢ plan may not be accurate/may be biased â€¢ plan may be unrealistic â€¢ business plan for a large business can be a very complex document â€¢ plans quickly become out of date/are short term â€¢ may cause inflexibility/over - reliance â€¢ reduce innovation â€¢ reveals future strategies to competitors â€¢ does not guarantee success AO2 Application 1 mark for application of one limitation â€¢ must be based on market research but no past experience of running this business â€¢ an entrepreneur may not have business knowledge, just an idea based on a hobby â€¢ unrealistic expectations puts pressure on employees 8 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 19 of 29 Question Answer Marks

5(a) â€¢ a lot of time and effort is taken to produce a detailed business plan â€¢ external and internal circumstances may not be predictable and can change rapidly â€¢ owner may prefer to follow the plan rather than reacting to external influences and ideas â€¢ plan may be critiqued by other businesses AO3 Analysis 2 marks for developed analysis of one limitation L2 1 mark for limited analysis of one limitation L1 â€¢ for a start - up business the plan might be weak due to inexperience or over - optimism of entrepreneur so will not help the business to make decisions â€¢ to be useful the plan must have accurate forecasts, the right actions must be planned and effectively implemented in the given circumstances, otherwise it is a waste of time for the business to produce â€¢ without being based on reliable research and experience in this type of business the plan may be inaccurate and will not help the business to survive or develop â€¢ if goals are not achieved employees are discouraged and motivation is reduced â€¢ an employee, probably a manager, will have to be allocated the role of producing the plan, which takes them away from other aspects of their job role, so the business may be less efficient â€¢ the business plan needs to be constantly updated if it is to be an effective working document which takes further time/effort â€¢ lack of quick responses causes missed opportunities and lack of competitiveness â€¢ simply having a plan does not guarantee success/must be good and carried out well â€¢ confidential company information is revealed to external sources, losing competitive advantage Accept all valid responses. 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 20 of 29 Question Answer Marks

question_text: October/November 2024 1 hour 15 minutes You must answer on the enclosed answer booklet. Define the term external stakeholders . [2] (b) Explain one way the aims of employees may impact on the decisions of a business. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term external stakeholders Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding of the term external stakeholders is worth 2 marks. â€¢ Those outside of (who do not work for) an organisation/business â€¦ but have an interest in or are affected by that business. Accept examples such as suppliers, customers, the community if they are given to explain externality (2) Partial understanding of the term external stakeholders is worth 1 mark. â€¢ Stakeholders who are outside of the business (1) Accept all valid responses. 2 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 10 of 32 Question Answer Marks

1(b) Explain one way the aims of employees may impact on the decisions of a business. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one way the aims of employees may impact a business. â€¢ Employees are a significant group of internal stakeholders with an interest in how a business should operate and may have aims that impact on a business â€¦ accept general impact on a business as well as or instead of impact on business decisions. â€¢ Pressure human resources to produce supportive working conditions (via trade unions â€“ threat to withdraw labour) â€¢ Seek wage/salary increases, profit share opportunities â€¢ Seek strong health/safety conditions â€¢ Seek regular training and development â€¢ Seek participation in decisions â€¢ May also be determined to be productive/hardworking leading to a positive impact on a business â€¢ May seek self - actualisation at work 3 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 11 of 32 Question Answer Marks

1(b) AO2 Application 2 marks for developed application/explanation of the way given 1 mark for limited application/explanation of the way given The ways the aims of employees may impact on business should include examples of how a business might respond to the aims of employees. â€¢ Business may have to accept increases costs to meet employee aims/demands â€¢ May need to postpone/abandon growth policies â€¢ May need to increase prices to cover increased costs â€¢ May need to increase resource allocation to employee stakeholder group â€¢ May need to reduce focus on dividends and profit retention â€¢ May respond positively if employee aims are aligned with business aims. Accept all valid responses. 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 12 of 32 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term full costing . [2] (b) Explain one limitation of contribution costing. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term full costing Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding of the term full costing is worth 2 marks. â€¢ The method of costing in which all fixed and variable costs â€¦ are allocated/applied to products, product prices or cost centres of a business (2) â€“ reference must be made to both aspects of this definition for the award of 2 marks - reference to only one aspect of this definition is a partial definition and should be awarded only 1 mark. Partial understanding of the term full costing is worth 1 mark. â€¢ The inclusion of fixed and variable costs (1) Accept all valid responses. 2 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 13 of 32 Question Answer Marks

2(b) Explain one limitation of contribution costing. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one limitation of contribution costing â€¢ Contribution costing allocates only direct costs to cost/profit centres â€¢ Segregation of fixed and variable costs often difficult and imprecise â€¢ Long term profitability is correctly calculated only using a full - cost basis â€¢ Not an appropriate method for price determination â€¢ It oversimplifies cost behaviour â€¢ Gives misleading impression of business performance â€¢ Difficult to allocate across a multi - product business â€¢ May not reflect economic reality â€¢ May prevent new product development â€¢ Calculation of contribution cost can be difficult â€¢ Only an internal tool 3 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 14 of 32 Question Answer Marks

2(b) AO2 Application 2 marks for developed application/explanation of one limitation of contribution costing 1 mark for limited application/explanation of one limitation of contribution costing â€¢ Ignores overhead costs and so delays/distorts the calculation of profit or loss â€¢ Takes no account that some products/divisions may have much higher fixed costs than others â€¢ May distort economic reality â€“ especially when inventory changes â€¢ May lead to a business continuing to produce a product long after it should have stopped production (makes a contribution) but delays a new product that could make a better contribution â€¢ Inappropriate pricing may lead to reduced sales/profitability â€¢ Gives misleading picture of business performance â€“ overstates profit â€¢ The segregation of costs can be difficult â€“ cannot be done with precision â€“ marginal costs cannot be represented as a straight line â€¢ Internal use only â€“ does not align with financial reporting regulations. Accept all valid responses. 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 15 of 32 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Define the term dynamic pricing . [2] (b) Explain one reason why a business might use price discrimination. [3]

question_text: Analyse two reasons why a business should measure labour productivity. [8] (b) Evaluate whether the sustainability of operations is the most important operational factor in a large retail distribution business. [12] OR
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse two reasons why a business should measure labour productivity. There are 8 marks in total for Q

5(a) â€“ 4 marks for each of the two reasons given : 1 mark for 1 mark for 2 marks for Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for giving one reason â€¢ Labour productivity is real economic output per labour hour â€¢ It is an important indicator of current/future business performance â€¢ It is an important indicator of efficiency/inefficiency â€¦ what is going well/what might need changing â€¢ It allows a business to make comparisons â€¢ It allows a business to regularly analyse the factors that affect productivity levels/motivation levels â€¢ It suggests regular appraisals of resource allocation e.g. capital/labour investment 8 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 20 of 32 Question Answer Marks

5(a) AO2 Application 1 mark for application of one reason â€¢ Labour productivity is an important determinant of business performance â€¢ Measurement of labour productivity gives a business information relating to any need to change/improve the productivity of its workforce â€¢ Measurement allows comparisons to be made between current and past labour productivity and with competitor levels of labour productivity AO3 Analysis â€“ 2 marks for developed analysis of one reason L2 1 mark for limited analysis of one reason L1 â€¢ Business needs to understand the strength or weakness of its current level of labour productivity â€“ how past decisions have impacted on labour productivity â€¢ Business needs to understand why and how changes in labour productivity are needed â€“ what level of improvement is needed â€¢ If labour productivity is seen to be declining change, options can be considered â€“ invest in more capital equipment â€“ automation, computerisation, robotics â€“ more investment in employee training and skills â€“ review production process etc . â€¢ Labour costs are often a significant proportion of production costs and the productive use of labour is closely linked to business efficiency and profitability â€“ non - measurement of labour productivity implies there is little management control or accountab ility of business performance â€“ â€˜what gets measured gets doneâ€™. Accept all valid responses. 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 21 of 32 Question Answer Marks

5(b) Evaluate whether the sustainability of operations is the most important operating factor in a large retail distribution business. Define the term labour turnover . [2] (b) Explain one implication for a business of having a low rate of labour turnover. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term labour turnover . Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding of the term labour turnover is worth 2 marks. â€¢ The rate at which employees are leaving an organisation â€¦ during a specified period (2) or â€¢ Number of employees leaving during a period, divided by the average number of employees during that period, multiplied by 100 (formula) (2). Partial understanding of the term is worth 1 mark. â€¢ When employees leave a business Accept all valid responses. 2 9609 / 13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 10 of 32 Question Answer Marks

1(b) Explain one implication for a business of having a low rate of labour turnover. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one implication of low labour turnover â€¢ Reduces cost of employee replacements + â€¢ Fewer interruptions in production process â€¢ Motivated/contented employees â€¢ Work culture likely to be supportive â€¢ Expertise and experience retained Implication could be negative. â€¢ Lack of new ideas, creative/innovative activity â€¢ High - cost employees AO2 Application 2 marks for developed application/explanation of the implication given 1 mark for limited application/explanation of the implication given The implications for a business with a low rate of labour turnover include : â€“ â€¢ Costs of replacing labour is small + â€¢ Reduced loss in production time in training new employees â€¢ Morale of workforce may remain high â€“ less stress on remaining staff if few leave â€¢ Knowledge and expertise is retained in the business â€¢ Customer service standards maintained with consistency of staff â€¢ Training costs and time saved 3 9609 / 13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 11 of 32 Question Answer Marks

1(b) The implications above are all positive and may be experienced as a result of a positive working environment. However, there may be some negative implications: â€“ â€¢ There may be some significant underlying problems in the business that need addressing - â€¢ Low labour turnover may mean that poor performers are retained and this may affect morale â€¢ The cost of employees may be very high, with many long - serving and senior employees earning high wages/salaries â€¢ The labour force may lack new ideas â€“ little creativity and innovation â€¢ There may be few promotion opportunities with some leaving the business â€¢ Existing staff may not have appropriate skills Accept all valid responses. 9609 / 13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 12 of 32 Question Answer Marks

AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one reason. â€¢ Keep up with competition â€¢ Exploit new markets/increase existing ones â€¢ Allows use of new technology â€¢ New products more appealing to customers/ more profitable â€¢ Important to be agile, respond to market research findings and market trends â€¢ Can extend the profitable life of a product. 3 9609 / 13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 14 of 32 Question Answer Marks

2(b) AO2 Application 2 marks for developed application/explanation of one reason 1 mark for limited application/explanation of one reason The reasons for the importance of product development include: â€“ â€¢ The need to respond to competitive products and initiatives and win new business â€¢ Sell more to existing customers â€“ increase the value offer â€¢ Reduce costs associated with current products and services â€¢ Produce new products and improve existing products in order to enter new markets â€¢ Businesses need to innovate or die â€¢ Support business growth â€“ diversification and increased range of products/services â€¢ Respond to changing customer/market expectations, trends and fashions â€¢ Exploit new technology opportunities - increase sales and profits â€¢ May lead to important extension strategies. question_text: Define the term government grant . [2] (b) Explain one advantage of using retained earnings as a source of business finance. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
3(a) Define the term government grant. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding of the term government grant is worth 2 marks. â€¢ Money given by government to a business â€¦ which normally does not need to be paid back or to fund a project or operation such as job stimulation/initiative (2) Partial understanding of the term government grant is worth 1 mark. â€¢ Money given by government to a business (1) Accept all valid responses. 2 9609 / 13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 16 of 32 Question Answer Marks

3(b) Explain one advantage of using retained earnings as a source of business finance. Indicative content AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one advantage â€¢ Provides a healthy pool of working capital â€“ to purchase assets/invest â€“ or to pay off debts â€¢ Internal funds quicker to organise and access â€¢ Internal control of business not affected â€¢ Avoids business debt â€¢ Reduced costs/no interest payments. AO2 Application 2 marks for developed application/explanation of one advantage 1 mark for limited application/explanation of one advantage. The advantages of using retained earnings as a source of finance include: â€¢ May be readily available and may be substantial if accumulated over time â€¢ Cheap source of finance â€“ unlike loans no interest payments or fees to pay â€¢ Flexible source of finance â€“ no conditions as to how to use â€“ can use for R&D or update equipment or hire more staff â€¢ Fast source of finance â€“ no waiting for lenders to process a request â€¢ Ideal for urgent/emergency funding. Accept all valid responses. 3 9609 / 13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 17 of 32 Question Answer Marks 4 Analyse one likely impact on a business of outsourcing its production process. Note: No marks can be awarded if a relevant impact is not given. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for giving one likely impact â€¢ Likely to lead to production cost savings â€¢ But could lead to increased production costs â€¢ Allows a business to exploit its core skills â€¢ Take advantage of production skills and expertise elsewhere â€¢ Could lose control of the production process - reduced quality â€“ inconsistent delivery â€¢ May damage the reputation of the business â€¢ May attract criticism from pressure groups â€¢ May mean internal redundancies AO2 Application 2 marks for developed application/explanation of the chosen impact 1 mark for limited application/explanation of the chosen impact â€¢ Outsourcing offers a number of advantages to a business â€“ notably the possibility of lower costs â€“ due to lower labour rates â€“ increased efficiency â€¢ Outsourcing also offers a business the opportunity to concentrate on its core competences, which may not include its production process 4 9609 / 13

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Analyse two reasons why a business might decide to grow internally (organically). [8] (b) â€˜Even a friendly merger between two social media companies may not be successful.â€™ Evaluate this view. [12] OR
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse two reasons why a business might choose to grow internally (organically). There are 8 marks in total for Q5(a) â€“ 4 marks for each of the two reasons given : 1 mark for 1 mark for 2 marks for Indicative content Note : This question does not mention alternatives to internal growth or ask for comparisons between internal and external growth though such an answer approach is acceptable and rewardable. Some candidates may simply analyse why a business may seek to grow ( through building an additional factory, opening up another shop, or operating in an additional different market) and make little reference to internal or organic growth issues. This approach is also acceptable and rewardable. Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for giving ONE reason â€¢ Reduces risk/ threat of poor mergers/alliances â€¢ Not subject to external contingencies â€¢ Business culture and morale retained â€¢ Preference to remain local/national â€¢ Avoid potential high costs involved with external growth 8 9609 / 13 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 20 of 32 Question Answer Marks

5(a) AO2 Application 1 mark for application of one reason â€¢ The risk to a business is lower â€“ own values and operations are retained â€¢ No necessity to seek strategic alliances or wait for the acceptable merger â€“ business focus is maintained â€¢ Risk of culture clash with another business is avoided â€“ less disruption to workers â€“ business morale â€¢ Avoidance of significant merger/acquisition financial costs â€“ external growth can be very expensive AO3 Analysis 2 marks for developed analysis of one reason L2 1 mark for limited analysis of one reason L1 â€¢ Internal growth is relatively inexpensive â€“ lower investment required â€“ retained profits used rather than additional loans or shares â€¢ Internal growth relies on the strengths of the business, built up over the years â€“ building on brand reputation, best - selling products, loyal employees â€“ evolution rather than revolution reduces business risks â€¢ Internal growth is easier to control and manage â€“ owners and managers retain total control of business operations and do not face unknown factors resulting from a merger/acquisition â€¢ Internal corporate culture is maintained â€“ organic growth allows work culture and management style to remain and avoids possible problems associated with integration with other businesses â€¢ Maintains healthy gearing position â€“ internal growth often funded from equity â€“ either retained profits or issuing shares to investors â€“ external debt is avoided â€“ providing financial stability and solvency â€¢ Internal growth ensures continuity â€“ focus of the business on pre - established policies is maintained â€“ management and workers on the same page â€¢ Existing owners and managers may have limited ambition â€“ limited internal growth may be seen as sufficient Accept all valid responses. 9609 / 13 Cambridge International

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Brendaâ€™s Bricks (BB) BB is a newly established social enterprise in country X. It was set up by Brenda. Brenda has no experience of running a national business, but she used all her savings to recruit a team of young intrapreneurs. Country X specialises in recycling plastic waste from other countries. It has a dynamic business environment and many businesses have recently failed. Brenda saw an opportunity to recycle low-cost plastic waste into construction bricks. These could be used to help the poorest in society to build waterproof houses. BB recently featured in a government promotion highlighting successful innovators. BB has trialled the method using job production and needs investment to produce its bricks commercially. A large national business, CC, is keen to invest in community projects to improve their corporate social image. CC needs to see a business plan before making a decision. Brenda has started to investigate the costs of production for the business plan that CC has requested. (See Table 1.1.) [1] (ii) Explain the term national business (line 2). [3] (b) (i) Refer to Table 1.1. Calculate the total cost for the 25-year period of using hire purchase if Brenda chooses to use flow production. [3] (ii) Explain one advantage for BB of using hire purchase as a source of finance. [3] (c) Analyse two limitations for BB of creating a business plan.
