DC (DE) 341665/1 2 9609/11/M/J/25 Section A Answer all questions. Define the term job enrichment . [2] (b) Explain one disadvantage to a business of using empowerment to motivate employees. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) Define the term job enrichment. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding â€¢ Job enrichment involves increasing the range/ interest/challenge/responsibility/complexity of jobs to further motivate an employee (2) â€¢ Occurs when employeesâ€™ jobs are redesigned to provide them with more challenging and complex tasks (2) Partial understanding â€¢ Give employees different work to do (1) Accept all valid responses. 2 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 11 of 35 Question Answer Marks

1(b) Explain one disadvantage to a business of using empowerment to motivate employees. There are 3 marks for this question: 1 mark for 2 marks for Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one disadvantage of using empowerment to motivate employees â€¢ Employees lack ability/experience to respond â€¢ Decisions are poorer â€¢ Business is less efficient â€¢ Employees become de - motivated â€¢ Employees more stressed â€¢ Some employees may leave. â€¢ Empowerment may not work/may not last 3 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 12 of 35 Question Answer Marks

1(b) AO2 Application 2 marks for developed of the disadvantage given 1 mark for limited of the disadvantage given â€¢ Employees may not be capable of taking more control of their work â€“ might lead to de - motivated employees. â€¢ Empowerment may mean decisions are made by less experienced and less expert personnel â€“ increasing the number of mistakes made and increasing risk. â€¢ May lead to decreased efficiency â€“ autonomous employees may work independently and decide to work more slowly and/or work at the pace of the slowest â€“ less collaboration and teamwork. â€¢ Relationships between managers and employees become blurred â€“ boundaries of authority broken in a flatter, more streamlined structure â€“ managers may lose control. â€¢ Employees stress increases, morale decreases in a preference for alternative forms of motivation. â€¢ Empowered employees become more attractive to competitors (risk of staff poaching). â€¢ May not work as some employees are only motivated by financial benefits e.g. bonus Accept all valid responses. 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 13 of 35 Question Answer Marks

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term price discrimination . [2] (b) Explain one disadvantage to a business of using competitive pricing. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term price discrimination. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding â€¢ The policy of selling the same product at different prices (1) to different buyers (1) Partial understanding â€¢ Selling a product at different prices (1) â€¢ Setting different prices for different people (1) Accept all valid responses. 2 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 14 of 35 Question Answer Marks

2(b) Explain one disadvantage to a business of using competitive pricing. There are 3 marks for this question : 1 mark for 2 marks for Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one disadvantage of using competitive pricing â€¢ Competitive pricing may mean a business makes a loss â€¢ Competitive pricing may force prices down/lower in the long turn â€¢ Could lead to business failure â€¢ Could lead to a price war â€¢ Could deflect a business from other objectives â€¢ May mean too much concern for competitor actions. â€¢ May lead to lower/less sales 3 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 15 of 35 Question Answer Marks

2(b) AO2 Application 2 marks for developed of the disadvantage given 1 mark for limited of the disadvantage given â€¢ A business that sets prices solely on competitor prices might risk selling at a loss as it may not be able to cover its manufacturing costs or business overhead expenses. â€¢ Competing using competitor prices (lower) might give a competitive edge in the short term, only as competitors fight back with lower prices. â€¢ Could lead to lower profit margins and increase the risk of failure, especially true of businesses with few financial resources. â€¢ May be difficult and costly to discover competitor prices â€“ need to hire pricing teams or purchasing automated competitor pricing monitoring tool. â€¢ Could lead to a price war and even if a business can sustain low prices, customers may see the low - price product as an inferior product. â€¢ A sole focus on competitive pricing might deflect a business from concerns for quality and added value. â€¢ Too much focus on competitorâ€™s prices may also mean too much time spent on competitors, to the detriment of insufficient time spent on internal issues such as strengthening its USP and competitive edge (other than price points). Accept all valid responses. 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 16 of 35 Question Answer Marks 3 ( a) Define the term capital intensive operations. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding â€¢ Capital intensive operations involve business processes/production that require large amounts of capital expenditure/machinery/equipment (1) compared to labour

subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Define the term capital intensive operations . [2] (b) Explain one limitation to a business of labour intensive operations. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
3(b) Explain one limitation to a business of labour intensive operations. There are 3 marks for this question : 1 mark for 2 marks for Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one limitation of using labour intensive operations â€¢ Expertise, experience of employees can vary â€¢ Skilled workers may take time to train. â€¢ It can be more expensive â€¢ Relatively inefficient and inconsistent/unreliable levels of effort/not as fast as machines â€¢ Labour relations problems. â€¢ Difficulty of replacing/recruiting staff. 3 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 18 of 35 Question Answer Marks

3(b) AO2 Application 2 marks for developed of the limitation given 1 mark for limited of the limitation given Production processes that have a high level of labour input and a low level of capital input may have the following limitations: â€¢ Variable levels of labour expertise and experience may lead to variable levels of product quality. â€¢ The training and re - training of employees may be very expensive and may disrupt the production process. â€¢ Due to lower productivity the unit cost may be higher than capital intensive operations â€¢ Unable to reach the consistency levels of machine production - employees fall ill and take holidays. â€¢ Employees may seek better conditions leading to disputes, strikes, unionisation. â€¢ Labour turnover may be high, competitive markets for labour may mean labour shortages - growth targets threatened. Accept all valid responses 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 19 of 35 Question Answer Marks 4 Analyse one way a business might fail as a consequence of lack of finance. There are 5 marks for this question : 1 mark for 2 marks for 2 marks for Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one way a business might fail due to lack of finance Inability to: â€¢ sustain cashflows â€¢ provide working capital â€¢ build up reserves â€¢ pay suppliers/variable costs â€¢ invest â€¢ obtain sources of finance â€¢ maintain quality/innovation/research and development â€¢ to recruit managers â€¢ bankruptcy/insolvency/administration/liquidity issues 5 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 20 of 35 Question Answer Marks 4 AO2 Application 2 marks for developed of the chosen way identified 1 mark for limited of the chosen way given â€¢ A lack of finance may undermine a businessâ€™s capacity to survive at various times and in various situation â€¢ Such as the inability to purchase essential equipment â€¢ An inability to cover day - to - day expenses (paying wages and suppliers) â€¢ An inability to build up reserves in times of recession â€¢ An inability to support essential growth and expansion plans.

question_text: Analyse two ways business enterprise can help the development of a country. [8] (b) â€˜Intrapreneurship is the most important factor that will ensure the success of a computer entertainment business.â€™ Evaluate this view. [12] OR
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Analyse two ways business enterprise can help the development of a country. There are 8 marks in total for Q5(a) 4 marks for each of the ways business enterprise can help the development of a country. These 4 marks consist of 1 mark for 1 mark for 2 marks for Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one way business enterprise can help the development of a country â€“ repeat for a second way identified. NOTE: The impact of enterprise can be within a business enterprise or within the wider economy â€¢ Stimulates economic activity â€¢ Stimulates and improves social welfare â€¢ Leads to economic growth â€¢ Market demand for goods and services is increased â€¢ Innovation leads to new products â€¢ Jobs are created â€¢ Consumer spending is stimulated â€¢ Export opportunities exploited â€¢ Bring factors of production together with entrepreneurial spirit and purpose, creating dynamism in an economy 8 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 22 of 35 Question Answer Marks

5(a) AO2 Application 1 mark for of a one chosen way identified - repeat this for the application of a second identified way. â€¢ In market economies, business enterprise acts as the wheels of economic growth and development. Un - utilised resources, labour and capital are utilised efficiently. â€¢ Entrepreneurs stimulate a business enterprise culture and thrive within it, creating new products and services, stimulating new employment and accelerate economic growth and development. Stimulates and improves social welfare â€“ such as electricity for all AO3 Analysis 2 marks for developed of one chosen way â€“ repeat for second identified way 1 mark for limited of one chosen way â€“ repeat for second identified way â€¢ Business enterprise stimulates new business formation, providing choice, dynamism, competition and employment, often locally owned and committed to local areas, and act as seedbeds for further development. â€¢ Business enterprise cultures lead to the creation of new jobs and opportunities â€“ often entry level jobs to turn unskilled job holders into skilled ones and prepares and provides experienced works for larger industries. â€¢ Business enterprise initiatives bring innovation and creativity to countries â€“ resources are used effectively as new and improved products, services, and technologies are developed, leading to the generation of new wealth, higher tax revenues, allowing gov ernments to offer citizens more benefits. â€¢ Business enterprise encourages research and development to solve problems that existing products and technology have not yet solved â€“ bring innovation to existing products â€“ secure new finance and experiment with new approaches to business functions, such as HRM, Operations, Finance, and Marketing. â€¢ The economic benefit of business enterprise may create the income and wealth for social change and development by reducing a dependency on

mark_scheme_text:
6(a) Analyse two ways international markets may present different challenges to a business than national markets. There are 8 marks in total for Q6(a) 4 marks for each of the ways international markets may present different challenges to a business than national markets. These 4 marks consist of 1 mark for 1 mark for 2 marks for Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understandin g 1 mark for identifying one way international markets may present different challenges to a business than national markets Repeat this for a second identified way . NOTE : International markets are global markets outside the international borders of a businessâ€™s country of citizenship â€“ people and businesses from different nations and sharing different cultures may compete in an international market. National market is a domestic market for goods and services operating within the borders of, and governed by, the regulations of a particular country. A business is likely to be more familiar with trading relationships and regulations and there is likely less risk, uncertainty and complexity when operating in a national market. 8 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 28 of 35 Question Answer Marks

6(a) Ways may include: â€¢ International markets may present language challenges to business entrants â€¢ International markets may have business cultures that do not match national market expectations â€¢ Operating in an international market may require more expertise, competence and special management skills â€¢ Competition is likely to be tough and intense in international markets â€¢ International markets may present more high - risk challenges to businesses, such as political volatility, changing fashion, etc â€¢ International markets may require acceptance of different tariff and non - tariff constraints and local laws and policies AO2 Application 1 mark for of the chosen way. Repeat this for a second identified way . â€¢ International markets may present language challenges to business entrants â€“ many languages may be used. â€¢ International markets may have business cultures that do not match national market expectations, e.g. in respect of religion, dress codes, working hours, personal space and facility, and gift giving. An ability to recognise, respect and adapt to these dif ferences may reduce your ability to succeed in an international market. â€¢ Operating in an international market may require more expertise, competence and special management skills to deal with the more complex circumstances and situations like changes in the strategies of governments and the mindsets of customers. â€¢ Competition is likely to be tough and intense in international markets â€“ global competition involves competition not only with competitors from home countries, but also with competitors from foreign lands. â€¢ International markets may present more high - risk challenges to businesses,

mark_scheme_text:

1(b) Explain one disadvantage to a business of having corporate social responsibility (CSR) as a business objective. There are 3 marks for this question â€“ 1 mark for knowledge and understanding and 2 marks for application/explanation Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding â€“ 1 mark for identifying one disadvantage of CSR as a business objective â€¢ May add to business costs â€¢ May reduce profits â€¢ May result in stakeholder conflict â€¢ Business operations more transparent â€¢ Business may be deflected from bottom line objective â€¢ Competitors may take advantage AO2 Application 2 marks for a developed of one disadvantage of CSR as a business objective 1 mark for a limited of one disadvantage of CSR as a business objective â€¢ CSR objectives may well involve additional costs for a business â€“ profit not the sole objective. â€¢ CSR objectives may well mean stakeholder conflicts â€“ shareholders concern for maximum dividends versus management commitment to ethical suppliers. â€¢ CSR businesses are in the public eye and likely to be scrutinised for all that they do â€“ subject to criticism over the smallest acts or operations. â€¢ CSR implies more accountability and responsibility and requires sustainable actions â€“ rather than â€˜window dressingâ€™ PR campaigns. â€¢ Sustainable resources may be more expensive â€¢ CSR companies may lose sight of their fundamental commercial/economic role as business organisation Accept all valid responses. 3 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 10 of 31 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Define the term involuntary redundancy . [2] (b) Explain one way a business could use employee development to encourage multi-skilling. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Define the term involuntary redundancy. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding (2 marks) â€¢ An employer terminates the job of an employee (1), employees are selected for redundancy (1) and the employee has no choice in this decision (1) Partial understanding (1 mark) â€¢ An employee loses their job (1) â€¢ The job role is no longer needed (1) Accept all valid responses. 2 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 11 of 31 Question Answer Marks

2(b) Explain one way a business could use employee development to encourage multi - skilling. There are 3 marks for this question : 1 mark for 2 marks for Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one way of using employee development to encourage multi - skilling â€¢ Training programmes â€¢ Offer job rotation â€¢ Offer job enlargement â€¢ Offer job enrichment â€¢ Motivate/pay employees to learn new skills â€¢ Provide rewards for employees that take up multi - skilling opportunities â€¢ Promote a learning culture in the business AO2 Application 2 marks for developed of the chosen way given 1 mark for limited of the way given â€¢ Specific training programmes that focus on giving employees the opportunity to cross the traditional trade - specific or craft - specific skill sets. â€¢ Develop a culture in a business that encourages and rewards employees who seize opportunities to extend their skills â€“ a clear business objective to unlock employee potential. 3 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 12 of 31 Question Answer Marks

2(b) â€¢ A consistent business focus on the need to update core skills to take advantage of a dynamic economic environment so that employees have a forward focus and expectation of change (threats and opportunities). â€¢ A business sees multi - skilling and upskilling as a critical workforce investment â€“ HRM access and make available e - learning courses, virtual workshops â€“ identify skill gaps and facilitate tailored learning pathways. â€¢ Develop a learning environment and link a focus on in - demand skills with career development opportunities to provide an incentive to develop skills employees want and the skills a business needs. Accept all valid responses. Question Answer Marks

question_text: Define the term inventory re-order level . [2] (b) Explain one advantage to a business of holding buffer inventory. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
3(a) Define the term inventory re - order level. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding Clear understanding (2 marks) â€¢ The level/quantity of inventory that triggers a new order to suppliers (2) Partial understanding (1 mark) â€¢ The time to re - order inventory (1) â€¢ A decision to replenish stock (1) Accept all valid responses. 2 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 13 of 31 Question Answer Marks

3(b) Explain one advantage to a business of holding buffer inventory. There are 3 marks for this question : 1 mark for 2 marks for Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one advantage of holding buffer inventory A term used in logistics that describes a level of extra stock that is maintained to mitigate risk or stockouts caused by uncertainties in supply and demand. â€¢ Avoid stockouts â€¢ Allow continuous production â€¢ Meet customer demands, including unexpected demands â€¢ Meet bulk orders â€¢ Avoid lost sales â€¢ Protect against fluctuations in demand/market â€¢ Helps if there is damage to parts of inventory AO2 Application 2 marks for developed of the advantage given 1 mark for limited of the advantage given â€¢ Adequate buffer stocks allow businesses operations to proceed according to plan. 3 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 14 of 31 Question Answer Marks

3(b) â€¢ Protects against unforeseen variation in supply â€“ supply chains are longer, more globalised with forces causing more frequent disruptions. â€¢ Compensates for forecast inaccuracies â€“ a consistent demand changes suddenly â€“ customer service is maintained through use of the buffer stock. â€¢ Avoids stockouts and keeps customer service and satisfaction levels high â€“ keeps warehouse and supply chain running smoothly. â€¢ Allows a business to take advantage of bulk buying discounts and so reduce the cost of inventory. Accept all valid responses. 9609 / 12 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 15 of 31 Question Answer Marks 4 Analyse one reason why a business may use cost - based pricing. There are 5 marks for this question : 1 mark for 2 marks for 2 marks for Note: no marks can be awarded if a relevant reason is not given Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying one reason why a business may use cost - based pricing. A pricing method adopted by a business by which the selling price of a product is determined by adding a specific fixed percentage to the productâ€™s unit cost. â€¢ Especially useful to a new business â€“ costs need to be covered with a small profit if possible â€¢ It is a simple method â€“ simple to calculate and simple to implement â€¢ It ensures that all production costs are cov

question_text: Natural Shampoos (NS) NS is a private limited company set up by Saira and Chantal as a social enterprise. NS sells natural hair care products and focuses on customer (market) orientation. The main aims of Saira and Chantal include donating to local charities and improving gender equality in the workplace. NS began by selling its products to small, independent businesses in country H. NS expanded its range slowly based on customer feedback, specific requests on its blog, and sales data. NS explored additional finance opportunities required for expansion. NS approached John, who owns manufacturing facilities in country H and has significant marketing experience. He invested into NS in return for ownership of one third of the business. Johnâ€™s main aim is to increase long-term profits in order to maximise the return on his investment. In the five years since Johnâ€™s investment, NS has become the market leader in country H for natural hair care products. NS products are now sold in major cosmetic retailers. NS recently analysed its output in 2024 (see Table 1.1). Table 1. NS output data for 2024 Units Actual output level 145 000 Capacity 150 000 Saira and Chantal have decided to employ a Marketing Manager for NS. Fig 1.2 shows an extract from the job advertisement. Marketing Manager â€¢ Experience of growing brand awareness and using online channels of distribution. â€¢ Using budgets to monitor the performance of marketing campaigns. â€¢ Developing and maintaining relationships with external stakeholders. Fig 1. PHâ€™s sources of finance Frank, the Operations Director, wants to improve the sustainability of PHâ€™s operations. He plans to: â€¢ stop renting out accommodation in some lower-income cities, and sell these buildings â€¢ upgrade accommodation in high-income cities â€¢ reduce the size of the workforce. (a) (i) Identify one way in which business size can be measured. [1] (ii) Explain the term demographic segmentation . [3] (b) (i) Refer to lines 6â€“8. Calculate PHâ€™s labour turnover in 2024. [3] (ii) Explain one way in which PH could use Maslowâ€™s Hierarchy of Needs. [3] (c) Analyse one advantage and one disadvantage to PH of using an overdraft as a source of finance. [8] (d) Evaluate whether Frankâ€™s plan will improve the sustainability of PHâ€™s operations. question_text: Crafty Toys (CT) CT is a co-operative that produces and sells a range of wooden toys, such as puzzles, cars and dolls. The co-operative was formed in 2020 by a group of 12 people who share a passion for making toys. They wanted to work together to create quality products that would appeal to children and parents. No one person takes on the role of manager; instead, they share the traditional manager functions between them. CT operates from a large workshop in a rural area of country Z, making toys using labour-intensive techniques. CT uses locally sourced wood that is certified as eco-friendly. CT also uses natural paints and finishes that are safe and non-toxic for children. Table 1.1 shows CTâ€™s output in 2024. Table 1. Output data for CT in 2024 Number of people Average hours worked per person per year Output 12 1800 54 000 units CT sells its products online through its own website and social media platforms, as well as through some independent retailers who specialise in ethical products. The co-operative also attends various trade fairs and exhibitions to showcase its products and network with potential customers and suppliers. CT has never researched customer wants. CT faces some challenges in its current operations. These challenges include: â€¢ high costs of production â€¢ a long lead time â€¢ a requirement for skilled workers. CT is considering changing to flow production to overcome these challenges. 5 10 15 Extract from FFâ€™s financial data for 2024 $000 Revenue 120 Direct costs

question_text: Define the term labour productivity . [2] (b) Explain one way that a business can improve labour productivity. [3]
mark_scheme_text:
3(a) Define the term labour productivity . Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding CLEAR UNDERSTANDING (2 marks) â€¢ the ratio of outputs to labour input during production (2) â€¢ output per worker (2) â€¢ = Total output Number of employees (2) Clear understanding of the term labour productivity is worth 2 marks. This is indicated by 2 ticks . PARTIAL UNDERSTANDING (1 mark) Partial understanding of the term labour productivity is worth 1 mark. This is indicated by 1 tick . â€¢ output of labour/workforce (1) â€¢ how much employees are able to do/how productive workers are (1) Accept all valid responses. 2 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 16 of 32 Question Answer Marks

3(b) Explain one way that a business can improve labour productivity. Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying ONE WAY that a business can improve labour productivity. â€¢ motivate employees â€¢ train employees â€¢ employ experienced workers â€¢ increase hours worked â€¢ change way work is done â€¢ invest in equipment and technology AO2 Application 2 marks for DEVELOPED application/explanation of ONE way 1 mark for LIMITED application/explanation of ONE way â€¢ effort and productivity may increase if employees are offered financial or non - financial rewards â€“ they may feel more valued and work harder e.g. piece rate pay â€¢ training/experience increases employee output by helping them to gain more skills and learn new and better ways of doing things â€¢ working more hours may mean an individual employee could produce more products i.e. increase their own total output â€¢ team working, altering the layout of the workplace, implementing employee recommendations on how they could work more effectively could improve the speed of production and productivity â€¢ if employees have modern and more efficient machinery, they should be able to have higher output than employees who are using outdated equipment. ( NOTE: suggesting replacing employees with machines to improve business productivity is not answering the question) Accept all valid responses. 3 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 17 of 32 Question Answer Marks 4 Analyse one benefit to a business of low labour turnover. Note: no marks can be awarded if a relevant benefit is not given Indicative content Responses may include: AO1 Knowledge and understanding 1 mark for identifying ONE BENEFIT to a business of low labour turnover â€¢ retain experienced, trained and skilled employees â€¢ motivated/loyal employees â€¢ lower costs â€¢ HR team focussed on current employees not recruitment â€¢ achievement of objectives â€¢ uninterrupted production process/good productivity â€¢ increased stability/reputation of business 5 9609 / 11 Cambridge International AS & A Level â€“ Mark Scheme

1(b) Explain one pricing method. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: Clever Bean (CB) CB is a public limited company that operates a chain of coffee shops in several countries. The company was founded in 2010 by two friends, Shopra and Ben. They share a passion for coffee and want to offer high-quality products to customers. CB started with one coffee shop in country Q and gradually expanded, always locating in high-income areas of major cities. CB operates in a dynamic business environment. There is a high level of competition from other well-known coffee shop chains. The company sources its coffee beans from fair trade suppliers who grow their coffee in an environmentally friendly way. CB offers a wide range of coffee products to the mass market. CB also offers other drinks and a variety of food items, such as sandwiches and cakes. Recent research shows that customers are now looking for healthy food options. CBâ€™s customers are mainly young professionals, students and tourists. Customers enjoy CBâ€™s inviting atmosphere, comfortable seating areas, free Wi-Fi access and music. Its staff are trained to be friendly, helpful and knowledgeable. Table 1.1 shows some financial data for CB for 2023 and 2024. Table 1. CBâ€™s costs and revenue for 2023 and 2024 2024 ($m) 2023 ($m) Revenue 50 Price and cost estimates Product Price Allocated fixed costs Variable costs per unit Face cream $5 $12 000 $

DC (DE) 345884 2 9609/21/O/N/25 Nataliaâ€™s Naturals (NN) NN is a small business owned by Natalia. NN produces and sells a range of natural skincare products. In 2020, Natalia started making her own skincare products at home using natural ingredients. She realised that there was a demand for her products among her friends and family. Natalia then decided to turn her hobby into a business and launched NN in 2021. Natalia operates her business from a rented workshop with limited space, where she makes her products by hand. She buys her ingredients from local suppliers who share her values of environmentally friendly production. NN has been growing steadily and has gained a loyal customer base. NN has received positive reviews from beauty magazines. Nataliaâ€™s skincare products are sold through her own website, social media platforms and local retailers who specialise in selling natural products. Natalia is developing a new face cream. Table 2.1 shows price and cost estimates for the new face cream. Table 2. The skincare market is very competitive. There are many established brands that offer similar products to NN, but with wider distribution channels. Natalia needs to balance her time between production, managing orders, delivery, marketing and other administrative tasks. She works long hours and is stressed and exhausted. Natalia is considering outsourcing to increase NNâ€™s production capacity. (a) (i) Identify one internal stakeholder of a business. [1] (ii) Explain the term distribution channels . [3] (b) (i) Refer to Table 2.1. Calculate the break-even level of output of the new face cream. [3] (ii) Explain one limitation to NN of break-even analysis. [3] (c) Analyse one advantage and one disadvantage to NN of being a small business. [8] (d) Evaluate whether outsourcing is the most suitable way to increase NNâ€™s production capacity. subject: A-Level Business
question_text: The Research Agency (TRA) TRA is a quaternary sector business. It is the market leader in providing social media data to food manufacturers in country F. TRAâ€™s market has grown by 40% over the last year. DF is a multinational business that specialises in non-alcoholic drinks. DF has asked TRA for data about a new bottle of water with pod attachments. The pods give a fruity flavour which is combined with sparkling water. TRA has sampled 8000 potential consumers on social media to identify their favourite fruity flavour (see Fig. 1.1). None, 6% Watermelon, 14% Lemon, 34% Strawberry, 28% Banana, 18% Fig. 1. Data about consumer flavour preferences for DF TRA employs 65 researchers. Since 2020, all of these employees work from home. They are provided with all the necessary IT equipment and a high-speed internet connection. However, motivation is poor, and this has led to high labour turnover. The Human Resource Director has received some feedback from employees. Fig. 1.2 shows some of this feedback. â€¢ I hate being so isolated â€“ I never get to speak to any other employees. â€¢ I have to use my own electricity, and the cost has doubled in the last two years. â€¢ I know some employees are not working very hard, but they get paid the same as me. Itâ€™s not fair! Fig. 1.2 summary of feedback from employees at TRA (a) (i) Identify one stage of the transformational process. [1] (ii) Explain the term multinational business . [3] (b) (i) Refer to Fig 1.1 and other information. Calculate the combined total number of potential consumers who would prefer lemon or strawberry flavoured water. [3] (ii) Explain one reason why TRA uses sampling. [3] (c) Refer to Fig 1.1 and other information. Analyse two reasons why the data collected for DF may not be reliable. [8] (d) Evaluate the most effective way for TRA to improve the motivation of the home working employees. [12] 5 10 15 3 9609/22/O/N/25 Budget report for Drakeâ€™s website in the previous month Budget Actual Total revenue $4500 $4000 Total costs $600 $750 Profit $3900 $3250 Drake has now left his job and is focusing full time on the website. He rebranded the website as Law Advice Forum (LAF). Drakeâ€™s business plan for LAF included: â€¢ Operating as a sole trader. â€¢ Continuing to develop and expand the website to provide legal advice and paid legal services. â€¢ Renting an office in city G where all employees would work. â€¢ Hiring five fully qualified lawyers. â€¢ Hiring three administrators. â€¢ Leasing IT equipment. However, Drake is concerned about the limitations LAF will face as a labour intensive operation. (a) (i) Identify one external stakeholder of a business partnership. [1] (ii) Explain the term outsourcing . [3] (b) (i) Refer to Table 2.1. Calculate the profit variance for Drakeâ€™s website in the previous month. State whether the variance is favourable or adverse. [3] (ii) Explain one way Drake could use the budget data in Table 2.1. [3] (c) Analyse two possible limitations for LAF as a labour intensive operation. [8]
